entries.title: Revealing a delete button
entries.summary: In your NoteCircle to do list, once you have a mixture of complete and incomplete items, they’ll reveal a button to remove the completed ones.
entries.analysis: What makes this good, is that the button isn’t cluttering up the screen the entire time. But once you have a mixture, it means you’re actively using the list, rather than keeping it as an archive.

entries.title: Hiding the advanced onboarding
entries.summary: Stuff hides their ‘advanced stuff’ section in your onboarding to-do list that both teaches you that you can have collapsable sections, and reveals extra items.
entries.analysis: This is a type of progressive disclosure—you're more likely to pay attention to the shorter list at all, because it's not bogged down by all of the advanced ones.
tags: add a plan to a task

entries.title: Cycling breakfast emojis
entries.summary: While adding a task, Lil Planner dynamically adds a task icon, and will cycle different emojis for the same topic, to avoid them all looking identical.
patterns: Dynamic emoji cycling for task variety
tags: dynamic emoji cycling for task variety

entries.title: Slack's dynamic subtitles
entries.summary: Slack will contextualise your plan type inside the subtitle for that menu item, adding certainty to what you’re “managing”.
tags: view or manage your free plan
view or manage your free trial

entries.title: Try our demo mode
entries.summary: While exploring the Halo app as a free member, you’ll be shown premium dashboards full of demo data, so you can more easily see what the value of the insights are.

entries.title: Stop the confetti
entries.summary: Stuff lets you turn off the confetti showers that appear after completing your daily tasks—which may help reduce irritation for some users.

entries.title: Try NoteCircle
entries.summary: After installation, NoteCircle pre-fill the to-do list input field with “Try NoteCircle”, which turns into an item, demonstrating how easy it is—without you needing to think of a real entry.
entries.analysis: i.e., they've managed to show you how the feature works, without you needing to think creatively.

entries.title: Forced daily breaks
entries.summary: The quote-saving app, Thoughts, have a customisable limit for the number of quotes you can binge in day.
entries.analysis: This added friction encourages you to stop scrolling, and may reduce the chances you get overwhelmed / bored by seeing similar quotes.

entries.title: Embedding iOS instructions
entries.summary: Grammarly paste installation instructions at the bottom of their iOS settings page.
entries.analysis: This is particularly useful, because you need to dive into your iPhone settings to allow the keyboard.
tags: how to activate the grammarly keyboard

entries.title: A secret reward
entries.summary: If you scroll all the way to the bottom of MacroFactor's list of subscription benefits, you get an easter egg that unlocks an extra week’s worth of a free trial.
entries.analysis: Importantly, by making you feel like you've earned (discovered) this, you're more likely to actually use it.

entries.title: What do you want to learn?
entries.summary: Emma has you customise your onboarding by letting you choose what you want to learn about (or to decide later and skip).
entries.analysis: This will likely increase attentional buy-in, because people can pick something that actually interests them.

entries.title: Resurface this email later
entries.summary: Hey has an option to snooze selected emails, including an option to ‘Surprise me’ to reduce the decision-making on when it should resurface.
entries.analysis: This is unusual, but may reduce Decision Fatigue, and utilise User-Initiated Triggers. i.e., users may actually find it easier to say "I don't know when to see this again, suprise me", rather than have to actually give a specific time frame.

entries.title: Discover new cookbooks
entries.summary: At the end of your library, CookShelf will show a CTA to add more books, and then start displaying recommendations.
entries.analysis: i.e., they merge your saved items with recommendations, but with a very clear distinction.
tags: made in india, cooked in britain

entries.title: Name vs typo
entries.summary: Apple Mail will stop suggesting a typo if that name is saved to your contacts.

entries.title: Vote for your dream cookie
entries.summary: Crumbl has a ‘Dream Lineups’ section where you can create and vote for your ideal combination of flavours in a cookie box.
entries.analysis: This is a great discovery engine, with the voting acting as social proof.

entries.title: I'm ready to learn now
entries.summary: Stuff has an option to reset the mini-tutorials (e.g., tooltips) in case you skipped them the first time they were shown.

entries.title: Turn your sound up
entries.summary: If you try and play an audible translation when your device is muted, Google will let you know that your phone is muted.

entries.title: Pester mode
entries.summary: Adulting has a ‘Pester Mode’ setting that reminds you of any overdue items on your task list from the last week.

entries.title: Get to work
entries.summary: When you start a new session on Claude Code, this mascot is animated to open a laptop and start working.

entries.title: Snake while you wait
entries.summary: After buying a coffee, Blank Street has a Snake mini-game to win a free one.

entries.title: Catch 'em all
entries.summary: Google has an easter egg, where you search for Pokemon and collect the original 151 to a Pokedex.

entries.title: Here are some images
entries.summary: When adding an event, Days picks up on your event name and suggests royalty-free background images based on what you’re adding.

entries.title: Our delivery area
entries.summary: If you don't give HeyTea location permissions, they'll show you a map of available delivery areas so you don’t waste time adding in your address.

entries.title: Secret locations
entries.summary: Crumbl has a secret menu offered at specific branches, and the icons on the map represent the items available at those locations.
ui_elements: Tab navigation (Requests, Lineups, Secret Menu, Journal)

entries.title: 100% of your tip
entries.summary: When leaving a tip on Blank Street, they’ll clarify that 100% of the tip goes to the barista.
entries.analysis: This is important because when tipping through an app, people may assume that a provider takes a cut.
tags: set preferred tip to use in checkout.
100% of the tip goes to your barista

entries.title: Demonstrating template sizes
entries.summary: When changing the zoom level on Spotify, they’ll use mock-up images to demonstrated what the layout will look like.

entries.title: Loading your rewards
entries.summary: While loading your Costa rewards, there’s a spinner of coffee cups and muffins.

entries.title: Cut your boarding pass
entries.summary: To start a timer on Focus Flight, you swipe across the screen to cut your boarding pass.
entries.analysis: For context, Focus Flight is a productivity timer that uses "boarding a flight" as a way of setting how long you want to focus for. i.e., a 60 minute timer would be a 60 minute flight.

entries.title: Your drink includes
entries.summary: HeyTea shows you a cross-section of what’s included in each drink.

entries.title: Study now to use Twitter
entries.summary: Duolingo has a feature to use iOS Screen Time, to block access to other apps until you’ve completed your daily learning.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this is optional.

entries.title: Importing your memory
entries.summary: To help users migrate to Claude, there’s a copiable prompt to paste into another LLM, which creates an output of your memory.
entries.analysis: In other words, if you pasted this prompt into ChatGPT, you’d be able to copy their response, paste it into Claude, and bring your memory over.

entries.title: Floating educational bubbles
entries.summary: Stuff helps you learn about their various icons, by having them float around as you tap them one by one.
entries.analysis: It’s also fantastic that they turn monochrome after viewing the icon’s description.
tags: tap an icon to learn about what it does

entries.title: Copying the transaction
entries.summary: Emma has an option to quickly copy a transaction name, useful when you have an unknown charge and want to share it.

entries.title: Real-time sharing links
entries.summary: If you take a screenshot within Grab, they’ll offer to share ride details using a dedicated real-time link instead.

entries.title: Customise your cup
entries.summary: HeyTea allows you to customise your drink with stickers, either drawing your own, or selecting one a community option.

entries.title: Swipe to start your streak
entries.summary: After returning to Duolingo from a break, there’s a moment of theatre as you swipe up to restart your streak.
entries.analysis: This helps make this moment more enjoyable and memorable.
tags: swipe up to ignite your streak!

entries.title: Full of to-do items
entries.summary: Stuff has an inbox component that literally fills up with your to-do list cards as you start adding them.

entries.title: Adding your cookbooks
entries.summary: CookShelf helps you quickly add your physical cookbooks to your library by prompting you to scan barcodes one after another.
tags: instantly scan or look up your books

entries.title: Reducing the effort to scroll
entries.summary: When selecting your date of birth, MacroFactor’s default date is exactly equidistant from the extreme values, reducing the effort required to find your real birthday.
entries.analysis: This is a thoughtful default, compared to the common one of just putting in today’s date and having you scroll back through years. Even the default year 1996 is thoughtful since it also seems to be equidistant to the app’s likely target demographic

entries.title: Adding drop-off details
entries.summary: While waiting for your food, Grab suggests adding more specific drop-off information, complete with a photo of the intended drop-off spot, to help riders complete the delivery more accurately.

entries.title: Improving conversions with context
entries.summary: Before creating an account on Acorns, you can interact with a visualisation of how much your money could grow.

entries.title: Adding from yesterday
entries.summary: When adding a transaction to your budget, Spendee has a quick toggle to change the date to "Yesterday", anticipating that you might be adding your transactions from yesterday, today.

entries.title: Contextualising dates
entries.summary: To contextualise filter options (like “7 days”), PostHog will show the actual dates as a tooltip.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you're looking at a filter like "last 90 days", it contextualises when that period actually starts.

entries.title: Celebrating a milestone
entries.summary: When you reach the minimum order threshold for free delivery, Foodpanda throws up some confetti on-screen as a visual reminder of the ‘achievement’.
ui_elements: Call to action button (View your cart)

entries.title: How far away are they?
entries.summary: To ease the pain of waiting, Gojek shows you a dynamic countdown of how many meters away the driver is from you.
tags: your driver will arrive in 3 min

entries.title: You have coupons
entries.summary: If you have coupons that are valid but you haven’t applied them to your order, HeyTea will let you know before you go to Checkout.
tags: you have 4 coupons available to use

entries.title: Adding to your list
entries.summary: When adding a recipe to your CookShelf list, you tap a bowl icon that literally opens its lid to add the recipe.

entries.title: Hot air balloon game
entries.summary: If you open the Google Play store without an internet connection, you’ll see a playable game while you wait.

entries.title: Dynamic browsing times
entries.summary: When opening Werksy to look for co-working space in an evening, it’ll default the search to the next working day.
entries.analysis: i.e., looking on a Friday evening at 8pm, will change your search to Monday morning.

entries.title: A nudge in the checkout
entries.summary: To increase conversion rates in the checkout, Airbnb will remind you if you're getting a good deal, even on the final page.
entries.analysis: It implies scarcity and also contextualises why you should act now, rather than wait.
tags: your price is below the 60-day average

entries.title: Smarter food reminders
entries.summary: Foodllama has an option to automatically snooze upcoming reminders if you’ve already logged your food.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is a smarter way of managing notifications that would otherwise be annoying. "Don't forget to track your lunch", 30 minutes after tracking your lunch.

entries.title: Satisfying onboarding
entries.summary: During onboarding, Lil Planner will get you to tick off to-do items, giving you an immediate sense of satisfaction, and teaching you how to use it.

entries.title: Spin the wheel
entries.summary: Adulting has a spin-the-wheel feature to help you decide what to cook from your saved recipes.
entries.analysis: This is likely a gimmick, but a fun one.

entries.title: Removing one filter
entries.summary: When filtering results, Airbnb will occasionally show you popular listings that don't match one of your filters, showing you fantastic options that almost match.
entries.analysis: i.e., "similar dates" or "without a hot tub".

entries.title: Social proofing a route
entries.summary: Grab uses social proof of “taken by many Grab drivers” to help you decide which route to take.
entries.analysis: The specificity of "taken by many Grab drivers" is likely more effective than a vague "popular" tag.

entries.title: Don't delete me
entries.summary: When long-pressing to delete Moonlitt, they’ll offer you a secret discount.

entries.title: Shortcut to add the time
entries.summary: When writing about your day in Daygram, there's a shortcut to add the exact current time into the text, making it easier to write in-the-moment notes.

entries.title: Fake fields during onboarding
entries.summary: To provide an interactive onboarding, without iOS defaults getting in the way, 1Password uses fake input field and keyboards to play with.
entries.analysis: In other words, they want to show you how to use it, with tooltips, without actually triggering a real iOS field.

entries.title: Popping bubbles
entries.summary: When you select a game from Pok Pok’s home screen, the other options ‘pop’ to disappear while it loads your game.

entries.title: Changing with the weather
entries.summary: Hello Weather’s colour scheme changes depending on the temperature outside.

entries.title: Even a kettle
entries.summary: When using Mi’s Smart Kettle, it’ll show an animation with bubbles floating up as it’s boiling water, with the colour getting warmer and warmer.

entries.title: Your return on investment
entries.summary: Foodpanda tells you how much you’ve saved with your Pro subscription, including a calculation of your return on investment.
tags: free delivery on food and groceries

entries.title: Only a few left
entries.summary: If there are only a few bags left in your area, Too Good To Go will let you know at the top of the feed.
tags: only a few surprise bags left

entries.title: Security theatre
entries.summary: 1Password intentionally delays the login process with an unlocking animation. This frames the experience as being safer.
entries.analysis: If you turn off your system animations, you’ll see that you can technically login immediately. The delay exists to set an expectation about the rest of the process.

entries.title: Your orders are safe
entries.summary: If Fiverr are having network issues, their error screen includes a relevant FAQ for sellers and buyers.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this reassures people what happens to their items, purchases and orders during any downtime.
tags: it'll be back up in no time
how will my orders be affected?
did i lose the gig i was setting up?

entries.title: Donating to charity
entries.summary: After donating money to charity through Revolut, you’ll see a blast of confetti.
tags: nice! you just donated to street child

entries.title: Creating your experience
entries.summary: As you create an experience on Airbnb, you’ll be asked questions that complete a card dynamically.
tags: where will you offer your experience?

entries.title: Contextual promos
entries.summary: As soon as you start typing in an airport as your drop-off point, Gojek will show you a banner for an airport promo code.
entries.analysis: As a side note, this would be much better if there was a single click to copy the promo code.
tags: where do you want to go?

entries.title: Need directions inside the terminal?
entries.summary: Grab recognises when you’re in an airport, and will show you directions to their pick-up point, complete with photos and short videos that guide you through sections of the airport.

entries.title: Showing (and hiding) context
entries.summary: If you start browsing ride options on Bolt, they’ll expand the rows to contextualise the benefit of each (e.g., faster, seating for 6).

entries.title: Your last scanned item
entries.summary: While shopping in-store with Waitrose, your last scanned item is dynamically shown inside your shopping lists.
entries.analysis: i.e., this stops you having to flick back and fourth between your basket and your list so frequently.

entries.title: Verify by sending a code
entries.summary: Gojek lets you verify an account via WhatsApp, by sending them a unique code. This may be easier than receiving one, as WhatsApp messages don’t appear in iOS’ pre-fill.
entries.analysis: i.e., you don’t need to go between apps more than once, to find and copy/paste a one-time code.

entries.title: 55% preferred this drop-off
entries.summary: When giving your destination as an airport, Grab will use social proof to recommend which drop-off areas to aim for.

entries.title: Attaching your flight
entries.summary: If you’re scheduling a Bolt in advance, to pick you up from an airport, they’ll pre-filtered flight options that match, so you can easily attach yours.
entries.analysis: This makes it easy for you to attach a flight, which automatically adjusts your times for delays.

entries.title: Revolut is not calling you
entries.summary: To help catch users who may be falling for a scam, Revolut will warn you if you're on the phone to someone who isn't them.

entries.title: A celebration for sharing
entries.summary: When you click to copy a sharing URL on Lovable, the screen will burst with love hearts.

entries.title: Picking suitable emojis
entries.summary: Finch will assign appropriate emojis to custom tasks, if you don't manually give them one.

entries.title: Having network issues?
entries.summary: If you’re having network issues while chatting to Gemini, they’ll let you know.
entries.analysis: This tries to avoid the frustration of chatting at it for ages, and then realising the WiFi had dropped.

entries.title: You are this far
entries.summary: Although John Lewis has an infinitely scrolling feed, they’ll periodically break the feed to tell you how far you’ve gone.
entries.analysis: This does two things: 1. It contextualises how many items you’ve looked at, which may nudge you to make a decision (i.e., “I’ve seen 100 items, I can stop browsing now”. 2. It anchors where you are, so you have more of an idea how far to scroll in the future, if you wanted to find those items.

entries.title: Because of your share
entries.summary: Substack makes sharing newsletters feel worthwhile, by celebrating when they get a new subscriber because of you.

entries.title: This is highly competitive
entries.summary: After listing an item, eBay will use social proof to nudge you into promoting that item.
entries.analysis: Note: this is good UX, but it could be considered too pressuring. It's on the fence.

entries.title: A riddle while you wait
entries.summary: For long loading screens, Gojek will throw up a variety of riddles for you to think about.
tags: while waiting, we got a riddle for you
how was it? did you get that right?

entries.title: Demo it, without thinking
entries.summary: Inside the Netflix feed, they’ll promote their new way to search with clickable examples.
entries.analysis: This increases the likelihood that people actually try it, because they don’t need to actually think of something to search.
tags: try a new way to search

entries.title: Dynamic celebrations
entries.summary: After completing a custom goal, Finch will dynamically change the confirmation notification to be thematically relevant to the task.

entries.title: Contextualising the day
entries.summary: As you scroll through your calendar on Timepage, it’ll show “Weeks from today” as an overlay.
entries.analysis: This maintan a sense of where you are, and contextualises that date from today.

entries.title: Dynamic background colours
entries.summary: Apple Books will dynamically change the background colour of the screen, depending on the book cover art.
ui_elements: Navigation bar with close and menu icons

entries.title: Do you need to book now?
entries.summary: If you make an Airbnb search for tomorrow, they'll contextualise why you should use their "Instant Book" filter.
tags: find places you can book right away.

entries.title: Automatically unlocking your phone
entries.summary: While driving (and navigating) with your iPhone locked, Apple Maps will automatically unlock / re-lock as you approach junctions.

entries.title: Remind me to check in
entries.summary: If you go to pause your streaks on Finch, they’ll suggest setting a reminder to check back in later.

entries.title: Hiding your username
entries.summary: While sharing an achievement badge on Reddit, you can show or hide your name and avatar.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you wanted to brag online, without exposing all the weird subreddits you follow.

entries.title: Opting into a free trial
entries.summary: Komoot has a toggle to opt into the free trial. This might psychologically motivate people to use it more, as it feels like they took an action to get it.
entries.analysis: i.e., in a small way, they feel like the "earned" or "found" a free trial.

entries.title: 121 days on a treadmill
entries.summary: Tesla will give you badges based on your charging usage, and contextualise that with something everyday, like walking on a treadmill.

entries.title: Tap to see the map
entries.summary: When browsing routes in Komoot, you can tap the map to swap it round with the image, so that you can view it larger.
entries.analysis: i.e., without this, navigating / browsing through options would be much harder, because the map would be so small.

entries.title: Here's your QR code
entries.summary: While scanning for QR codes, Lego show you an illustration of exactly where to find them on your instructions.

entries.title: Stop scrolling
entries.summary: If you swipe up the Go app (a step counter), instead of refreshing the feed, it’ll give you a random motivational quote to get outside.
tags: nature is cooler than your screen. go!

entries.title: I don't want to forget
entries.summary: After signing up, Komoot will remind you (twice), that you’ll be notified before the trial ends. This lowers the anxiety of forgetting, and reduces the number of people who instantly cancel.

entries.title: Where do you rank?
entries.summary: To make your yearly recap more engaging and memorable, Monzo will quiz you on where you rank for specific things.
entries.analysis: This is another mechanism for getting people to pay attention. It turns a (somewhat boring) slideshow of facts into a more interactive mini-game.

entries.title: Nudging a sign up
entries.summary: If you earn a badge without having an account on Wordle, they’ll leverage that moment to get you to create one.

entries.title: Go check if there's a fire
entries.summary: Google won’t let you silence a fire alarm if you’re not physically close to it.
entries.analysis: This is a piece of intentional friction, designed to get you to pay attention to the alert. i.e., the use of UX here is to not let you accidentally silence it when there's a real fire.

entries.title: Teaching you in the moment
entries.summary: After giving a vague image creation prompt, Gemini will teach you about refining images after one has been generated.

entries.title: Your custom card
entries.summary: If you customise a new physical Revolut card, your design will show in the app too.

entries.title: Wipe away this AI dust
entries.summary: During the ChatGPT annual recap, they’ll ask you to wipe away the screen to reveal a statistic.

entries.title: Monzo's hook for insurance
entries.summary: If you abandon a new home insurance quote on Monzo, they’ll offer to set a reminder for when your current policy ends.
tags: we'll save your quote for 30 days.
when did you get your current policy?

entries.title: Shake your head to reply
entries.summary: After connecting a pair of AirPods, Apple will teach you new gestures that you might not have known, and prompt you to turn those features on.
tags: respond to siri by moving your head

entries.title: Stop shouting
entries.summary: If you’re talking on WhatsApp, but holding the phone away from your face, it’ll prompt you to turn on the speaker.

entries.title: Interactive presentations
entries.summary: To add some interactivity into your yearly review, Monzo will ask you to brush or blow confetti off the screen to reveal a statistic.
entries.analysis: By requiring some input, it's more likely to grab your attention (and people may find it more enjoyable).
tags: blow or brush to reveal your legend

entries.title: Dynamic onboarding benefits
entries.summary: During onboarding, Wispr Flow asks you what you’re going to use it for, and then demonstrates the app in action in the exact context you specified.
entries.analysis: Even better, if you choose more than one context, it’ll follow with a quick cycle through all the scenarios/apps.
tags: and yes, flow works in any app you like

entries.title: Saving your discount for later
entries.summary: If you try using a voucher that isn’t valid for your order, Foodpanda will automatically add it to your wallet to use later, so you don’t lose it.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this reduces the loss aversion of "not using this voucher", and instead of feeling like it's wasted money, reframes it as a discount for later.

entries.title: Pushing for notifications
entries.summary: If your notifications aren't turned on with Etsy, but you place an order, they'll prompt you to turn them on, citing specific benefits to that order.

entries.title: Opt-in for discounts
entries.summary: Flighty has an in-app prompt to sign up for a discount alerts. By taking the action to specifically ask for discounts, users will be psychologically primed to react to it when they do.
entries.analysis: This makes it effortless to consider, and the “max 2 emails/year” makes it more palatable.

entries.title: 5 hours ago
entries.summary: When returning to a long document on Microsoft word, they’ll show you where you were last time.

entries.title: Is this taking too long?
entries.summary: If searching for a device on SmartLife takes more than a few seconds, they’ll show you a link to the instructions.

entries.title: Giving you a 500-word target
entries.summary: When you first start using Wispr flow, they'll contextualise your usage, and set you a target of 500 spoken words to unlock your "super stats".

entries.title: Gathering data for others
entries.summary: If you take a different route to the one suggested by Google Maps, it will ask you why at the end of your trip.
tags: wanted to go a different way

entries.title: A welcome return
entries.summary: If you’ve been away from Flighty and reinstall it after a long time, it’ll welcome you back with a prompt to enable two of its most valuable features.

entries.title: Dynamic query add-ons
entries.summary: As you start a search on Etsy, they’ll show dynamic query add-ons.
entries.analysis: These work in a single click, and also help to demonstrate the types of search you can make. i.e., this is about discovery as much as it is an actual search query.

entries.title: You're 3rd globally
entries.summary: To contextualise just how much you listen to a particular artist, Spotify will tell you a specific global rank.
entries.analysis: This also creates a much more sharable moment, because “3rd in the world” is easier to digest than “top 0.001%”.
tags: snow patrol is your top artist.

entries.title: Where is my parcel?
entries.summary: If you go to message a seller who you’ve recently bought an item from, they’ll dynamically show your order status at the top.
tags: request help with an order issue

entries.title: Turning delays into permission
entries.summary: If you look up a flight on Flighty that is delayed, it’ll prompt you to get delay alerts as a way to enable notifications.

entries.title: Headspace will motivate you
entries.summary: After a Headspace meditation, you’ll be shown a motivational quote underneath your streak stats.

entries.title: Nudge your family
entries.summary: If you're sharing the Duolingo Max plan, you'll be given a selection of nudges to use. These are pre-filled with that user's data (like their current streak).

entries.title: Did we solve your issue?
entries.summary: After chatting to Stripe’s customer support chatbot they’ll ask if you solved your issue. If they didn’t, they’ll give you more options to change that.
entries.analysis: To be clear, I think most people would rather have these options available immediately, but at least they don't let you leave with an unresolved issue.
tags: before you go, did we solve your issue?

entries.title: Forwarding emails to Flighty
entries.summary: Instead of manually finding your flights, Flighty lets you forward your booking confirmation to them, so it can automatically parse and add your trips.
entries.analysis: The upsell here is great too. It catches people after they’ve recognised the value, at the moment when they’re faced with actually bothering to do the hard work (of forwarding emails).

entries.title: Emailing your employer
entries.summary: Inside the feature to bring your salary into Monzo, there’s a pre-written email to send to your employer, that includes your bank details.

entries.title: Strava's recap
entries.summary: In your yearly recap, Strava will contextualise how you’ve done compared to their other users.

entries.title: Showcasing the uses
entries.summary: To showcase all the apps you can use Wispr Flow in, their onboarding includes app icons you can cycle through and click on to test out.
entries.analysis: They’ll also load a video tutorial in picture-in-picture mode, so it overlays the demo you’re being shown.
tags: send your first message with flow

entries.title: Select your weight
entries.summary: When selecting a weight on Cal AI, you slide a scale which reacts with haptic feedback.

entries.title: Confirm the magic
entries.summary: During Wispr Flow’s onboarding, they’ll visually show you how to record your voice for the first time, and then “confirm the magic” with a CTA.

entries.title: Testing your colours
entries.summary: Yahoo Mail will show you theme colours with a dynamic illustration above, so you can roughly preview the changes.

entries.title: Clearer expectations
entries.summary: When setting a goal on Cal AI, they’ll contextualise each option so you can pick something realistic, and with clearer expectations.
entries.analysis: Also, the selected icon animates—which is a nice touch.

entries.title: Onboarding easter egg
entries.summary: If you interact with the example notification during Blip’s sign-up, they’ll show you an easter egg where they explain that their notifications are useful.

entries.title: Playing iOS video instructions
entries.summary: Wispr Flow will ask you to enable access in your iOS input settings. Clicking will take you to the settings, with a picture-in-picture video on your screen to remind you of the instructions.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic use of the native iOS video playback. Also, the video will automatically end once the setting permission is granted.

entries.title: Nudge your friends
entries.summary: Duolingo will encourage you to nudge friends (in a single tap), which acts as a hook to bring them back into the app.

entries.title: Holiday magic
entries.summary: Cal AI have a setting for turning on “Holiday Magic”.

entries.title: Paying by QR code
entries.summary: Wise let’s you pay someone by scanning a QR code. But, predicting that people may have screenshotted and sent them to each other, will let you upload one instead.
tags: scan to pay with a qr code supporting

entries.title: Rewarding early risers
entries.summary: If you extend your streak early in the day, Duolingo will reward you by telling you how well you did compared to everyone else.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this nudges people into completing their streak early in the day, and not waiting until the last minute.

entries.title: Photograph your bookshelf
entries.summary: Instead of manually adding books one by one, Tome gives you the option to take a photo of your bookshelf during onboarding so it can recommend communities based on your reading list.
tags: upload a photo of your bookshelf
you are now part of these communities!

entries.title: Here's what you've unlocked
entries.summary: After subscribing to Spotify Premium, you’ll see a breakdown of the features you’ve unlocked (but might not know about).

entries.title: A waxing crescent?
entries.summary: When looking at the moon visibility on Apple Weather, you can drag the timeline and watch the shadows move in real time.

entries.title: Look, it's us!
entries.summary: If you’re on a Duolingo family plan, you’ll see all of your avatars together in a banner.

entries.title: Personalised referral links
entries.summary: If you share a Wise referral link, your name will be used on the landing page.
entries.analysis: This makes the referral feel more personal, more real, and likely increases the conversion rate of this landing page.

entries.title: Scanning a receipt
entries.summary: During onboarding, Billy encourages you to scan a receipt to try their features. But if you don’t have one, they'll use a sample receipt as a demo.
entries.analysis: This is a great way to demonstrate a realistic Aha! Moment, without forcing the user to upload a receipt.

entries.title: The most in a day
entries.summary: To give you a shot of motivation, Duolingo will tell you when you've broken a daily record.
entries.analysis: Importantly, you're not working towards this milestone, it comes as a nice suprise, which makes you feel good about your progress.

entries.title: Finding the best emoji
entries.summary: When adding a to-do task, Tiimo will automatically try to find a matching emoji based on the task you typed.

entries.title: Searching for the right image
entries.summary: When setting up a new trip, Tripsy automatically searches for images based on your trip name.
entries.analysis: This reduces friction for you to immediately personalise your trip.

entries.title: You're in check
entries.summary: During a game of Duolingo chess, your avatars will react if they're put into check.

entries.title: Timed activities
entries.summary: While completing a timed activity on Timespent, you'll see a random motivational quote.

entries.title: Just tell me what you have
entries.summary: Under My Roof is an inventory tracking app. Instead of manually entering every item, you can use a Quick Entry Mode to point at things and say their names and quantities out loud.
entries.analysis: The app will take a photo and automatically fill out relevant fields based on what you said.

entries.title: Did you win or lose?
entries.summary: After playing a real person at Duolingo chess, both of your avatars will react to the win or loss.

entries.title: Completing your tasks
entries.summary: When you've complete your tasks, Tiimo will show each of them as balls dropping from the top of the screen to fill it up, giving you a visual sense of your accomplishments for the day.

entries.title: Exploring sample activities
entries.summary: To prevent a blank canvas, Timespent will let you add demo activities in one tap. This allows you to explore the app and how their features will work.
entries.analysis: This helps you see the vision of how everything could look once your data is entered.

entries.title: Adding to basket
entries.summary: Amazon Prime has a single "Add to Basket" button when showing ads on a TV.

entries.title: Upload an invoice
entries.summary: Wise will let you upload a PDF invoice, and will pull out recipient details automatically.
entries.analysis: i.e., if someone has sent you an invoice, you wouldn't need to copy and paste every field individually.
tags: we use ai to process your document.

entries.title: Intentional friction
entries.summary: When restoring a Coinbase wallet, you’ll be forced to wait for a cooldown timer. This intentional friction may help you recognise fraud.
entries.analysis: i.e., by being forced to stop, you’re more likely to read the text above the button, and (hopefully) notice the signs of a scam.

entries.title: Pasting a WhatsApp message
entries.summary: PayTM will let you paste an entire message, and then automatically pull out and input any bank details.

entries.title: When will you be free?
entries.summary: After saving your first entry on Day One, they’ll ask if you want to be reminded to journal regularly, defaulting to the current time as your “daily reminder”.
entries.analysis: i.e., the best indication of when you’ll be free to journal in the future, is probably when you journaled today.
tags: set the time for your daily reminder:

entries.title: Left-handed light switches
entries.summary: Philips Hue has a left-handed mode that swaps the brightness and colour controls to make them easier to access, depending on which hand you use.

entries.title: 30 min cool down
entries.summary: Nike lets you cancel an order, without penalty, for half an hour after being placed.

entries.title: Stop clicking me
entries.summary: If you click on Claude’s animated bubble, it’ll ask you how it can help. Click it again, and it’ll change the message.

entries.title: Hyping small creators on YouTube
entries.summary: YouTube are experimenting with a feature called “Hyping”, where every week you can give points to 3 small creators, for videos published in the last week.
entries.analysis: This is likely testing a few things; can we help surface better content from unknown creators, and how can small channels with ultra-fans actually make more noise? It's great that after hyping, you see the number roll up in realtime—this demonstrates your impact on the score.

entries.title: Dynamic hero images
entries.summary: As you complete the Nike “Find a shoe” form, they’ll dynamically show and edit shoes that match your criteria in the background.
entries.analysis: i.e., this creates the impression that Nike actually is helping you find the perfect shoe, and you can see some of the work that's going into that process. This is a great example of the Labour Illusion.

entries.title: Calculators aren't always boring
entries.summary: If you calculate a number bigger than 15 digits on the “Not Boring Calculator”, they’ll show you a written easter egg.

entries.title: A loading trick
entries.summary: Sora have a loading trick to help the “similar” content feel endless. They’ll load a few, and if you swipe to see the second, they’ll immediately load more.
entries.analysis: i.e., if they loaded 10 “similar” videos for everything, it’d feel really slow.

entries.title: Multiple angles to view
entries.summary: Freeletics will show you a video of every workout activity, and they’ll let you change the camera angle at any time.
entries.analysis: This is useful, because sometimes a particular exercise requires a stretch, or something that isn’t obvious in one fixed angle.

entries.title: Changing the default tab
entries.summary: Nike will default to either the “Men” or “Women” tab, depending on your logged in profile.
entries.analysis: This is a really small detail (almost boring), but it's really common to miss this little trick.

entries.title: A glowing chart
entries.summary: When loading a trading chart on Revolut, they’ll briefly emphasise the line with a glowing animation.

entries.title: Filling the dead air
entries.summary: While Lovable is working (or waiting), they’ll show a carousel of educational tips.
entries.analysis: This helps to fill some of the dead air that you create while waiting for the AI to “think”.

entries.title: Reducing the pressure
entries.summary: When selecting your fitness level on Freeletics, they’ll contextualise the bar with written descriptions.

entries.title: The anatomy of a shoe
entries.summary: If you make a major change to a custom shoe on Nike, they’ll show you context about what that change actually means.
entries.analysis: i.e., without this, you might feel like you’re not educated enough in shoes to make good decisions.

entries.title: Dynamically changing thumbnails
entries.summary: To help avoid content blindness, Netflix will routinely change cover art and title fonts.

entries.title: Pasting items on Claude
entries.summary: If you paste more than 600 words into Claude, it’ll add it as an attachment instead of putting it all into the input field.
entries.analysis: This helps to keep the input section clear for your actual question (i.e., they’ve assumed your pasting something to read / process, and not a question itself).

entries.title: Pulling your profile
entries.summary: When asking if you want to import your settings into ChatGPT Atlas, they’ll dynamically pull your email address.

entries.title: Are you sure you've saved it?
entries.summary: When creating an account on Mullvad VPN, you need to save your account number. To nudge you into actually saving it, you need to tick a box that blurs the number before you can proceed.
entries.analysis: i.e., the process of checking that box, and then seeing the number blur out, is what reminds you that it’s probably important. It’s a visual cue.
tags: have you saved your account number?

entries.title: Encouraging you to click
entries.summary: Peacock will show live scores and stats directly inside the feed, designed to make that game feel interesting to watch.

entries.title: Contextually aware design
entries.summary: Netflix use a different episode selection design for adults profiles and children’s profiles (no description, bigger images).

entries.title: Getting extended limits
entries.summary: To nudge you into saving ChatGPT Atlas as your default browser, they’ll offer 7 days of extended limits.
entries.analysis: This relies on the fact that many people will either enjoy Atlas, or be too lazy to switch back.

entries.title: Comparing prices on hover
entries.summary: If you hover over the price name (i.e., to copy it), AO will show you a price comparison of other retailers.
entries.analysis: In other words, they're noticing a moment of intention (you're about to copy the name to paste it somewhere else), and intercepting it to keep you on the page.

entries.title: Continue your journey on foot
entries.summary: As you approach your destination, Google Maps shows a button to launch a walking navigation the rest of the way.
entries.analysis: i.e., if the car park isn't actually at the location, or if you've parked down the road, this helps you quickly continue your journey without having to follow a driving route.

entries.title: Robinhood isn't calling you
entries.summary: To help reduce fraud, Robinhood will notice if you’re on the phone to anyone while using their app, and remind you that you’re not speaking to them.

entries.title: Sora's sharing hack
entries.summary: If you go to share an AI video on Sora, using someone's clone, they’ll show that real person at the top of your share list.
entries.analysis: This encourages you to share it with them, who may in turn share it more broadly.

entries.title: Vinted holiday mode
entries.summary: Vinted has a holiday mode which temporarily hides your items—this prevents scenarios where someone buys your item but you aren’t around to post it.
entries.analysis: This is likely to reduce churn, because it makes it clear that uploading 100s of items to Vinted doesn't mean you have to take them all down manually when you go on holiday for a few days.

entries.title: Delivery estimations
entries.summary: When showing a carousel of supermarket options, Uber Eats will show an estimation of delivery times, so you can quickly see which are most suitable.
entries.analysis: i.e., if an item is missing for a recipe, you might need it really quickly.

entries.title: Closing a pocket
entries.summary: After you close a pocket in Revolut, it'll quite literally disappear and dissolve into the screen.
ui_elements: Action buttons (Add money, Move, Info, More)

entries.title: Dynamic illustrations
entries.summary: During onboarding, Preply will animate the illustrations in response to your input.

entries.title: Turning a title into suggestions
entries.summary: After naming a listing, Vinted automatically takes your title and the uploaded images to suggest recommendations for the brand, category, size, and colours.

entries.title: A profile worth keeping
entries.summary: To leverage the Endowment Effect (and framing), ChatGPT will show you when you joined both ChatGPT and Atlas, as a profile badge.
entries.analysis: This reframes Atlas as a brand you already trust (i.e., you've trusted them for 700+ days already), and gives you something to lose (your profile badge).
tags: you're all set — welcome to atlas!

entries.title: The Dogs of Amazon
entries.summary: On error pages, Amazon will sometimes show a dog from their “Dogs of Amazon” list.
tags: sorry something went wrong on our end

entries.title: You made a good choice
entries.summary: During onboarding, Speechify will ask you to select a voice. They’ll follow it up with social proof about how many other people listen to that voice.

entries.title: Upselling contents insurance
entries.summary: If you change your address on Monzo, it assumes you’re moving and suggests contents insurance.
entries.analysis: i.e., they've identified a great moment to upsell the user.

entries.title: A dynamic cursor
entries.summary: While observing someone else on FigJam, their cursor represents the tool they’re using.

entries.title: Displaying previous references
entries.summary: In order to not clutter the interface, Starling will only show previous payment references as suggestions after you’ve clicked into that field.

entries.title: Helping you price this
entries.summary: Vinted helps you set an appropriate price for your item by automatically pulling up listings of the same (or similar) items.

entries.title: Dynamic post-review animations
entries.summary: Google Maps does a dynamic animation that changes depending on what type of establishment you review.
tags: see posts on your public profile >

entries.title: 27,781 people are like you
entries.summary: When prompting you to upgrade, Speechify will show social proof of how many other people started the trial this week.
entries.analysis: The specificity of this number, in both the number of subscribers, and the recency of it being "this week", makes this more effective. It's a form of very specific social proof.
tags: try speechify premium free for 7 days

entries.title: A Lego explosion
entries.summary: To help create short moments of celebration, Lego will periodically suprise you with exploding lego pieces after finishing a step.

entries.title: Should we remember this?
entries.summary: When you change the playback speed of a podcast, Apple offers to remember that setting for all episodes of that show.
tags: save custom settings for this show?

entries.title: Small, Medium or Large?
entries.summary: Vinted contextualises parcel size by giving a visual description of what small, medium, and large means, and auto-recommends a size based on the item itself.
entries.analysis: This works because it's easier to visualise "a shoebox", rather than "15 inches by 7 inches". By being easier to visualise, it reduces the effort of making this decision.
tags: for items that'd fit in a shoebox.
for items that'd fit in a moving box.

entries.title: Waiting on hold
entries.summary: While waiting on hold for Robinhood support, you get a notification giving you a heads up that it’s almost your turn, along with which number they’re going to call you from.

entries.title: Interactive onboarding
entries.summary: When setting up a new Apple Pen, you’ll be shown interactive shortcuts that you can try for yourself.

entries.title: Likely out of stock
entries.summary: If you add an item to your DoorDash basket that is likely out of stock, they’ll suggest a substitute as a back-up.
entries.analysis: To be clear, it'd be better if it actually knew if it was out of stock. But assuming that there's some technical limitation here, this is a nice UX back-up plan. It gives people reassurance that the reserve item won't be awful.

entries.title: Contextualising the validation
entries.summary: When naming a board in Miro, they’ll actually tell you how many characters you need to remove if you go over the limit.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is easier to digest than “maximum of 30 characters”, because you know exactly what you need to do (and how many to remove).

entries.title: Contacting the team
entries.summary: After sending a message to the Citymapper team, they’ll include an on-brand easter egg of how long your message took to send.
entries.analysis: i.e., in the same way they shown when the next train arrives.

entries.title: A hidden crop button
entries.summary: If you zoom into an image on Apple’s photos app, they’ll automatically show the crop button.

entries.title: Custom shopping feeds
entries.summary: On DoorDash, you can paste a shopping list and then see a custom feed of all the items you need.
ui_elements: Product cards with images and prices

entries.title: Multi-zone scrubbing
entries.summary: On Apple Podcasts, you can start scrolling the playbar and move your finger down (or up), to change scrub speed.

entries.title: Thoughtful onboarding
entries.summary: If you send multiple negative prompts in a row, Lovable will trigger onboarding for creating a plan.
tags: use chat mode to create a plan.

entries.title: Driving or walking?
entries.summary: Google Maps will default to walking or driving, depending on how far away your destination is.

entries.title: Visualising your investment
entries.summary: When buying a stock on Trading 212, you’ll see a green ring representing how much of your available funds you’re spending.
entries.analysis: This frames your investment, and may make it easier to do things like "okay, I'll invest 25% of my funds in this", without actually having to work out a specific dollar value.

entries.title: Matching your vibe
entries.summary: Spotify will show different playlists in the feed depending on the time of day.

entries.title: Onboarding in A1
entries.summary: When looking at an empty Google Sheet, the first cell will cycle between shortcuts.

entries.title: Specific examples
entries.summary: When testing your internet speeds on EE, they’ll try and contextualise what the speeds mean.
entries.analysis: The specificity of the examples is important here—it's memorable. You can visualise 8 TVs streaming Lord of the Rings.

entries.title: You invested at this price
entries.summary: When looking at a stock chart, Trading 212 will show a line for your average position cost.

entries.title: Clarifying credit usage
entries.summary: When you look at your credit balance, Lovable reminds you that you get 5 free daily credits, and dynamically indicates when you're using them.
entries.analysis: This is a clever way of encouraging a habit. There's an incentive to log in every day.

entries.title: Looking for something specific?
entries.summary: If you scroll up through a long chain of Teams messages, you’ll be reminded how to search inside the chat.
tags: press ⌘ f to find in this chat

entries.title: Removing the tip
entries.summary: If you remove the tip from a donation, GoFundMe will contextualise that the money is used to keep the platform running and help others.

entries.title: Action-triggered onboarding
entries.summary: When pasting a presentation outline, Gamma will ask you if you need help with splitting up the content.
entries.analysis: This both onboards the concept of “cards”, and acts as a shortcut to help you get started on the presentation itself.
tags: you can also type --- to add breaks

entries.title: Demonstrating the why
entries.summary: Inside the home feed, Too Good To Go show you a quiz about why manufacturers waste food. This then contextualises why their service is so valuable.
entries.analysis: What’s important here is that the quiz makes you think about the core issue. Then, by being interactive, it forces the user to actually engage with the task.

entries.title: Your speakers are muted
entries.summary: If you join a call with your speakers muted, Zoom will show this reminder.

entries.title: Rendering your preview
entries.summary: When publishing a Notion page, the preview dynamically shows a render of your actual content.

entries.title: Counting supporters
entries.summary: When you first load a GoFundMe campaign, the number of supporters is animated to tick up a few numbers.
entries.analysis: This creates the sense of progression and community. i.e., it feels like people are doing it right now.

entries.title: Shared with you
entries.summary: When playing songs that have been shared with you from a friend, their name appears underneath the playbar.
entries.analysis: Clicking on their name then links to your chat with them (i.e., how they shared it). This reminds you about the social aspect of Spotify, and could encourage you to share songs with others.

entries.title: Getting to 80%
entries.summary: While plugged in, iOS will predict how long it will take to get to 80% charge.

entries.title: Your donation matters
entries.summary: After donating to a GoFundMe campaign, you’ll see a yellow bump on the progress bar. This makes your donation feel like it was more impactful.

entries.title: Notion + Google Meets
entries.summary: If you have Notion open in the background and launch a Google Meet, it offers to start AI meeting notes automatically

entries.title: Great vitamin boost
entries.summary: After adding items into your food tracker, Yazio will briefly show a key benefit of that item.
entries.analysis: This helps you feel good about what you’re eating, and therefore what you’re tracking.

entries.title: Abandoned your basket
entries.summary: The first time you abandon a checkout, Shop will teach you that your basket will be synced between Shop and the online store.

entries.title: Onboarding, on time
entries.summary: When creating a private folder in Granola, they’ll show contextual onboarding inline.
entries.analysis: This is displayed every time, and helps remind you why you would use a private folder in the first place.

entries.title: You can use this code later
entries.summary: If you create a Lego party, but then leave, you’ll be reassured that you can join again using the same party code.
entries.analysis: This is useful if you’ve already shared the code, and want to join the party later in the day.

entries.title: Top 29% of users
entries.summary: After reaching a daily streak on MyFitnessPal, you’ll be told how you compare to other users.

entries.title: Squeezing onboarding into the feed
entries.summary: When looking at your achievements on Waze, if you haven’t used the “report by voice” feature yet, it’ll show onboarding in the carousel.

entries.title: Finding more items on Shop
entries.summary: Shop dynamically populates the “Find more ideas” section inside a Collection, to relate to the first item you added.
entries.analysis: i.e., whichever product you use to create a new collection, they'll dynamically change the images in this prompt.

entries.title: Dynamic tab labels
entries.summary: To help reduce tab width, Granola will automatically minimise inactive items (e.g., “Joined Folders” becomes “Joined”).

entries.title: This restaurant is closed
entries.summary: If you visit a store on Uber Eats that is closed, it’ll suggest similar shops that are open, or let you schedule an order.

entries.title: Slides' progressive onboarding
entries.summary: If you hold down a key to move an item in Google Slides, they’ll onboard a shortcut to move that item more quickly.
entries.analysis: This is a great form of progressive onboarding, with a shortcut that is otherwise fairly obscure.

entries.title: Removing distractions from recordings
entries.summary: While mirroring and recording an iPhone on Quicktime, it’ll show full signal and battery.

entries.title: Editing your profile
entries.summary: While editing your profile, Etsy will name the component in the action bar to contextualise what you're doing.
entries.analysis: This sounds trivial, but it helps you understand what the "discard" action means. i.e., what changes you're actually discarding.

entries.title: Reducing customer service frustration
entries.summary: When tracking a parcel, UPS mentions that you’re looking at the same details as a customer service agent would, so that you don’t contact their support team expecting more information.
entries.analysis: This reduces the frustration of waiting on the phone, and being disappointed by the outcome.

entries.title: Ramp's invoice messaging
entries.summary: On Ramp, you can comment on invoices. They’ll dynamically change the placeholder message and clarify that the other party will be notified.
entries.analysis: This reduces the anxiety of commenting, and the other party missing the message (which in turn, would hurt motivation to use this rather than simply emailing the person).

entries.title: Hiking on Citymapper
entries.summary: For walks longer than 20 minutes, Citymapper changes the walking man icon to a hiking man icon.

entries.title: Splitting Airbnb stays
entries.summary: For longer stays, Airbnb automatically shows you options to split your stay between two nearby properties.
entries.analysis: This cleverly helps to increase the number of options, and perceived value, of using Airbnb.

entries.title: What our AI can do
entries.summary: If you hover over the (i) when using PostHog’s AI, they’ll tell you what it can and can’t do.
entries.analysis: This helps to set clearer expectations, and avoid disappointment.

entries.title: Publishing on Figma Make
entries.summary: While publishing a site on Figma Make, they’ll reassure you that you can continue working while it processes.

entries.title: Etsy's earnings calculator
entries.summary: While adding an item to your Etsy shop, they’ll give you a simple calculator to work out your estimated earnings, with embedded onboarding.
entries.analysis: i.e., if your estimated earnings are below 0, they'll show onboarding about how to better price your items.

entries.title: Only you can see this offer
entries.summary: When showing an inline upsell, Slack clarifies that only team owners can see it. This removes the anxiety that someone else will see it and sign up.
entries.analysis: Without this, the upsell might actually worry you (as a team owner), because someone else on your team might sign you all up. Plus, because you know that you're the only one seeing it, it puts the responsibility on you to make a decision.

entries.title: Better Open Graph images
entries.summary: Mobbin dynamically changes their social Open Graph images based on the collection, author and content.

entries.title: Designed for children
entries.summary: Storytel has a “child” mode, which removes certain features like filters and notifications.

entries.title: Sentry's marketing mode
entries.summary: Sentry's website allows you to turn off "marketing mode", which replaces the typical landing page content with an AI chatbot that'll discuss their features.
entries.analysis: To be clear, that may not convert any better—it's not something I'd rush out and copy. But it's an interesting idea at least, and demonstrates Sentry's personality. i.e., it may boost brand perception, even if the new mode doesn't actually generate sales.

entries.title: Subscription set to expire
entries.summary: If you’ve set your Yazio subscription to expire, they’ll show a new menu item full of reasons to stay subscribed.
entries.analysis: This is a great way to frame the benefits, and trigger Loss Aversion (i.e., look at all the great stuff you'll lose).

entries.title: Tiny AI thumbnails
entries.summary: While Manus AI is working through your request, it’ll dynamically show small thumbnails of what it’s doing.
entries.analysis: You obviously can't see the detail of what's happening, but it's just enough that you can work out when it's looking at a webpage (for example). This helps both build confidence in what it's doing, and makes the wait more bearable.

entries.title: Granular notification options
entries.summary: If you reject notification permissions during sign-up, Co-Star will give you a more granular choice about which notifications are available.
entries.analysis: This is like a double-dip, giving people a chance to accept some of them. But, and it's worth stressing, this could be considered annoying—you'd need to test it.

entries.title: Changing the ticket icon
entries.summary: Dice shows different icons on the digital ticket stub, depending on what the ticket type is.

entries.title: Typo or onboarding
entries.summary: If Google Docs notices a typo, but doesn’t have a suggestion, it’ll dynamically show onboarding instead.

entries.title: A newsletter's onboarding
entries.summary: The newsletter Every gives every new member a to-do list exploring the features of their subscription.
entries.analysis: This helps highlight some of the features, even if the user goes straight to their homepage.

entries.title: Clerk's AI prompt
entries.summary: During set-up, Clerk includes a pre-built prompt that you can copy into an AI tool of your choice.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is a prompt designed to help AI coding tools integrate Clerk.

entries.title: A subtle way to learn more
entries.summary: On this "action" step, Dice shows a nudge to scroll to learn how that feature works.
entries.analysis: Importantly, the height of the content is set so that if you don't scroll, they've not distracted the main "action" area. i.e., you can only see it on scroll.

entries.title: More comfortable sharing
entries.summary: After clicking to share a trip that you've already been on, Airbnb will show context that details about your reservation will be hidden.
entries.analysis: This reduces the anxiety about sharing a trip in the first place.

entries.title: Revolut's progressive onboarding
entries.summary: After deleting a card in Revolut, and then going back to view them all, they’ll show a tooltip showing how you could get that card back.

entries.title: Forward this to your team
entries.summary: After signing up to the project management tool Breeze, you’ll get an email that you can forward to your team for them to join.
entries.analysis: This is a great way of lowering the friction of inviting your team, without having to build loads of functionality.

entries.title: FigJam's automatic naming
entries.summary: If you copy & paste a sequential board in FigJam (e.g., “Option 1”), it’ll automatically rename the copy “Option 2”.

entries.title: Swipe to sprint
entries.summary: As you swipe to refresh the Reddit app, the mascot will move into a ready position, and then start running.
entries.analysis: This isn't just a fun animation, the transition from "ready" to "running" actually guides you about how far you need to swipe up to refresh.

entries.title: Dice's wait list
entries.summary: When you join a wait list on Dice, they’ll prompt you to allow notifications specifically for that event.
entries.analysis: Note: this only appears if you have notifications already turned off. They've framed a reason to turn them on.

entries.title: The next action
entries.summary: After investing stocks or crypto, Revolut will use the confirmation message to contextualise another feature.
entries.analysis: This is a form of progressive disclosure. (i.e., take an action, predict how the user might expand their usage, and suggest that next step).

entries.title: Frictionless upselling
entries.summary: Discord will show free users the available premium themes, letting people instantly change whatever view they're looking at.
entries.analysis: This is a great way of upselling, because the user doesn't have to commit to anything to see what the themes would look like.

entries.title: Suggested field types
entries.summary: Notion will suggest new field types, based on the content already in the table.

entries.title: You've only got 1 minute left
entries.summary: If you attempt to quit the account opening flow, WeBull will contextualise how much longer is left.

entries.title: Creating a payment link
entries.summary: When creating a payment link, Zettle has a line of context to clarify that the customer name is only visible to you.
entries.analysis: This line of context makes it more comfortable for sellers to answer, without having to ask the buyer any questions. i.e., if they felt like the buyer would see their own name, they might ask how to spell it, or be more careful. (Which is unnecessary in this context).

entries.title: Contextualised weight loss (or gain)
entries.summary: During onboarding, FoodLlama will ask you how much weight you want to lose per week, and contextualise that in the time it'll take to reach your goal.

entries.title: Transcribing your audio
entries.summary: After recording a voice note, ChatGPT will reuse the placeholder to make it clear that it’s transcribing your audio while you wait.

entries.title: The difference between 3 and 4 stars
entries.summary: JustEat use framing to contextualise the difference between star ratings, with labels that are more conversational.
entries.analysis: This feeds into the psychological problem where two people have different perceptions for what a 3 star review is. For some, it may mean "good", for others it might be negative. This framing tries to align everybody that "good" is 4 stars.

entries.title: It's WeBull, but lite
entries.summary: WeBull offer a “lite” mode, which strips away a lot of the more advanced interface components.
entries.analysis: This is great for helping people who may be otherwise overloaded. It reduces the Cognitive Load.

entries.title: Flying into your cart
entries.summary: When adding an item into your Zettle cart, you see the item literally fly into the icon.
entries.analysis: This is more than a bit of User Delight, this actually shows you (and draws your attention to) the cart button. i.e., it's a form of onboarding.

entries.title: Framing the benefits of Uber One
entries.summary: If your Uber One subscription is set to cancel, they'll remind you how much you've saved on an order, and use that moment to encourage you to renew.

entries.title: Sign using BSL
entries.summary: When recording an ID video on Starling, you can either say the words on screen, or sign using BSL.

entries.title: An onboarding payment
entries.summary: After setting up “Tap to Pay”, Zettle offers to demonstrate taking a refundable £1 payment as a form of onboarding.

entries.title: A dancing llama
entries.summary: While FoodLlama is analysing a photo, it’ll show an animated llama dancing above your image.

entries.title: A useful empty state
entries.summary: If no matches are found when searching for someone on Wise, the empty state gives you tips on finding people you know.

entries.title: Look, this is your plan
entries.summary: During onboarding, Mob uses a swiping interaction to build your first meal plan. They then dynamically leverage this at the account creation step.

entries.title: Pasting payee details
entries.summary: When adding a payee, Chase have a button to paste all their details in one tap.
entries.analysis: And yes, this pastes into all the fields at once.

entries.title: Conversation prompts
entries.summary: After using a conversation prompt on Fiverr, they’ll keep the CTA disabled until you’ve typed a few words. This helps avoid semi-complete messages.

entries.title: Framing your fees
entries.summary: If the savings are above a certain amount, Wise will frame their fees compared to banks and other providers.
entries.analysis: Clicking on this then shows a breakdown of those options.

entries.title: Really simple onboarding
entries.summary: When taking a photo food entry for the first time, FoodLlama will show you two clear tips with example photos.
entries.analysis: What's great here is that 2 is really easy to digest. There's no faff, and the examples are really clear.

entries.title: Framing the silent benefits
entries.summary: After trying Mob’s AI meal planner, they’ll frame the value of the feature in terms of "time saved planning".
entries.analysis: This is important to help differentiate between “oh, they’re just 10 options I could have found”. The silent benefit of time saved isn’t necessarily obvious—so Mob want to remind you.

entries.title: Automatic tag detection
entries.summary: When creating a task on Linear, specific words will trigger suggested tags. For example, typing "issue" will show the "bugs" tag.
entries.analysis: This also works for custom tags (e.g., "UX Bites" shown).

entries.title: A smarter empty state
entries.summary: When you have an empty inbox, Structured shows an empty state. But after adding a task, they’ll contextually explain the benefits of their Pro plan.
entries.analysis: i.e., there’s not as much value in promoting the Pro plan until people actually have a reason to want it (or understand why it’s valuable). This times the upsell much better.

entries.title: Patreon's playbar
entries.summary: Patreon’s playbar has a dynamic background colour showing the track percentage completion.

entries.title: Capturing intent with Etsy
entries.summary: When creating a gift list on Etsy, you can select an occasion and it’ll dynamically offer to remind you 30 days in advance.
entries.analysis: This is a great way to capture this moment of intent (bothering to save a list) and convert it into a purchase (being reminded, and then actually buying that item).

entries.title: Framing the value of a library
entries.summary: During onboarding, Headway asks you to select titles that you’re interested in. They then use those to contextualise the value of their library page.
entries.analysis: i.e., this frames the "library" feature with items you're already interested in, and have already selected (the Endowment Effect).

entries.title: The order was successful
entries.summary: After ordering an item, Argos briefly shower the screen in confetti.
entries.analysis: This is only worth experimenting on if people make infrequent purchases on your app. For example, if Amazon did this 3 times a day, it'd feel tacky. But for an infrequent (or large) purchase, it may add to the moment. You might then be more likely to remember "Argos" when you next think about making a purchase.

entries.title: Double tap to focus
entries.summary: A single tap on the menu item takes you to Patreon’s search page, but a double tap focuses inside the search field.
entries.analysis: i.e., it's a shortcut to immediately start typing. They never actually teach you this though—they should onboard it after your first search.

entries.title: Mob's cooking mode
entries.summary: The cooking app Mob includes a mode where your screen will stay awake.
entries.analysis: i.e., so your phone screen doesn't turn off while your hands are covered in flour.

entries.title: Embedded AI during onboarding
entries.summary: After creating an account, Slite will take your domain and embed realistic questions inside their onboarding slides.
entries.analysis: In other words, I didn't tell Slite that Built for Mars was a UX resource. It crawled that information, and then (perhaps used AI) to create a question to embed. This makes their onboarding feel far more personalised, and more memorable—you're likely to pay more attention.

entries.title: Hover to compare plans
entries.summary: When hovering over the "upgrade" component on Vercel, you'll dynamically see what your usage limits would be on the new plan.
entries.analysis: This frames the benefit based on your usage (and draws a comparison to your existing plan).

entries.title: Searching multiple categories
entries.summary: When searching on BookBeat, you can select multiple categories at once, and get a breakdown of suggestions for each.
entries.analysis: i.e., rather than merging all your suggestions being merged into a "category A" OR "category B" selection.

entries.title: Your size is in stock
entries.summary: If you're logged in, ASOS will immediately tell you if the size that best fits you is in stock.

entries.title: Making your goal achievable
entries.summary: Headway will ask you what your monthly goal is, and then contextualise how achievable it is.
entries.analysis: This is a great way to frame the value of Headway during onboarding.

entries.title: Pausing the video
entries.summary: If you're watching a show on Dropout and click to share it, they'll automatically pause the video.

entries.title: Dynamic company placeholders
entries.summary: When inviting people to join your team, Devin will dynamically use your domain in their email placeholders.

entries.title: Your device storage
entries.summary: When downloading books, Bookbeat will contextualise how much space you have left on your device. This helps frame how little space a book actually takes.
entries.analysis: In other words, by framing how small a book is to download, you're more likely to use this feature even if you're worried about space.

entries.title: Timing your notifications
entries.summary: During onboarding, Headway will ask you when you like to see new ideas, and then use that to set notification alerts.
entries.analysis: There are two great things here. Firstly, by giving you some autonomy over the alert—making you more likely to pay attention to them (User Initiated Triggers). Secondly, by framing the question as "when do you like to find new ideas", they're putting you in a mindset to think about a routine (rather than ad-hoc).

entries.title: Confirm a few bank details
entries.summary: While booking an Airbnb, the animated card reader will display a warning if you need to confirm your bank details.
entries.analysis: Plus, notice that they're clarifying that they'll hold your booking while you do this (lowering anxiety).

entries.title: Uber's simple mode
entries.summary: Uber offers a "simple mode", which has slightly larger buttons, clearer button labels and fewer options displayed at once.
entries.analysis: This is aimed at older users, but is generally great for accessibility.

entries.title: Creating confidence from errors
entries.summary: When Lovable errors, they'll make it clear that the logs have been sent somewhere internally.
entries.analysis: This gives you confidence that they're attending to errors—perhaps boosting brand trust.

entries.title: 7 days of rewards
entries.summary: After returning to Duolingo from a period of inactivity, you'll be given daily rewards for 7 days.
entries.analysis: This is an incentive to log 7 consecutive days of activity—hoping that after this time you'll be back in the habit.

entries.title: Double copy!
entries.summary: If you copy your name multiple times on Discord, you'll see tooltips showing "Double / Triple Copy" messages.

entries.title: City-specific image
entries.summary: When changing location on Citymapper, they'll show a related loading image.

entries.title: Showing social "proof"
entries.summary: To make their social proof more specific and effective, Loops will show how long they've been customers for when hovering over their logos

entries.title: Keeping a free subscriber
entries.summary: After cancelling a Patreon subscription, you'll be asked if you want to stay subscribed to the free updates.
entries.analysis: This makes it clear that free updates actually exist, and prepares you for receiving them.

entries.title: Basecamp's pie charts
entries.summary: Basecamp show a pie chart in their to-do list sections, showing the percentage of completed items.

entries.title: Try the pro feature
entries.summary: AllTrails will let you interact with their custom routes feature for free, and after setting 3 waypoints, then prompt you to subscribe.
entries.analysis: This hands-on approach lets you experience what the feature is like, and how easy it is to use. i.e., rather than just telling you how powerful it is, you can see the map route update in realtime.

entries.title: Here's how you can use it
entries.summary: The first time you try Perplexity's voice assistant, they'll show a carousel of things that you can use it for.
entries.analysis: This gives you a few concrete examples of things to try, and makes it more likely that you have a good first experience.

entries.title: Smart add-ons
entries.summary: After creating images, Grok will suggest contextually relevant follow-on prompts that modify the original.
entries.analysis: This help demonstrate the type of images that you can create, and how specific you can be with the prompts.

entries.title: Banned words
entries.summary: When you "ban" a specific word on TikTok, it'll let you automatically block similar words that appear often.

entries.title: Trending placeholders
entries.summary: Threads will use dynamic placeholders for their search, showing what is currently trending.

entries.title: A reward for refreshing
entries.summary: If you pull the IKEA app up to refresh, you'll see a random related fact.

entries.title: Creating artifacts
entries.summary: While working on your request, Claude will dynamically fill the preview artifact with real generated text.
entries.analysis: Yes, there's an element of user delight. But it also builds confidence that Claude is actually creating something worthwhile. It's The Labour Illusion. It looks like a lot of effort is going on behind the scenes.

entries.title: There's a tunnel on your trip
entries.summary: If your planned route includes a tunnel, Waze will prompt you to turn on Bluetooth before you set off.

entries.title: Undo payments
entries.summary: Monzo buffer sending a payment for a few seconds, to give you a chance of “undoing” it.
entries.analysis: This is an important nuance: they're very likely not actually "undoing" a payment. They're just adding some time between when you click "send", and actually sending it. It's more like a "cancel" payment—but it gives you a short window to notice and correct mistakes.

entries.title: One Popeyes on every corner
entries.summary: Popeyes adds friction into their checkout screen if multiple stores exist within really close proximity.

entries.title: Editing the preview
entries.summary: When sharing a link, Plane Finder lets you modify the preview image to show either a map, or the raw flight info.

entries.title: Auto PIP mode
entries.summary: When scrolling a video post on Patreon, if the video is playing (but goes out of view), it’ll automatically move into “Picture in picture” mode.
entries.analysis: Importantly, if the video is paused, it does not do this.

entries.title: The free trial toggle
