entries.summary: Flo have a “free trial” toggle on their pricing page, which is a clever way of getting the user to take an action and make a decision.
entries.analysis: In other words, by getting someone to select the toggle themselves (as apposed to auto-applying it), the user may feel more like the "earned" the trial, and therefore deserve it.

entries.title: Permission to do list
entries.summary: Cue combines their permissions requests into a single screen, like a to do list, with context for each one.

entries.title: Clarifying search types
entries.summary: Before making a search, Plane Finder both rotate example searches, and contextually give you tips.
entries.analysis: This is important because of how specific the search options can be. For example, searching for a specific flight number might not be something you'd try, unless you were told that you could.

entries.title: It's raining chicken
entries.summary: After adding items into your Popeyes basket, fried chicken will animate and fall into the menu tab.
entries.analysis: This also subtly draws attention into the basket icon (i.e., a visual cue of where to look next).

entries.title: Dynamic home icons
entries.summary: Patreon will dynamically combine your membership images into an “all creators” tab.

entries.title: Tomorrow is today?
entries.summary: If you’re adding a to do item at 1am, TickTick will contextualise “tomorrow” and “today” with this warning.

entries.title: Under 30 seconds?
entries.summary: Cue will show a more prominent discard action if the audio clip that you recorded was less than 30 seconds.
entries.analysis: i.e., because it was so short, they're pre-empting that you're likely looking to delete it and start again.

entries.title: Turning likes into notifications
entries.summary: If you like an item on Pinterest, without allowing notifications, they’ll use it to frame the value of turning them on.

entries.title: Easy to delete
entries.summary: When creating a new sheet, Google will automatically highlight the default title, so you can delete it in one action.
entries.analysis: i.e., they automatically name it for you (which is good), but they make it very easy to delete (which is even better).

entries.title: A sign of commitment
entries.summary: To try and leverage your initial commitment, Flo asks you to tap and hold this button during onboarding.
entries.analysis: This leverages both Cognitive Dissonance and The Foot in the Door effect. You've now declared your commitment—this typically makes you slightly more likely to stick to it.

entries.title: Look, this is what you need
entries.summary: When you have no active filters, Plane Finder show a literal arrow pointing at where you need to click.

entries.title: Mind has gone blank
entries.summary: When asking Queue's AI for movies to watch, you can tap this dice to cycle through creative prompts.
entries.analysis: This is a clever way to demonstrate the types of prompt that you can use. It’s not necessarily because you’d actually want to use these prompts.

entries.title: Automatically pause the audio
entries.summary: If you open the settings while listening to a Headspace meditation, they’ll automatically pause the audio.
entries.analysis: i.e., they've assumed that you're not focusing on the meditation (because you're in the settings).

entries.title: 404 minigame
entries.summary: When you hit a 404 on Honey, you'll find a hidden mini game where you have to jump to collect cash.

entries.title: Realtime notification previews
entries.summary: When asking you to turn on notifications, Plane Finder will show an example flight that counts down dynamically.
entries.analysis: i.e., as you sit on this screen, you can see the seconds counting down.

entries.title: Intercepting a share
entries.summary: If you take a screenshot on Flo, they'll open a pop-up that teaches you about their more secure "partner mode".
entries.analysis: This intercepts the moment that a user would do a habitual move (to screenshot), and then teaches them a better way.

entries.title: Auto-scrolling when changing styles
entries.summary: When you change an item style on Gymshark, it'll automatically scroll you up so that you can actually see the new images.

entries.title: Copy and delete
entries.summary: When saving a screenshot on iOS, you can copy an image to your clipboard without having to save it to your library.

entries.title: Avoiding double payments
entries.summary: JustPark will warn you if you're about to double book parking, and give you context about how to pay for larger vehicles (i.e., that need two spaces).
entries.analysis: Also, notice that the CTA label is "Pay for another space", and not something generic like "Continue".

entries.title: Interactive bidding demo
entries.summary: During onboarding, Whatnot will let you try the "swipe to bid" button, with clear context that it won't charge you.
entries.analysis: This happens during a real live stream (i.e., it mimics how you would really place a bid).

entries.title: Two purchase options
entries.summary: If you've got items in your basket, and click "buy now" on an item (skipping the basket), it'll ask you if you want to take those items to the checkout with you.

entries.title: No WiFi? No problem
entries.summary: If you open the Gails app without WiFi, they'll show your loyalty ID so you can at least collect your stamps.

entries.title: Common sense warning
entries.summary: If it's warm outside and you turn on "defrost mode", Tesla will ask you if you're sure.

entries.title: Video receipts
entries.summary: The live shopping app WhatNot will link "video receipts" to every purchase (i.e., the recording of when you bought that item).
entries.analysis: This is important because the bidding happens quickly and all on-screen, so there aren't listings like you'd expect on eBay.

entries.title: Delete and redraft
entries.summary: When deleting a post on TruthSocial, you'll be able to delete and "redraft" (rather than having to create it from scratch).

entries.title: Dynamic placeholders
entries.summary: After selecting a rating on Trustpilot, the placeholder text will dynamically change to reflect that tone.
entries.analysis: This helps collect better data, because it suggests topics that are relevant to your rating.

entries.title: Tipping a robot?
entries.summary: If you try and tip Tesla's Robotaxi, they'll show you an easter egg. This isn't just for fun, it actually frames the value of an autonomous ride.

entries.title: Creating your feed
entries.summary: During onboarding, Whatnot asks you what you're interested in. They'll then pin relevant tabs directly on your home feed.
entries.analysis: This makes it more likely that you'll quickly find items that you're interested in. i.e., may reduce the "first 60-second" churn.

entries.title: A smiley heart
entries.summary: When liking an item on Pinterest, the icon animates into a smiley heart.

entries.title: Chess levels
entries.summary: When starting a new chess course, Duolingo uses chess pieces as icons to indicate difficulty.

entries.title: You didn't pay
entries.summary: If you start paying for parking, but close the app before it's complete, you'll be almost immediately notified.
entries.analysis: They don't want the user quitting the app too early, thinking that they've paid, and then blaming PayByPhone for "not working" when they get a ticket.

entries.title: Dog mode
entries.summary: If you turn on "Dog Mode" in the app, Tesla reveals a new option to view the interior camera.
entries.analysis: It then overlays the temperature with a paw icon to indicate that Dog Mode is turned on.

entries.title: Welcome back to Yazio
entries.summary: When returning to Yazio after inactivity, they'll check if your goals are still the same, and allow you to start fresh if they're not.
entries.analysis: This makes it easier to return to the app, because you can either start again (Fresh Start Effect), or pick up where you left off.

entries.title: Find your address
entries.summary: When inputting your address, Whatnot will suggest you share your location settings to help find it.
entries.analysis: The timing of this is what makes it work (i.e., framing). You're being asked to share notification permissions at exactly the time when it's beneficial to you.

entries.title: A themed progress bar
entries.summary: The cannabis app "Stash" uses an animated joint as the progress bar.

entries.title: Exploding kings
entries.summary: When taking a king on Duolingo Chess, it'll explode into pieces.

entries.title: Tipping your rider
entries.summary: When adding a tip on Deliveroo, you'll see a burst of confetti fall in that area of the screen.
entries.analysis: This small moment of delight might make you feel positive about your decision, and therefore less likely to remove the tip.

entries.title: Your good deed
entries.summary: After reporting congestion, Google will frame your action as helping others.
entries.analysis: This may make it feel slightly more worth your time, because it's reminding you (framing) the impact it can have on others. i.e., rather than: "Congestion Marked Successfully".

entries.title: Apple's haptic trailers
entries.summary: Apple's haptic trailers will vibrate the phone in-sync to key moments in a trailer (like the roar of an F1 engine).

entries.title: Save chat as an image
entries.summary: Dia lets you copy your recent AI chat as an image—making it easier to share.
entries.analysis: Importantly, the difference between an image and sharing a URL, is that with the image you know exactly what you're sharing. But with a "share URL", you might accidentally share really old chat logs. This anxiety might reduce the likelihood that you share interesting conversations.

entries.title: Phobia inclusivity
entries.summary: Art of Fauna allows you to turn off pictures of animals that you have a phobia of, as it includes animal-based puzzles.

entries.title: Drag and drop onboarding
entries.summary: Play is a drag-and-drop design app. The components they show as examples during onboarding are interactive, and you can use them to modify the onboarding itself.
entries.analysis: e.g. you can use their example opacity slider to see things appear/disappear, or use a colour picker and change the colour of the onboarding.

entries.title: Slide to commit
entries.summary: During onboarding, Evolve makes you slide to confirm your commitment to follow your new routine.
entries.analysis: This taps into Cognitive Dissonance—people don't like to go against their previous commitments. (And, the physical act of doing this is more memorable than tapping a button).

entries.title: Colour customisation
entries.summary: When getting set-up with Dia Browser, the customisable colours update the mock-ups dynamically.
entries.analysis: This actually gives people context as to what will change colour (e.g., buttons and chats).

entries.title: Flappy bird is back
entries.summary: Github has a hidden game of flappy bird behind the version number in their app.

entries.title: Literal easter eggs
entries.summary: Staying true to its nature-based theme, Art of Fauna hides literal eggs to serve as easter eggs across the app.
entries.analysis: These also act as a small moment of celebration (and a motivation to look around the app), by unlocking new puzzles.

entries.title: Try Googling "Wordle"
entries.summary: If you search for "Wordle", the Google logo will animate in the style of the Wordle game.

entries.title: Where did it go?
entries.summary: If you hover to the empty space where Stripe's "test mode" used to be (i.e., out of habit), they'll remind you that it's been moved.

entries.title: Instagram's practice mode
entries.summary: When using "Quick Snap" for the first time, Instagram will give you a practice round to see how it works.

entries.title: Built-in privacy
entries.summary: Gmail highlights orders arriving that day, but will hide details about certain item categories.

entries.title: Negative prompts
entries.summary: When generating AI images, Icons8 lets you include negative prompts (i.e., things to avoid).
entries.analysis: Sometimes it's easier to say what you don't want, than what you do.

entries.title: Connecting AirPods
entries.summary: When you connect AirPods with a personalised case, you'll dynamically see them on screen.

entries.title: The value of your wait
entries.summary: While restarting your router, EE will explain what it's doing. This makes the wait feel slightly easier.
entries.analysis: This is a subtle way of using the Labour Illusion—by just letting you know what's happening.

entries.title: Using growth to motivate action
entries.summary: Instagram will leverage your recent growth (followers) to encourage an action (posting).
entries.analysis: This is a masterclass in timing and contextualising benefits. By framing it this way, they're triggering Loss Aversion—you don't want to bore your new followers.

entries.title: Notification routing
entries.summary: When setting up the Substack app, they'll allow you to route notifications to your email, the app, or both.
entries.analysis: By giving the user a choice, they're allowing autonomy—this will make it more likely that the user actually acts upon the notification (i.e., User-Initiated Triggers).

entries.title: Uber's waiting game
entries.summary: Uber lets you play a game while you wait for your ride to be confirmed.
entries.analysis: This isn't just an easter egg—it might actually help to reduce cancellations. It makes waiting that slightly bit easier. You don't mind waiting so much if you're doing something.

entries.title: Asking for notifications (well)
entries.summary: While asking for notification permissions, Tomorrow will ask you which notifications you care about, and show you dynamic examples.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic way to frame the value of notifications, and give people choice. i.e., because they're creating the triggers themselves, they'll be more receptive to the alerts when they come through.

entries.title: Contextualising your wait
entries.summary: While waiting for AI to generate an image, Icons8 shows a timer counting up. This then frames their premium tier (by reminding you how long you've been waiting).
entries.analysis: At first, it seems strange to count up. This is because they don't really know how long the generation will take. But, counting up demonstrates that it's not stuck. It also validates that they are still waiting, and by giving context to how long you've been waiting, it can frame an upsell better.

entries.title: Time-limited onboarding
entries.summary: Instead of doing their onboarding matching exercise indefinitely, Queue limit you to just 30 seconds. This might actually increase the average number of swipes per user.
entries.analysis: To understand why this works, you need to consider that the task of doing something indefinitely is daunting. You (the user) have to decide when you've completed it, so you either finish too early, or do it until you're bored. But then you're starting the experience already tired. This is a smart way of reframing the process as "what can you get done in 30 seconds?".

entries.title: Scrolling past options
entries.summary: If you scroll past a required selection, JustEat will remind you at the bottom of the page. There's then a single tap to-jump-up to what you've missed.
entries.analysis: To be clear, you don't need to click a button to hit this validation. It's just there if you reach the end of the screen.

entries.title: First-time arrow
entries.summary: The first time you use Icon8's AI generator, they'll literally point at where you need to type.
entries.analysis: After you've used it once, it changes to be a more generic empty state. It's great that they're separating these use cases slightly.

entries.title: Psst, here's some advice
entries.summary: Trainline will let you know if there’s a train that departs later and arrives earlier than the one you’re considering.

entries.title: Dynamic temperature
entries.summary: While saving locations, Tomorrow will dynamically show the current temperature in their hero image.
entries.analysis: Also, the image itself shows "home", "away" and "work".

entries.title: Clever notification context
entries.summary: When asking for notification permissions, Threads will place context on the screen underneath.
entries.analysis: This frames the value of notifications in a way that relates to your goal (e.g., when people follow you).

entries.title: Easier importing, easier switching
entries.summary: While creating an account, Queue will let you paste in a list of titles you want to watch—making the import and switching process easier.

entries.title: Superhuman's practice mode
entries.summary: During onboarding, Superhuman gives you a sandbox to practice in. This includes dynamic mentions of your name to make it feel more personal.

entries.title: Their first thread
entries.summary: To encourage engagement on someone's first post (it stands out in the feed), Threads will display a badge.
entries.analysis: In other words, this might act as a "I'm new around here, say hi to me" cue. Especially if it's a brand you actually know from elsewhere.

entries.title: Overlaying difficulty
entries.summary: Nike Running Club will overlay the difficulty on your post-run map.
entries.analysis: This might make it feel more impressive to share.

entries.title: Where are you parked?
entries.summary: PayByPhone will let you scan an area or parking sign, and input your location code.

entries.title: Delightful invoices
entries.summary: Canva will include random (and weird) facts in their monthly receipt emails.
entries.analysis: Usually these types of email are administrative and not worth opening. This small trick may be enough to increase their open rate, and get people to read them.

entries.title: Understanding user intent
entries.summary: Figma notices if you've named an empty page "-----" (for organisation reasons), and creates a separator line instead.
entries.analysis: Importantly, if there's anything on that page, it won't do this. But it will if it recognises that you're trying to separate your pages, not rename one.

entries.title: A moment of delight
entries.summary: Airbnb show open and closed door icons in your itinerary.

entries.title: Magic is happening behind the scenes
entries.summary: As you set up Superhuman, you'll see how many emails they've processed in real time.
entries.analysis: This is really clever as it gives you a really good reason not to give up on the process (Loss Aversion). You want to get to the end of the onboarding to see what's happened. It's also smart that it doesn't explicitly tell you what it's doing—that creates a curiosity gap.

entries.title: Timed unlocks
entries.summary: To encourage early retention, StudentBeans unlocks deals on a timer.
entries.analysis: This is a form of intentional friction. Simply put: it stops you binging every deal, and gives you a reason to come back the next day (and then the next).

entries.title: You're not talking to us
entries.summary: Monzo notices if you're using the app while on a call, and dynamically tells you if you're on the phone to them or not.
entries.analysis: i.e., to help avoid bank scams.

entries.title: Subtle design cues
entries.summary: Strava's activity calendar will show different sized dots depending on the length of the activity.
entries.analysis: This is actually acts as a slight moment of celebration. In simple terms: it feels great to see a big dot.

entries.title: Surfacing active bookings
entries.summary: Airbnb will show active bookings on their homepage, with a dynamic animated calendar countdown.

entries.title: Dynamic CTA labels
entries.summary: Amazon Prime dynamically change their CTA labels if you've watched a title before.

entries.title: Tackling the price objection
entries.summary: If you're cancelling your Strava subscription because of price, they'll dynamically remind you that you can save money by switching to the annual plan.

entries.title: Switching the action
entries.summary: If you go to refer someone on Superhuman who shares your email domain, you'll be asked if you want to add them to your team instead.
entries.analysis: This encourages sticky long term behaviour (creating a team), rather than incorrect short term actions (having lots of separate accounts).

entries.title: Contextualising quality
entries.summary: Amazon Prime will contextualise the bandwidth required for each quality setting.
entries.analysis: i.e., incredibly useful if you're watching on a data plan, and not wifi.

entries.title: Framing the value of Premium
entries.summary: YouTube will remind you when you're using Premium features—surfacing the silent benefits.
entries.analysis: In other words, it makes you go "oh, this queue is useful", and you associate that feeling into your Premium subscription.

entries.title: Disconnecting "quietly"
entries.summary: When leaving a live event, Discord will specify that you'll leave quietly—reducing the anxiety that you'll distract the host.
entries.analysis: i.e., it won't show up to the host as "BFM has left the chat", while you're speaking.

entries.title: Claude's 404
entries.summary: Claude's 404 page uses one of their LLM models to create a poem.
entries.analysis: I love that you can also select the model—this allows people to functionally buy into the differences between them.

entries.title: Reframing your goals to reduce churn
entries.summary: If you go to cancel your subscription, Strava will use both Loss Aversion and Framing to remind you about your goals.
entries.analysis: i.e., "don't leave your goals behind, Strava will help you make progress more likely, and faster".

entries.title: Reducing "deletion churn"
entries.summary: Instead of simply having the option to delete a post, TikTok let you delete and re-edit one.
entries.analysis: This likely reduces "deletion churn", as people can more easily edit a post. (i.e., they might not be bothered to recreate it from scratch).

entries.title: Cycling through emojis
entries.summary: When you hover over the inactive emoji icon on Discord, it'll morph into a random emoji.
entries.analysis: This subtly tells users that they don't have to click that one emoji reaction—they can cycle through more.

entries.title: The button effect
entries.summary: On iPhones, when you press a physical button, you'll see a slight darkening effect on the screen around it.

entries.title: Changing the main nav
entries.summary: Linear will dynamically change their main navigation if you've got a cookie saved in your browser.
entries.analysis: i.e., if they think you've previously logged in.

entries.title: The carrot is alive
entries.summary: As you opt to pay more on Yuka, their carrot mascot comes out of hiding.

entries.title: Here's what you'll lose
entries.summary: After cancelling your subscription, Strava will dynamically remind you that features you're using will be removed.
entries.analysis: By showing a countdown to when you'll lose something, they're leaning into Loss Aversion.

entries.title: Sniper link(ed)in
entries.summary: During sign-up, Peerlist includes a link that opens your LinkedIn profile (so you can get your URL).

entries.title: Specific testimonials
entries.summary: Before you hit the paywall, Cherrypick will match your diet with specific testimonials.
entries.analysis: This is likely to mean that people arrive at the paywall with a greater sense of value. i.e., Cherrypick is specifically for "people like them".

entries.title: A condensed CTA
entries.summary: As you scroll the feed, this AllTrails CTA condenses to take up less room.

entries.title: Motivating social proof
entries.summary: Before rating new places, Google will dynamically remind you how influential your previous reviews have been.
entries.analysis: This nudge of motivation probably increases both the number of reviews, and the quality—because people know that their previous posts were actually seen.

entries.title: Finding alternatives automatically
entries.summary: If you make a search on Uber that has no results, it'll tell you, and automatically find nearby alternatives.
entries.analysis: Importantly, it keeps the context of what's happening, and displays a notice that these are nearby train stations (not the one you searched for).

entries.title: Searches = Loading
entries.summary: If you search by a known "category", Google Maps will display a more specific loading state.

entries.title: Turn on notifications
entries.summary: To encourage you to turn on notifications, Perplexity will prompt you after starting a "deep research" request.
entries.analysis: This gives a clear reason to turn notifications on, and demonstrates that the user doesn't need to sit and watch the screen the whole time.

entries.title: A better notification prompt
entries.summary: After starting your trial, AllTrails gives you a clear reason to turn notifications on.
entries.analysis: i.e., it's a combination of the timing (after starting your trial), and the context behind the moment.

entries.title: Intercepting a share
entries.summary: If you take a screenshot on Endel, they'll prompt you to share the app directly.

entries.title: Personalising the search bar
entries.summary: After completing your pet's profile, Petbarn will personalise their search bar.

entries.title: Reward-based streaks
entries.summary: Peerlist encourage streaks by rewarding the user with power features after they've hit milestones.
entries.analysis: What I like about this, is that that the rewards are mostly tied to power users. e.g., unlocking "more influence" or "better profiles". It leverages the Endowment Effect, as people they value their profile more.

entries.title: Custom confetti
entries.summary: After subscribing to X, the screen is showered in logos.

entries.title: Turning waiting into learning
entries.summary: If a query (e.g., a chart) is taking a while to load, Posthog will teach you about ways to speed up the results.
entries.analysis: This is a great form of progressive onboarding—they're teaching you about the settings at exactly the right time.

entries.title: Contextualising Notion templates
entries.summary: When you look at a template, Notion will dynamically change the CTA label if you've already used it before.
entries.analysis: This has a few subtle uses. It could both be reassuring (e.g., "yeah, this is the one you used last time"). And, it could be a nudge that you might be accidentally adding this template a second time.

entries.title: Airbnb's sticky CTA
entries.summary: When you first land in the checkout, Airbnb show a small prompt to review the details. After reaching the bottom of the page, it transforms into a sticky confirm button.
entries.analysis: This keeps the page clean while you're reviewing the details (i.e., your focus is on the content itself), and then springs into being more action-driven.

entries.title: Monzo's shoe easter egg
entries.summary: While getting an insurance quote, Monzo visualise "people in your house" with a shoe rack. Eventually, the shoes just pile up on the floor.

entries.title: Upselling a team plan
entries.summary: WeTransfer will prompt you if multiple email addresses associated with your company are using the free plan.
entries.analysis: Plus, this includes both a link to share context with your admin, and a link to click if you are the admin.

entries.title: Instagram's dynamic CTAs
entries.summary: When sending a post to multiple contacts, the CTA label dynamically changes to clarify that it's sent separately, and not in a joint conversation.
entries.analysis: Without this, people would be nervous about accidentally creating groups of people who don't know each other.

entries.title: A work in progress
entries.summary: If a user's profile is incomplete, Contra automatically shows a badge to let the community know.
entries.analysis: This helps reduce some of the "cold water shock" of seeing your half-baked profile, and worrying what others will think.

entries.title: Loveable's last sign in
entries.summary: Lovable displays a badge next to your most recent sign in method.
entries.analysis: i.e., did you login with me@email.com , or using your me@email.com Google account?

entries.title: Contextual default options
entries.summary: While creating a new plan, Runna will display your current running times (based on previous runs), and use that to pre-fill relevant questions.
entries.analysis: This not only improves input quality, but reminds you about the lock-in value of using one app over time.

entries.title: Custom confetti explosion
entries.summary: After finishing your daily tasks, Grit will shower the screen in the emojis that are related to your routine.
entries.analysis: It's possible that the custom emojis make this celebratory moment feel more personalised (and therefore is more effective).

entries.title: Thoughtful notifications
entries.summary: The food delivery service Swiggy will let you turn off notifications during Ramadan.

entries.title: European GDPR meme
entries.summary: Posthog show a meme of the President of the European Commission next to their GDPR request.

entries.title: You have 5510 lego pieces
entries.summary: Your Lego profile includes the number of individual lego pieces you have.
entries.analysis: i.e., it makes your account feel more personalised. It's a combination of the Endowment Effect and User Delight.

entries.title: Don't tell, show
entries.summary: Instead of just telling you about their better image processing, OpenAI instantly load a prompt and then generate it.
entries.analysis: This solves another problem, which is prompt engineering. By using their own prompt, they can increase the likelihood that the image created is good.

entries.title: Oozing the brand
entries.summary: RevenueCat show an on-brand loading state in their CTAs (a rolling ball of yarn).

entries.title: Duplicate image download
entries.summary: WhatsApp will notify you if you attempt to download an image that's already in your camera roll.
entries.analysis: This is helpful because WhatsApp can automatically save images (based on your settings). You could improve this message by reminding people about that setting—so they know why their images are being saved.

entries.title: Behind the Dynamic Island
entries.summary: Duolingo hide their logo behind Apple's Dynamic Island, so it appears on screenshots.

entries.title: A nudge for your second order
entries.summary: Based on your previous basket, Cherrypick will track the days until you're likely down to your final meal, and then prompt you to re-order.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you create 4 dinners in the app, they'll remind you 3 days later (as you're down to your last meal).

entries.title: Try our sandbox mode
entries.summary: To deter people from rushing onboarding, RevenueCat offer a sandbox you can play around with in a new tab.
entries.analysis: This acts as an escape route (in a new tab) for users who just want to experience the product first. i.e., they'll then have more attention to go back and actually set up RevenueCat properly, including the integrations.

entries.title: Recognisable logos
entries.summary: To make the filters more recognisable, Lego utilises the logos in the buttons themselves.
entries.analysis: i.e., it makes it immediately more recognisable, and therefore usable.

entries.title: Want to eat healthy?
entries.summary: During onboarding, Cherrypick will dynamically show testimonials depending on what you select as important to you.

entries.title: There's a cat on my keyboard
entries.summary: Whilst getting a quote for pet insurance, Lemonade use this "cat on keyboard" gimmick in their chatbot.
entries.analysis: Ordinarily this would annoy me, but it's fast, done well, and at a point in the process where it actually was delightful.

entries.title: Delightful loading
entries.summary: When creating your weekly food plan, Cherrypick show calendar days flying by on the screen.

entries.title: Sleeps to go
entries.summary: After inputting your date of birth, Vitality displays how many sleeps you have until your birthday.
entries.analysis: I don't suspect that this improves conversion rates in any meaningful way. But it does set a tone, which may be part of broader framing.

entries.title: Framing the benefits of sharing
entries.summary: If you hover over the share button, your position in the Contra waiting list will dynamically change to give you a nudge into sharing.
entries.analysis: i.e., the animation of watching 1,000 places be knocked off your position demonstrates a clear value. It's framed the benefit. This is better than simply saying "you'll move up the waiting list", as it's more visual.

entries.title: Stripe's hidden Snake
entries.summary: Stripe's developer console has a playable Snake easter egg.

entries.title: Lemonade's welcome animation
entries.summary: Lemonade's welcome screens are a connected scene, where a person walks a dog between each page.

entries.title: Scrub to reveal
entries.summary: When opening the app for the first time, ABY gets you to scrub the screen to reveal the app. This grabs your attention and sets a tone.
entries.analysis: i.e., although it functionally does nothing, it sends a strong signal that this app is playful and interactive.

entries.title: Sniper link to your LinkedIn
entries.summary: To help you attach your LinkedIn while creating an account, Contra have a sniper link that directly opens your LinkedIn profile.
entries.analysis: i.e., it opens a URL that takes logged in users into their own profile. This then helps them find their unique URL, and attach their LinkedIn account to their Contra profile.

entries.title: Grouping similar files
entries.summary: When uploading images to Playbook, they'll automatically group files with very similar titles.
entries.analysis: By reminding people every time, it's a form of progressive onboarding. i.e., a helpful feature, that you only learn about when you actually use it.

entries.title: Arc + Gmail empty state
entries.summary: If you have no unread emails, Arc (via Gmail), will show a playful empty state.

entries.title: We've saved you 11 minutes
entries.summary: Swiggy automatically switches to the fastest delivery option and displays the time saved.
entries.analysis: i.e., by being specific with how many minutes were saved, it gives the user a frame of reference.

entries.title: Claude is hiring
entries.summary: Claude has a hiring easter egg in their website's source code.

entries.title: Temporary tooltips
entries.summary: When you go to leave a journal entry, ABY will show these tooltips for a few seconds, which then disappear to remove the distraction.

entries.title: OpenAI's 404 poem
entries.summary: OpenAI's 404 page will use one of their LLM models to generate a short poem.

entries.title: Linear's "undo" warnings
entries.summary: Linear has triggers to stop you accidentally undoing the wrong things. For example, if the action was completed more than 10 minutes ago, or on another page.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this prompt doesn't appear if you undo a recent action.

entries.title: Anti-churn triggers
entries.summary: When you go to enter your first password on Arc, it'll suggest transferring all your passwords from another browser.
entries.analysis: This is great because it addresses a likely major reason to churn: all your passwords need to be re-entered.

entries.title: Triggering On-The-Go mode
entries.summary: If you join a Google Meet call on your phone, with your camera off, it'll automatically suggest switching to on-the-go mode.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic form of progressive onboarding. It triggers at exactly the right time.

entries.title: Stop-motion code entry
entries.summary: When entering a party code, Lego Builder shows a stop-motion animation that progresses slightly with every digit entered.

entries.title: Setting notification triggers
entries.summary: Libby has a traffic light system for notifications, and lets the user customise how and when they're alerted.
entries.analysis: Usually, having optionality over alerts (and having them be self-initiated) increases the likelihood that the user acts upon them. i.e., they're less likely to be ignored.

entries.title: Hey, look over here
entries.summary: Pinterest will draw your attention to new filters by shaking them slightly when you first see them.

entries.title: Notification-free sessions
entries.summary: Udemy will automatically turn off notifications when you start a study session.

entries.title: Swipe to swap
entries.summary: If you've got multiple Gmail accounts, you can swipe your face to instantly swap between them.

entries.title: Specificity creates comfort
entries.summary: When booking a ticket, Trainline will use dynamic loading and success states that mention where you're going.
entries.analysis: This specificity makes you more comfortable about what's happening.

entries.title: Delightful voice chat
entries.summary: When using Perplexity's voice chat, you can use your finger to interact with the dots on screen, creating particle effects.

entries.title: Didn't pick up? Try this
entries.summary: If someone doesn't pick up your WhatsApp call, you'll see a prompt to message that person when you open the app.
entries.analysis: For example, you call Person X (they don't pick up), open WhatsApp and your last message was to Person Y—so it loads that chat. They'll then show a prompt to message Person X, because they've assumed that's what you'll want to do. i.e., this is a clever way to handle intention.

entries.title: Upselling a specific feature
entries.summary: During an upsell, Sentry identifies previous tiers you've trialed, and shows a badge next to the specific feature that you came from.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you were browsing custom dashboards, that feature will be highlighted when you actually go to upgrade.

entries.title: Getting back on track
entries.summary: Runna offers to automatically rearrange or skip missed workouts when returning after a period of inactivity.
entries.analysis: This will be great at reducing churn. People miss a week (because they were ill, or lazy), and coming back to a messed up schedule could be a deterrent. This helps people get back on track effortlessly.

entries.title: Triggering a new feature
entries.summary: If you use Open Banking to transfer money from another bank, Monzo will dynamically show a prompt above your cards to promote their "connected accounts" feature.
entries.analysis: This is timed really well, and a non-intrusive form of progressive onboarding.

entries.title: Carbs per hour
entries.summary: Precision Hydration has a calculator in the checkout to estimate (and contextualise) how much product to purchase for your routine.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this helps you be confident in the purchase. i.e., even if you don't purchase more, you can be more confident that you have bought the right amount.

entries.title: Dynamic pen strokes
entries.summary: Figma automatically invert the pen colour from black to white, if you start drawing over a very light (or dark) background.

entries.title: Getting back to your run
entries.summary: During a run, Nike will keep a CTA at the bottom of every screen making it easy to get back to the default running view.
entries.analysis: There are a few subtle touches worth noticing: 1. It's contextualising the direction in which you swiped with an arrow. This helps you learn where each view is, in relation to the default running screen. It's harder to become disoriented. 2. It maintains the timer, which is useful if you're on one of those pages for a reason.

entries.title: Car rental tips
entries.summary: Kayak will warn you if you're overpaying for a few hours of car rental.
entries.analysis: This helps build trust in the purchase.

entries.title: Watch your chip grow
entries.summary: As you modify your VPS package with the slider, you'll see an animated representation upgrade (or downgrade) in the hero.

entries.title: Suggested, but quick to remove
entries.summary: Evernote will automatically suggest note titles based on the content, but also immediately highlight it so that it's easy to delete.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you click on a blank title (to create one), it'll suggest one for you—but highlight it so that you can delete it all in a single tap.

entries.title: Contextualising the effort
entries.summary: To help you provide an "effort rating" to a run, Nike contextualises each choice by comparing it to something more obvious, like being able to talk.
entries.analysis: Without this, it'd be far less clear if your run was a 3/10 hard, or a 6/10. This gives a way of framing each option.

entries.title: If you just keep typing...
entries.summary: As your search query gets longer, Perplexity will automatically reduce the font size, and eventually hide the input suggestions.
entries.analysis: Without this, short questions might feel lost on the page. Or, longer questions might elapse out of view.

entries.title: Dynamic search suggestions
entries.summary: Instagram will show a selection of different placeholders, showing a variety of ideas.

entries.title: International travel tips
entries.summary: When making a payment in a foreign country, Monzo welcomes you with key information on exchange rates, fees, and travel tips.
entries.analysis: This is a form of progressive disclosure. i.e., it introduces the key features (allowances) exactly when you need them.

entries.title: Downloading company assets
entries.summary: If you right click on Clay's logo, they'll make it easy to download their brand assets.
entries.analysis: This is getting more common, and it just makes it slightly easier to find high quality assets for articles or press.

entries.title: Timing the upsell
entries.summary: During your first colouring session, Lake will trigger a clever upsell flow from a likely "Aha! Moment".
entries.analysis: This is really well executed for a few reasons: 1. It waits until you've got into the colouring, and then latches onto a feeling "you could do this for a while". 2. It leads with the health benefits of colouring (which then goes into an upsell).

entries.title: Monzo will send the notification
entries.summary: When assigning a transaction to a shared tab, Monzo gives context about who will be notified automatically.
entries.analysis: i.e., this prompt reminds you that you don't have to message someone privately every time you assign a transaction.

entries.title: Bundling a guest pass
entries.summary: After completing a Calm meditation, you can share it bundled together with a 30-day guest pass.
entries.analysis: This is likely to become a really high conviction share. i.e., it's a specific exercise, that you've completed, along with 30-days to try it for free. But importantly, you (the user) can be more confident that the other person won't end up paying, regretting it, and then holding it against you. It de-risks the share.

entries.title: Leveraging your previous orders
entries.summary: If you've not given Shop notification permissions, they'll use one of your recent orders as an animated example to demonstrate how notifications are used throughout the process.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is a brilliant use of historical data, and by making it personalised to you, you're more likely to pay attention. It benefits from the Endowment Effect: you feel like that's your order.

entries.title: Lego's dynamic CTAs
entries.summary: The Lego Builder app uses dynamic CTAs with built-in progress bars, to indicate the status of a build.

entries.title: Shopping in real time
entries.summary: After ordering groceries on Uber Eats, you'll be able to watch as a shopper finds each of your items in real time.
entries.analysis: This data makes waiting far more bearable. You know the order is progressing, and you can visualise someone walking around the store.

entries.title: Reactive (and interactive) onboarding
entries.summary: To teach you about collages, Pinterest walk you through an interactive process, including reactive onboarding if you make a mistake.
entries.analysis: i.e., they highlight your mistake in real time, and explain what you should have done differently.

entries.title: Open the bag
entries.summary: The Lego Builder app will tell you when you need to open a new bag. Tapping on the screen will explode the bag into pieces.
entries.analysis: i.e., these relate to the literal bags you have inside lego kits.

entries.title: Highlighting the related items
entries.summary: After making a search, Swiggy will automatically tabulate parent and child categories (and items).
entries.analysis: This is great, as it keeps the search visually clean as results appear, but if you pause for a few seconds, it visually organises them for you.

entries.title: Onboarding incorrect shortcuts
entries.summary: Usually, CMD+K is a shortcut to add a link, but Slack uses it for opening the search. To teach you this difference, they'll show a tooltip if you press CMD+K whilst highlighting text.
entries.analysis: This is a form of reactive onboarding (i.e., reacting to an input).

entries.title: Hiding the item outline
entries.summary: Whilst changing the colour of an item, Figma will temporarily hide the blue "outline border".

entries.title: Spoiler-free catch-up
entries.summary: When watching catch-up sports on Dazn, you can hide spoilers (i.e., timestamps of key events, like goals).

entries.title: Immediate meal summaries
entries.summary: Every time you log a meal, Yazio will remind you about their true value—showing you a contextualised breakdown of the individual food items.
entries.analysis: This actually helps remind users about the true value of the app. You enter 5 food items, and Yazio immediately provides a breakdown of what was good, bad, high, low or processed. It's an instant reminder why you bother tracking your food in the first place—i.e., you can't easily just do that by memory, or by hand. They also show different animations depending on which meal it was.

entries.title: Dynamic leaderboard motivation
entries.summary: When looking at the weekly leaderboard, Duolingo will frame your position, encourage you to improve it and offer a CTA with some built-in commitment.
entries.analysis: i.e., "I can do it" may be slightly better at creating motivation than "close" or "back home". Also notice that the colours change depending on your gold, silver or bronze status.

entries.title: An on-brand slider
entries.summary: When creating an account, the age input slider is a piece of Lego.

entries.title: Animating your savings
entries.summary: Up Bank visualise your savings with dots. When you deposit more money, you'll see more dots pour in and create animated waves.
entries.analysis: It's a small moment of User Delight that can make it more satisfying to save money.

entries.title: 10% to avoid disappointment
entries.summary: To save disappointment (and stop you from Googling better codes), Bloom & Wild will give you 10% off if you enter an incorrect discount code.
entries.analysis: They don't want potential customers searching for discount codes, or they might find one for their competitors. So instead, they're prepared to give you a one-click 10% off, to save you from the disappointment of trying expired ones you find online.

entries.title: Did you miss what they said?
entries.summary: If you rewind a show by 10 seconds, Apple TV will temporarily show the subtitles (i.e., in case you missed some dialogue).

entries.title: Onboarding Spotify comments
entries.summary: When you first attempt to comment on Spotify, you'll see interactive onboarding about how it works. It forces you to pay attention.
entries.analysis: What's also great UX here, is that the first tick box is active by default. You need to interact with the other two. But by having the first one active, it clarifies what you're meant to do (i.e., to tick them).

entries.title: Framing your late-night order
entries.summary: Uber Eats will frame the tip screen if it's late at night—which might make you more likely to tip.
entries.analysis: This reminds the buyer about the effort that the delivery driver has gone into. i.e., "it's late at night, everyone else is asleep, this driver is working hard to bring you your food". To a certain extent, this is a third party Labour Illusion.

entries.title: Shake to extend the timer
entries.summary: After an Audible timer ends, you can extend it by shaking your phone.
entries.analysis: This gives a great way to extend an audiobook sleep timer without looking at your phone.

entries.title: Building confidence before purchase
entries.summary: Amazon will show badges if specific items have fewer returns than average, in their category.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic form of social proof as it's framed and specific. i.e., "people bought this" is weaker than "people bought and didn't return this".

entries.title: Figma's interactive hero
entries.summary: The Figma community hero section will dynamically show items (like plugins or icon sets), when you hover over them.

entries.title: Leveraging social pressure
entries.summary: Duolingo will tell you if a friend has had a perfect month. This might motivate you more, as you know that your friends will get notifications about your achievements.

entries.title: Animated emojis
entries.summary: As you select how you feel, the face will animate to represent that emotion.

entries.title: Boosting credibility with sources
entries.summary: To try and boost the credibility of their answer, Perplexity will display which sources it's crawling.
entries.analysis: This has a huge psychological effect on how much you trust the answer.

entries.title: Location permission reminders
entries.summary: If you start a search without location tracking being turned on, Hopper will show you a reminder (in context).
entries.analysis: What's great about this is that it's the context of what you're trying to do.

entries.title: Wait, is this a duplicate?
entries.summary: To help avoid duplicate payments, Monzo will remind you how long ago you made an identical transfer.

entries.title: Framing your usage
entries.summary: When upselling you to a paid account, Amie will frame your prior usage, reminding you how much value you've had.
entries.analysis: As there's no free tier any more, this attempts to leverage loss aversion. (i.e., look how much history and data you'll lose).

entries.title: Interactive Duck Hunt
entries.summary: Rogers Sporting Goods (a hunting store) has an interactive "Duck Hunt" game to unlock a discount.

entries.title: One final attempt to keep you
entries.summary: Monica will ask why you're removing the Chrome extension, and then dynamically offer fixes like resetting your free credits.

entries.title: Dynamic sign-up offers
entries.summary: Breethe will ask you why you've rejected the free trial, and then dynamically improve your offer.
entries.analysis: e.g., if you say that 7 days isn't enough, they'll up it to 30.

entries.title: Celebrating your new features
entries.summary: After upgrading your Canva account, the features you've unlocked will animate and explode the first time you use them.

entries.title: An obvious cue
entries.summary: The Athletic will show a thumbnail image in black and white after it's been read. This (may) help you find new content as you scroll.
entries.analysis: To be clear, I'd treat this as an experiment to run, not a certain benefit. With some audience groups, it may look messy, or detract from ever re-reading that material.

entries.title: Context + feature onboarding
entries.summary: Instagram will educate you about their new "scheduled messages" feature, if you go to privately message someone late at night.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic example of progressive onboarding. They're educating the user at exactly the right time, in context of the thing that they want to do.

entries.title: Animated trains and helpful tips
entries.summary: While a trip is underway, the train will animate slightly, while showing a carousel of information about your journey.

entries.title: The consequences of snoozing
entries.summary: When snoozing a task on Planta, there's clear context about what that action will do to your schedule.

entries.title: Framing your usage (deliveries)
entries.summary: When cancelling Prime, Amazon will show you how many parcels you've shipped over the last 12 months.
entries.analysis: This would be better if it contextualised the return-on-investment of those deliveries, both in money and time saved. i.e., you saved £126, and 76 days of waiting.

entries.title: Upselling non-paying users
entries.summary: Slack has an upsell funnel where they'll promote a feature to a basic channel member, and allow them to request an upgrade from a user with billing permissions.
entries.analysis: One way to improve this would be to show who in your channel is going to get the email, and what the email will say. People may be reserved about triggering an email to their boss. It's a very specific version of Social Proof (e.g., " David from accounts really wants this feature ").

entries.title: A version of categories
entries.summary: Etsy makes shopping for gifts feel less daunting by showing tabs for upcoming occasions.
entries.analysis: This works for Etsy, but might not work for Nike (as an example). The important nuance here is that Etsy have understood what their audience does (buy gifts), and have designed dynamic categories to meet those needs.

entries.title: Using specific benefits to convert more
entries.summary: During onboarding, Breethe will ask for your goals and then dynamically change the free trial benefit screen to address them.

entries.title: Dynamically displaying the tense
entries.summary: The "day rating" block is dynamic depending on which day you're doing an entry for.

entries.title: Weekly email leaderboards
entries.summary: Instead of a live leaderboard, DataCamp sends a weekly summary email showing you how you compare to other users.
entries.analysis: It'd be an interesting experiment to run, but this might be more motivating, especially as it somewhat "resets" every week. You don't need to constantly grind in the same way.

entries.title: Dynamic layover suggestions
entries.summary: When looking at flights, Hopper will dynamically suggest that you can exclude long layovers, and show the cost for doing so.

entries.title: Contextualising benefits
entries.summary: Amazon Prime will frame the value of their subscription, by directly telling you how much you've "saved" in the last 12 months.
entries.analysis: This would be slightly better if they contextualised it as an ROI on the cost of Prime.

entries.title: A nudge to add a payment method
entries.summary: Upwork encourage businesses to add a payment method (for seamless billing), with some data-driven social proof.

entries.title: Watching the sky change
entries.summary: When scrolling through the clockwise timeline, the sky changes dynamically.

entries.title: Pausing a challenge
entries.summary: Monzo lets you pause a savings challenge, and makes it clear that you can rejoin later and catch up with anything you've missed.

entries.title: Social "proofing" an upsell
entries.summary: When upselling cancellation insurance, Hopper shows specific social proof about how many other travellers took that option, and frames the expected refund value.
entries.analysis: i.e., they're framing "you could get back £182" right next to the cost of "£24". By showing it in this way (with social proof), they're making it feel like a popular option with a good ROI.

entries.title: A seasonal QR code
entries.summary: When sharing your Slopes trip, the QR code background has animated snowflake and earth emojis.

entries.title: Notion's avatar builders
entries.summary: To help users personalise their profiles, Notion has built an on-brand avatar builder.
entries.analysis: This is perhaps an experiment to try and get more users to change their default icons (which might make collaboration between teams look boring). Seeing your team with custom avatars might nudge others to do it too—embedding the Team a little bit more into Notion.

entries.title: The 1p challenge
entries.summary: During Monzo's "1p challenge", a representation of your total savings will collect on the screen.
entries.analysis: This helps to amplify the goal gradient effect (i.e., you can visually see your progress).

entries.title: Cheaper options are hidden
entries.summary: Kayak will remind you if active filters are hiding cheaper options.

entries.title: Merch to incentivise retention
entries.summary: To encourage people to track ski activity in the app, across both multiple days and multiple trips (boosting retention), you'll unlock merch for doing so.
entries.analysis: This is brilliant, because a free hoody for simply tracking your activity feels like a valuable gift. But you need to track multiple seasons. i.e., they're encouraging retention with branded merch. This also gets them through their trial period, so may have an upsell benefit too.

entries.title: Planta's social proof
entries.summary: When adding a new plant, Planta will leverage a moment of contextualised social proof.

entries.title: Teasing benefits on hover
entries.summary: When hovering over the upgrade button, Dub will tease the key benefits inline.
entries.analysis: This is smart because it both contextualises the benefit, and encourages you to click to learn more. i.e., may increase the click-through-rate of this button.

entries.title: A variable explosion
entries.summary: When liking a comment in Asana, the exploding "Like" animation is slightly different each time.
entries.analysis: Or, it's on a long enough / randomised cycle that you can't notice.

entries.title: A retention-boosting email
entries.summary: If you stop using Duolingo for a few days, you'll get an email reminder framing how much "further ahead" other learners are.
entries.analysis: There are three biases this leans into. It frames the "delay in days" in terms of your goal and minutes you could have spent learning. It highlights the opportunity cost, and a pinch of loss aversion of falling behind, mixed with some social proof.

entries.title: Interactive Pizza Bot
entries.summary: If you poke the Dominos "pizza bot", it'll react both visibly and with a text message.

entries.title: Go on Monzo, grill me
entries.summary: Monzo lets you decide what style you want your end of year review in. Having a choice may engage you more, as you feel more in control.

entries.title: Notion's AI icon
entries.summary: Notion's AI icon will animate based on the task it's doing (e.g., writing, thinking or checking).

entries.title: The 'Rare find' interaction
entries.summary: If an Airbnb is a "Rare find", you'll see an animated bubble appear, which collapses into the sticky footer as you scroll.
entries.analysis: The great bit of UX here is how they draw attention to it initially, and then collapse it into a more traditional position.

entries.title: Contextual reminders (permissions)
entries.summary: When you set a show reminder, Netflix will check to see if you've allowed notification permissions, and show you a contextualised prompt.
entries.analysis: i.e., they're actually checking every time to make sure that you won't miss a reminder.

entries.title: Making it about you
entries.summary: Blinkist will nudge you into sharing a URL, by showing someone with your name doing that thing.
entries.analysis: There's a subtle nuance here worth experimenting with. Blinkist are showing you a picture of a person with your name , taking an action, with the receiver waiting. It's a nudge to do that thing, and triggers cognitive dissonance. i.e., "you're the type of person to do this action, see ".

entries.title: A reminder to charge
entries.summary: The Uber Eats driver app will remind drivers to charge their phones.
entries.analysis: i.e., to reduce the number of orders that go missing because a driver's phone dies en-route.

entries.title: Dynamic preferences on Shop
entries.summary: When setting your preferences, you'll see a selection of items that dynamically highlight (or get more opaque) depending on your choices.

entries.title: Uber's rider checklist
entries.summary: When signing up as an Uber bicycle Rider, you'll see a road safety checklist where items visually appear (like a bag, or phone holder) on the rider.

entries.title: Lowering the commitment
entries.summary: Blinkist reduces the commitment to create an account by wording the CTA as "tell us your interests". It also frames the experience as being personalised.

entries.title: Hidden logo in screenshots
entries.summary: RevenueCat hides their logo behind Apple's Dynamic Island, so that it appears in screenshots.

entries.title: Starling's snow
entries.summary: In December, snow will fall down your screen and settle on the container where your balance is shown.
entries.analysis: This is very similar to the previous "falling show" animation they did—but with the added bonus of it cleverly settling.

entries.title: Santa is on his way
entries.summary: When looking at your home's geofencing settings, Tado will show Santa just out of radar.

entries.title: Share for a reward
entries.summary: To incentivise people to share their yearly recap, Duolingo offer a secret reward.
entries.analysis: To be clear, you could consider this to be engagement farming (in a bad way). But, it does incentivise an action, and you do actually unlock a collectible.

entries.title: It's not about the shoes
entries.summary: Runna automatically tracks the "running miles" of your shoes. This gives you an incentive (new shoes) and an overarching trackable goal.
entries.analysis: This is an important nuance: counting down the remaining miles of the lifecycle of a pair of shoes, is not the same as counting total miles ran. Lifecycle = counting down, the Goal Gradient Effect (you get closer). Total miles ran = counting up, you're keeping a tally with no set goal. They could have an arbitrary target like "run 1,000 miles", but it lacks the incentive at the end. i.e., you get to treat yourself to a new pair of shoes.

entries.title: 100% zoom
entries.summary: When changing the zoom level on Craft, the default "100%" setting is slightly blue to help frame the other options.

entries.title: Your local store closes at...
entries.summary: When browsing Home Depot, your local store will be shown in the header, along with when that store closes.
entries.analysis: This is particularly useful for Home Depot, because unlike Amazon, people often pick things up in person. It gives people confidence that they actually can get to that store in time.

entries.title: A seed into an oak tree
entries.summary: During focus sessions, your seed will slowly grow into a plant.
entries.analysis: This is essentially a slow sand timer. It leverages the Goal Gradient Effect, because (unlike a timer), you're watching something get bigger. It carries a more visual sense of progress.

entries.title: Who hurt you?
entries.summary: When looking at your "year in review", Duolingo will show on-brand comments about low usage statistics. It's like a reverse celebration.
entries.analysis: Importantly, it demonstrates the value of catering to low (unimpressive) numbers. Even better, when combined with a prompt to share, I imagine these do quite well. It's more novel than your "positive" streaks.

entries.title: Live travel times
entries.summary: On Citymapper you can drag a pin around the map and get a real time calculation of the total travel time from your current location.
entries.analysis: it being in real time demonstrates the accuracy of their data. Plus, it's a bit of user delight seeing how roads, turnings and areas add (or remove) travel time.

entries.title: Skipping in-content ads
entries.summary: YouTube will show a "jump ahead" button to skip in-content ads.
entries.analysis: What's interesting here is that they're (possibly experimenting) with ways to allow an ad-free experience on Premium. i.e., making it easier to skip adverts that are inside the video content itself.

entries.title: Sing Along!
entries.summary: When viewing lyrics in Apple Music, you can "turn down" the voice track so you can sing along like karaoke.

entries.title: Jam-packed with psychology
entries.summary: Right before you create an account, Ahead use three pieces of clever psychology: framing (x2) and compliance.
entries.analysis: Let's go through each in turn. 1. Framing: they're calling it the " Fair Trial " policy. It frames how you think about the rest of the screen. 2. Framing: they'll directly compare the cost of Ahead to a cinema ticket. 3. Compliance: using the FITD effect, the CTA label makes you agree that it's fair.

entries.title: When the CTA is hidden...
entries.summary: If the order button is out of view in the checkout, Too Good To Go will show a prompt to scroll.
entries.analysis: This is surprisingly nice actually. It helps to avoids the moment of " oh what, where do I click? ".

entries.title: Custom designed perks
entries.summary: The Monzo Perks redemption screen has a dynamic design depending on what you're redeeming.

entries.title: Starter challenges on Twitch
entries.summary: When you start streaming on Twitch, they'll give you a few challenges. These set achievable targets, without you having to lower your own aspirations.
entries.analysis: i.e., nobody wakes up and thinks: " I'll be happy if I can get 5 people talking ". But actually, it's a reasonable goal to aim for in the short term. And leverages the Goal Gradient Effect.

entries.title: Popcorn alerts
entries.summary: When pre-ordering cinema snacks on Fandango, you can ask to be texted 10 minutes before it's ready to collect.
entries.analysis: i.e., you don't have to queue up and wait around—you could go and find your seat first.

entries.title: You look like your passport
entries.summary: When verifying your ID, Incode will show a picture of your ID next to your recent selfie, and then merge the two as it completes.
entries.analysis: It's satisfying to watch your ID picture, and your "real" picture merge into one.

entries.title: Cliffhanger + Social Proof
entries.summary: Instagram will occasionally show featured posts from Threads. These then dynamically show which of your friends use the Threads app.
entries.analysis: This is a piece of clickbait, followed up with some well timed (and very specific) social proof.

entries.title: Exploding reactions
entries.summary: To make leaving a reaction more satisfying, the emoji will explode in a confetti cannon.

entries.title: A balloon to grab your attention
entries.summary: To draw your attention to their onboarding prompts, Libby will show a balloon that animates and rises out of view when you tap on it.

entries.title: Setting the tone
entries.summary: Before taking an image, BeReal will show a dynamic loading state that sets a tone about being the "real you".
entries.analysis: BeReal's whole USP is about showing the "real you". So, as the app is loading, these quotes help remind people that BeReal isn't Instagram. It reminds you why you use the app.

entries.title: You've got another account
entries.summary: TradingView will recognise (and warn you) if you log into a free account, while they suspect you also have a premium one.
entries.analysis: This is intended to catch you before you panic that all of your premium features have disappeared.

entries.title: Quick microphone test
entries.summary: When you first join a StreamYard room, you'll see a dynamic message on your microphone settings, which reassures you if it's working.

entries.title: Framing your savings
entries.summary: If you're a Canva Pro subscriber, they'll frame how much you're saving in "premium elements" (add-ons you'd otherwise pay for).
entries.analysis: This reinforces the value of Canva Pro, and frames the monthly cost.

entries.title: 5 paws out of 5
entries.summary: If you take your pet with you on a Lyft ride (called a "Pet Ride"), the rating system will be paws.

entries.title: Creative one-time passwords
entries.summary: When creating an account on Coolors, the one-time-password is a colour hex code.
entries.analysis: This is really clever (and creative) branding. Like an easter egg in your inbox.

entries.title: Remind me about this
entries.summary: Hargreaves Lansdown will prompt you if you have cash available to invest. You can then dismiss it and be reminded about it later.
entries.analysis: I'm more impressed by the "remind me in 2 weeks / 1 month" options. It avoids you forgetting to invest cash, and then missing out on time in the market.

entries.title: Craft's DIY onboarding
entries.summary: For onboarding, Craft will show you "things" and get you to actually interact with those components in a short demo.
entries.analysis: This is almost like a video game tutorial, and it works really well.

entries.title: Custom push notifications
entries.summary: Twitch lets you customise the push notification that your followers get when you go live.
entries.analysis: This makes that initial hook more dynamic and personal.

entries.title: Clarifying your age
entries.summary: When signing up to BeReal, the CTA label will confirm your age. This makes it easier to spot mistakes, and feels more personal.

entries.title: Pie chart icons
entries.summary: To make it slightly easier to digest the payment schedule, Klarna show pie charts next to the values.

entries.title: Apple TV's dynamic CTAs
entries.summary: Apple TV show dynamic CTA labels, depending on if you've watched that show before or not.
entries.analysis: Plus, by saying "Play First Episode", they're making it inviting and clear to get started. i.e., it's not committing to watching an entire series. It's "just the first episode".

entries.title: Welcome to Telegram
entries.summary: When you set up Telegram for the first time, the empty state shows a chick hatching from an egg.

entries.title: Paired with a masala
entries.summary: Swiggy will recommend dishes that are usually paired together, directly in your basket.

entries.title: Your convo will be saved
entries.summary: When the ChatGPT app has an update, you'll be reassured that your conversations will be saved.

entries.title: Dynamic purchase CTA
entries.summary: When placing an order with Etsy, the final CTA will dynamically show where you're sending the item to.
entries.analysis: This helps you be that little bit more confident in the purchase, and it feels very personal. Especially useful when sending gifts to other people's addresses.

entries.title: A clever CTA label
entries.summary: Once a daily surprise bag is out of stock, Too Good To Go will dynamically display exactly when it sold out.
entries.analysis: This ramps up both social proof and loss aversion. i.e., "it sold out at 9.15, that means it must be good".

entries.title: Rewatch the tour
entries.summary: After signing up to Airbnb, a link to rewatch the onboarding tour is nestled above the search. This disappears once you've started browsing.

entries.title: Okay, let's focus on one
entries.summary: If you select multiple goals when signing up to Rocket Money, they'll ask you which you want to focus on first. This regains focus (and framing).
entries.analysis: This is actually really important, because you might select goals that have very different time horizons. i.e., "buying a house in 5 years" and "saving for a holiday this summer". By focusing on one goal, you're having to select the most important one, and that gives them context to frame the benefits of Rocket Money.

entries.title: Templates without the hassle
entries.summary: When using a template, Trello has a clear toggle to keep (or remove) the sample cards inside that template.
entries.analysis: i.e., you can copy the architecture (columns), and the design, but not inherit the cards themselves.

entries.title: Managing group orders
entries.summary: Chipotle will ask you who specific items are for and then group your basket by people—making it easier to double check your order.
entries.analysis: i.e., instead of the basket arbitrarily showing the order by "time added". To be clear, one problem with Chipotle is that they force you to do this. It'd be better if it was optional, and if you skipped, it dismissed this question for that order.

entries.title: Apple's private email addresses
entries.summary: If you sign up to Atlassian using Apple's "hide my email", you'll see a warning that your team might not recognise your email address.
entries.analysis: Atlassian (Jira & Trello), are very collaborative tools. Being able to find co-workers is a key feature. This context helps reduce future pains / issues.

entries.title: Cancelling Headspace
entries.summary: When going to cancel Headspace, you'll be prompted to "sleep on it", and be shown a sleeping meditation.
entries.analysis: The reason I don't feel like this is a dark pattern, is because it's still easy to cancel. Instead, they're framing one of their benefits (sleep exercises) along with some heavy social proof.

entries.title: A rollercoaster is loading
entries.summary: The loading animation for Europa Park is a rollercoaster.

entries.title: Zero-click onboarding
entries.summary: When pointing out key features, Spoil Me will show a tooltip that automatically hides after a few moments.
entries.analysis: This removes the click to hide each onboarding tooltip. But, as a warning, this might not be suitable for complicated or long tooltips. This only works because what they're pointing out is short and very easy to understand.

entries.title: We didn't call you
entries.summary: When paying someone new, Starling will dynamically show a message that customer services haven't called you.
entries.analysis: i.e., if someone has called you fraudulently claiming to be Starling, and asked you to move money.

entries.title: A nutrition label worth sharing
entries.summary: Spacebar creates “memories” from audio journaling, and provides a sharable summary in the style of a nutrition label.
entries.analysis: i.e., the creativity might create more shares.

entries.title: That deal is "hot"
entries.summary: The "liking" mechanism on HotUKDeals is a temperature gauge which changes colour.

entries.title: Number of guests
entries.summary: When requesting to reserve a special Airbnb experience, the number of guests will be shown as illustrated people standing next to each other.

entries.title: Your Medium account
entries.summary: Medium will remind you that you’re logged out of your members account, and that's why you can't read the content.
entries.analysis: The magic here is recognising that the user has previously logged in (perhaps a cookie), and then contextualising why the user is seeing a sub-par experience.

entries.title: Creating an itinerary
entries.summary: Travel planner Eighty Days lets you paste a message (or video transcript), and turn it into a draft itinerary.
entries.analysis: This is a great use of (possibly AI), to make better recommendations, and save the user time. It'd be even better if you could just paste a URL.

entries.title: Google Pay QR
entries.summary: Instead of scanning a QR code live, Google Pay will let you upload an image of a QR code (i.e., if a friend sent you one).
entries.analysis: This is great, and once you've seen it once, it's obvious. People send QR codes over WhatsApp, and this is a much easier way of quickly paying that person.

entries.title: Priority hospital delivery
entries.summary: When ordering food to a hospital, Blinkit show this compassionate message.

entries.title: Conversations are happening
entries.summary: When browsing channels, Reddit will show a typing indicator to give the impression that conversation is happening in real time.
entries.analysis: This makes it feel like you're missing out on real conversation / community. (Rather than a static forum or inactive channel).

entries.title: Quickly removing filters
entries.summary: If Airbnb has no results to show you, they'll suggest removing specific filters with a single tap.
entries.analysis: i.e., you don't need to click into the main "filters" tab, to then remove something.

entries.title: Smarter notification prompts
entries.summary: If you've taken the free trial, Headspace will show a different notification prompt during sign up.
entries.analysis: This uses the more convincing reason of "being notified before your trial ends". i.e., by being dynamic, they're likely getting a better opt-in rate.

entries.title: Referral calculator
entries.summary: When inviting friends, you'll have an interactive calculator to estimate how much you'll earn as a reward.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this also frames the benefit, and anchors your expectations.

entries.title: Pay after ordering
entries.summary: If a payment fails on Swiggy, they offer the option to place the order first and pay later while waiting for the delivery.
entries.analysis: This likely improves conversions, because it allows users to avoid delays and resolve payment issues afterwards. i.e., you're hungry, you want the food to be on the way , and you'll sort out the payment later.

entries.title: Browsing Airbnb co-hosts
entries.summary: When browsing co-hosts on Airbnb, they'll dynamically show host information about whichever filter you applied.

entries.title: Explore in guest mode
entries.summary: Before creating an account, JazzCash will let you explore the app as a guest—giving you a low commitment way of understanding the features.
entries.analysis: This is great because the account creation process has several steps (including verifying your mobile number, IDs).

entries.title: Finite streaks to start
entries.summary: When signing up to Finch, you don't need to commit to an infinite streak. You can choose to just use the app for a few days in a row.
entries.analysis: This lower commitment might help people to actually use the app, knowing that it's not going to go on forever. It feels more achievable.

entries.title: Hiding your Headspace streaks
entries.summary: Headspace lets you hide your streaks, giving your flexibility to meditate on your own schedule.
entries.analysis: To be clear, these streaks are only visible to you anyway. This is simply to reduce the anxiety of seeing your streaks constantly fail (i.e., experiencing the loss), because you're not a daily user.

entries.title: Predictive emojis
entries.summary: As you type an entry title, the emoji icon will update in real time, showing their best suggestion.

entries.title: Hold to commit
entries.summary: When signing up to Yazio, you'll be asked to tap and hold the screen as a form of commitment.
entries.analysis: This small act of compliance (and commitment) may help to increase effort and motivation during the session.

entries.title: Getting back on the wagon
entries.summary: If you return to Headspace after a period of inactivity, one of your daily tasks will be to learn about "getting back on the wagon". This explains the importance of routine, and then prompts you to turn on reminders.
entries.analysis: It's really effective timing.

entries.title: NBA's spoiler-free mode
entries.summary: The NBA app will let you hide match spoilers as you browse.

entries.title: A feed of social proof
entries.summary: The meditation app Open will show how many active members there are, along with a real time "community" feed (which updates every few seconds).
