entries.analysis: This is clear social proof. But also, it's helping expose the types of activities that other people are doing in the app. i.e., it's showing off the breadth of content available.

entries.title: Biggest sale in 60+ days
entries.summary: Etsy will frame a seller's deals with contextual banners, if that's the best deal they've had for a while.

entries.title: Planning your day
entries.summary: When creating a daily plan on Structured, you can either answer questions about today (in the past tense), or plan for tomorrow (framed in the future).

entries.title: Select your hold music
entries.summary: Octopus Energy allows you to customise your hold music if you call them.
entries.analysis: Waiting on the phone is a lot more tolerable if you’ve had some input over what you're listening to (or, it can be silence). The default is also smart: it uses a popular song from your childhood. (i.e., probably something nostalgic that you've not heard in a long time).

entries.title: Your first task is easy
entries.summary: When signing up to Me+, you'll be encouraged to pick one "easy" task to start. This both acts as onboarding, and gets a quick win under your belt.
entries.analysis: Importantly, you're given 4 options that are all simple. That feeling of satisfaction motivates you to continue.

entries.title: You're not on Wi-Fi
entries.summary: When viewing your camera feeds on mobile data, DMSS will warn you about high data consumption.

entries.title: Sharing is turned on
entries.summary: After copying a link on Endel, you'll see this spinner appear. This is a cue that the page will update in real time.

entries.title: Social proof + habits = savings
entries.summary: The finance app Jupiter will frame the expected annual savings from your new "habit", backed up with some social proof.
entries.analysis: This is interesting for two reasons: 1. By framing it annually, you're showing how a small habit can grow over time. 2. By leveraging social proof, you're increasing the perceived likelihood of that outcome happening.

entries.title: Let's make a contract
entries.summary: When signing up, you'll be prompted to sign your name to "create a contract". This signals a moment of commitment.
entries.analysis: This is both Cognitive Dissonance (not wanting to break previous promises) and the Foot-in-the-door effect. It creates a memorable moment of commitment, and may reduce churn.

entries.title: Knowing your target market
entries.summary: When sharing links on Napper (the baby sleep tracker), you'll see a link to open your Facebook groups, with a prompt to post it to a "mom group".
entries.analysis: i.e., they've really nailed a growth strategy, and outright ask for what they want. Then, have a deep link to open your groups inside Facebook.

entries.title: Dynamic icon suggestion
entries.summary: When creating a new task, the app icon will change dynamically (in real time) as you type.
entries.analysis: Something I noticed about this, was how much more trust you place in the dynamic suggestions, if you can watch it update. i.e., as apposed to coming up with one icon when you've finished typing.

entries.title: Trial ending reminder
entries.summary: Duolingo will remind you when you've only got a week left of your trial, and then show you all the features that you'll lose access to.
entries.analysis: i.e., triggering loss aversion.

entries.title: Testimonial exposure
entries.summary: During onboarding, Me+ will leverage the Labour Illusion and some (likely) intentional latency to create space to show you social proof.
entries.analysis: i.e., "creating your personal journey" is designed to make you associate more value to the experience. And while creating this space, they're showing you social proof. This primes you for the upsell on the next step.

entries.title: Framing your savings
entries.summary: After logging milage into Stride, you'll be shown exactly how much you've saved. This frames the benefit of logging milage, to encourage a habit.
entries.analysis: Without this, you'd need to wait for weeks (or months) to find out what you actually saved from logging that trip.

entries.title: Super Duo-app
entries.summary: After subscribing to Duolingo, they'll prompt you to change the icon (default is on). This visually reminds you that you're getting a special experience every time you see it.
entries.analysis: i.e., every time you see the Duolingo app, you'll remember you're paying for an extra experience.

entries.title: Taking a break
entries.summary: If you go to delist your property on Airbnb, you'll instead be prompted to pause bookings for a while.
entries.analysis: Pausing subscriptions have been shown to reduce churn in subscriptions. So it's fair to assume this makes it easier for hosts to come back to Airbnb after an extended break. This leaves the default as "active but paused", not "delisted". Psychologically that makes a difference.

entries.title: Studying with you
entries.summary: The focus app Flipd will show you how many other strangers are focusing on a "library session" right now.
entries.analysis: This combines Social Proof (i.e., look how many people use this app), and a kind of social pressure to reach your goal.

entries.title: Apple's starter pack
entries.summary: During the Apple Arcade onboarding, you'll be asked to select four categories that you're interested in. They'll then present you with a " starter pack " of items to download.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this component is then shown on the homepage feed. You can download all four in a single tap.

entries.title: Starting a program
entries.summary: When starting a program on Headspace, you'll be prompted to commit to the process and turn on "mindful reminders".
entries.analysis: There's a lot going on here. Firstly, it frames the benefit of the reminders, with some positive reinforcement of your future goal. i.e., "I will show up for..." The process also leverages Cognitive Dissonance, as you're identifying yourself as the type of person who will show up every day. With a final CTA as a declaration of your commitment.

entries.title: The Hinge monster
entries.summary: While creating your Hinge dating profile, this character will get more detailed. At the end, it bounces off the screen to find your dates.
entries.analysis: i.e., it starts as a pair of eyes rolling on the floor, and morphs into the monster. It keeps you interested as you want to see what the next stages are.

entries.title: Mentioned in a doc
entries.summary: If you mention a contact by name in a Google Doc, but they don't have access to the file, you'll see a sharing prompt.

entries.title: Dynamic review prompts
entries.summary: When leaving a review on Amazon, these prompts will automatically be ticked off as you type related content.
entries.analysis: i.e., it recognises that you've talked about a specific topic, and will tick it off, and then show you another prompt.

entries.title: Growing a forest
entries.summary: The Swiss bank Neon will plant real trees when users spend money. This is then visualised to the user inside their app.
entries.analysis: i.e., this helps to frame the benefit (and progress) of planting trees.

entries.title: Wait, email the CEO
entries.summary: When going to remove the Matter app, you'll see a prompt to email the CEO.
entries.analysis: Presumably this is to gather feedback, and hopefully reduce churn.

entries.title: Unlocking Amazon's joke
entries.summary: When reviewing items, Amazon will incentivise you to review more by offering to tell you a joke.

entries.title: Creating a habit
entries.summary: After using Slack's "Catch Up" feature (and feeling "complete"), they'll prompt you to add the widget to your home screen.
entries.analysis: i.e., while celebrating the moment of completion (Peak End), they'll nudge you about how to get that feeling more easily in the future. The timing is perfect.

entries.title: Spotify prompting mixes
entries.summary: Occasionally, Spotify will show you your top genres, and prompt if you want a playlist mix created for you.
entries.analysis: This is a way of onboarding the "mixes" feature, with very low effort. i.e., "Law of minimal effort". It's just one click.

entries.title: Dynamic event previews
entries.summary: When changing the default length of a calendar event, Amie shows a dynamic preview of that event using your current time.
entries.analysis: i.e., it's both dynamic for the current time of day, and updates to show the length of the event.

entries.title: Onboarding Aha! Moment
entries.summary: During onboarding, Arc will encourage you to toggle adverts on a webpage on and off, to demonstrate the difference in experience (and the value they're adding).
entries.analysis: This works really well because it's interactive. It uses your curiosity to deliver a (maybe) Aha! Moment. A video, or a text callout wouldn't work nearly as well.

entries.title: Social pressure (and proof)
entries.summary: Etsy will show a banner when there is limited stock of an item, but many people have it in their baskets.
entries.analysis: To be clear, this is on the "social pressure" side of UX, and I wouldn't recommend it for everyone. Many sites do this when it isn't true, and that's absolutely a dark pattern. But, it's an interesting technique if you can dial in the right level of nudge (without being too manipulative).

entries.title: Intelligent post tagging
entries.summary: When adding a daily journal, Joor will interpret your content and assign a title and emotion. This reduces decision fatigue by making your daily entries simpler.
entries.analysis: This is an interesting idea, because you don't have to come up with an interesting daily title every day.

entries.title: Using onboarding as a nudge
entries.summary: Headspace nudges you into sharing your 30-day passes with a friend, at the same time as onboarding that component.
entries.analysis: This might sound obvious, but the UX magic is subtle. They're explaining where the "share" feature is, and drawing your attention to it. But also linking the " why ". i.e., you want to have someone to share this with, and here's 30-days to get them started.

entries.title: Dynamic suggestions
entries.summary: When entering gift mode on Etsy, the main search box will dynamically show themes and ideas, with matching background art.
entries.analysis: Similar to Pinterest, this helps you think of ideas, and break some " urrrm, what do I search for " paralysis.

entries.title: Helping you stay organised
entries.summary: Airbnb will automatically add recently viewed listings into a specific collection, to help you track everything you've seen.

entries.title: Smarter button placement
entries.summary: If you close the keyboard while adding a description to an event, Amie will automatically switch the position of the CTA to be more easily reachable.
entries.analysis: The really clever thing to notice here, is that if you've tapped "return" on the iOS keyboard, your thumb will still be in that area. By moving the main CTA to that position, it's much easier to close the keyboard, read what you've written, and continue.

entries.title: Draw your avatar
entries.summary: The journaling app Joor asks you to commit during onboarding, by drawing your avatar. This is then shown to you every session, as a reminder of your pact.
entries.analysis: This is a really great innovation on similar "commit to using this app" techniques. What I love about this is that your commitment is literally shown to you all the time. It's a constant reminder.

entries.title: Wink to flip
entries.summary: Crouton uses the iPhone's camera to offer hands free navigation. You can wink to flip through the recipe steps or open your mouth to view the ingredients list.

entries.title: In-feed benefit reminders
entries.summary: While scrolling down the homepage, you'll occasionally be reminded about the silent benefits of your YouTube Premium subscription.
entries.analysis: This helps to frame the benefit you're receiving, and remind you why you're still paying for the service. It'll likely reduce churn.

entries.title: Nudging the health benefits
entries.summary: Zepto adds tags to local vegetables, herbs and fruits. This creates a halo effect around their health, nudging users to purchase.
entries.analysis: By reinforcing the (probably known) health benefits, they're able to reframe the purchase as being for health , not dinner. i.e., "I'm buying this to feel healthy ", not "I'm buying this to have in my pasta". That reframing may increase the average basket size.

entries.title: Now share your meal
entries.summary: Creme prompts the user to share photos as the last step in a recipe.
entries.analysis: This is a simple, but brilliant way of timing a share. You've just cooked a meal and you're keen to eat it—so might just put your phone down. But this interjects and reminds you about sharing all your hard work.

entries.title: Financial 404s
entries.summary: The Financial Times' 404 page lists "economical satire" to justify the missing content.

entries.title: Contrast warning
entries.summary: Slack will warn you if your SlackBot's text contrast is too low, and then confirm when it is readable.

entries.title: Dynamically changing keyboard
entries.summary: In iMessage, the keyboard dynamically changes alphabets to fit the conversation language.

entries.title: Hold this fare
entries.summary: Air New Zealand allows you to hold the price of a fare for a small fee. This frames the cost of losing that flight, and offers a lower commitment way of proceeding.
entries.analysis: There are a few psychological biases at play here. 1. Simply by offering this (even if you don't select it) they've framed the cost of losing this flight. (Loss Aversion & Framing). 2. It provides a low-commitment way to proceed (the foot-in-the-door effect). Which will have very high conversions later down the funnel.

entries.title: Something's cooking...
entries.summary: While the recipe app Creme is loading, you'll see a dynamic quote with four oven dials.

entries.title: An animated hero
entries.summary: Focus Traveller is a focus app where the user's hero hikes a trail. The longer you focus, the further they walk. When the timer is paused, they take a break by a bonfire.
entries.analysis: There are lots of elements done really well here. You can track your progress on a top-down map (right screenshot). While resting, there are sounds (rain, crickets, stream, trees) with beautiful animations. And the longer you focus, you'll unlock "gear" for your hiker hero.

entries.title: A worldwide treasure hunt
entries.summary: The weather app Carrot includes "missions" to locate specific places on a map, some of which are puzzles.
entries.analysis: These have no direct value on your experience, but it's pure User Delight (and wanting to hunt them all). This then likely creates shares, referrals and talking points.

entries.title: You're so close...
entries.summary: Headway will remind you if you're only a few "key points" away from extending your streak, but you've closed the book you're reading.
entries.analysis: i.e., it'll frame how much effort is required to reach your reading goal.

entries.title: Wear your medal
entries.summary: After unlocking a running badge, you're prompted to ‘wear your medal’ and display it on your profile.
entries.analysis: This adds an element of social pressure to maintain streaks, and an incentive to beat your friends. Importantly, the timing is great here. If the user had to go into their profile to "add a badge", then the conversion rates would be far lower.

entries.title: Growing your garden
entries.summary: The app hero starts out with an empty garden, a promise that it'll grow, and over weeks it'll build an entire castle.
entries.analysis: This is a brilliant use of the Curiosity Gap, User Delight and the Endowment Effect. You want to see what the next stage is, and it keeps the app interesting every day.

entries.title: Dynamic recipe portions
entries.summary: Julienne will automatically scale all of the portions, steps and ingredients based on the number of people you're cooking for.

entries.title: Selecting overkill mode
entries.summary: The weather app Carrot starts by letting you select your "style". This modifies the content and the humour throughout.
entries.analysis: This both personalises the experience for your taste , but also demonstrates a real attention to detail. It makes you curious about what "over-the-top" content you'll find. It's both User Delight and the Endowment Effect.

entries.title: Swapping the keyboard
entries.summary: When entering your flight number on TimeShifter, the keyboard automatically shifts from letters to numbers.
entries.analysis: It's rare you see a field have both letters and numbers in a fixed pattern. But when you do, this is a great way of reducing clicks / effort.

entries.title: Planning a route
entries.summary: When planning a trip in advance, predicted traffic will be shown in this scrolling chart, with context on when to leave.
entries.analysis: What feels special about this, is how they've visualised the traffic over time. It gives you a really clear way of finding a good time to leave (and understanding when traffic is high / low).

entries.title: Incentivising action
entries.summary: Before going live on Instagram, you'll be told how many of your contacts are online.
entries.analysis: This does two things: 1. Nudges you to go online when the count is high. It encourages action by limiting the risk that nobody watches. 2. Avoids the disappointment of going live if nobody is online, and you have a bad first live experience. (Loss Aversion).

entries.title: Completing the value loop
entries.summary: After submitting a traffic report on Waze, you'll see gems appear and the context that you're helping nearby users.
entries.analysis: They're trying to complete the action loop by framing the value of the action that you just did. i.e., "you're doing this to help others". This is the element that makes the action feel satisfying.

entries.title: An interactive onboarding demo
entries.summary: Explain Everything is a whiteboard app, and the onboarding is an interactive presentation for you to scribble on as it introduces you to the features.

entries.title: Time zone context
entries.summary: If you’re planning a trip to a different time zone, Citymapper will specify which time zone it’s using for the departing time.
entries.analysis: It's a small line of context, but really important for reducing anxiety.

entries.title: Playful sound EQ
entries.summary: Overcast shows the sound visualizer inside the pause button.

entries.title: Please don't go...
entries.summary: When deleting the Captions iOS app, the action menu acts as a last attempt to avoid churn.

entries.title: Choosing what to eat
entries.summary: Swiggy tackle meal decision fatigue with Eatlists, using a combination of social proof (sharing meals) and Hicks Law (easier decisions).
entries.analysis: To be clear, the social proof here is that someone you know has added this meal into their list , and then shared it with you.

entries.title: Opps, go back
entries.summary: If you accidentally skip forward on a podcast, Overcast gives you a button with a short timer to go back to where you were.

entries.title: You're probably in a cupboard
entries.summary: British Gas have a quick-tap torch button on their meter reading page, knowing you're probably in a dark cupboard looking at your meter.

entries.title: This item has been delivered
entries.summary: When manually marking an item as delivered on Shop, you'll see a burst of confetti.

entries.title: Framing the benefits
entries.summary: Overcast dynamically shortens silences in podcasts, and then tells you how many hours you’ve saved with this feature.
entries.analysis: This frames an otherwise silent benefit of Overcast over say, Apple Podcasts.

entries.title: Double purchases
entries.summary: If you attempt to buy the same item on Airalo twice in quick succession, it adds intentional friction to make sure you’re not accidentally doing so.

entries.title: One word social proof
entries.summary: For some restaurants, Deliveroo will show one word "tags" from recent reviews as a social nudge.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you see 4.8 stars, and the tag "tasty food", it sets an expectation. You start to imagine the food being tasty.

entries.title: People keep coming back
entries.summary: To leverage social proof, Fiverr will display a badge if a seller has a lot of repeat customers.

entries.title: Your barista is...
entries.summary: When ordering Philz Coffee to pick-up, you'll be told who the barista making your coffee is.

entries.title: Replace this ad
entries.summary: The free version of Maple is ad-supported, but it provides a compelling reason to upgrade by showing you a ‘Replace this ad with family photos’ prompt.
entries.analysis: In other words, they show ads, but occasionally replace ads with prompts to upgrade.

entries.title: Wait, change the voice
entries.summary: The first time you leave a meditation, Balance offers to switch the voice of the trainer, in case the first one was putting you off.
entries.analysis: This is great as it helps reduce churn and introduce a new feature at the same time.

entries.title: Cancelling Spotify
entries.summary: When cancelling Spotify, you'll be shown a breakdown of the downgrades you'll experience on the free plan.
entries.analysis: This triggers Loss Aversion (I don't want to lose access to my downloads), and frames how often you'll hear adverts.

entries.title: A cookie graveyard
entries.summary: Crumbl has a ‘cookie graveyard’ for flavours that are no longer sold, giving an incentive to turn on notifications for future cookie updates.
entries.analysis: In other words, they're creating FOMO (Loss Aversion), and combining it with a call to action (notifications).

entries.title: Daily facts
entries.summary: Balance will show tap-to-reveal daily facts, both educating you about the topic, and creating curiosity.
entries.analysis: i.e., once you've seen the prompt, it's hard not to click it. This increases engagement, and slowly educates people about women's health.

entries.title: Deleting paid apps
entries.summary: iOS will prompt you if you're about to delete an app that you have an active subscription for.

entries.title: Copying your order number
entries.summary: Clicking on the 'help' button automatically copies your order number, so you can paste it easily when reaching out to customer support.

entries.title: Sharing map data to WhatsApp
entries.summary: iOS will share recent Maps destinations to other apps (like WhatsApp), predicting when you're about to send someone an address.

entries.title: Playful permission request
entries.summary: While asking you to opt in to receive offers, the Lego store shows a toy megaphone that if you tap, will shoot out Lego bricks.

entries.title: One-tap to answer
entries.summary: After leaving a review, the screen is showered in confetti, and you may be asked a series of one-tap questions to get more data.
entries.analysis: This is great because it gives you that moment of celebration before asking you to invest more time. i.e., if they asked those questions first, more people would skip them.

entries.title: Framing the value
entries.summary: Speechify frame how much time you've saved compared to a regular listening speed.
entries.analysis: i.e., your analytics of total listening time might be interesting, but the actual value is how much time you've saved .

entries.title: Recording audio on Discord
entries.summary: When recording an audio clip on Discord, the footer will bounce around to the level (volume) of your voice.
entries.analysis: This isn't just for fun, it actually signals to the user that it's picking up your voice. i.e., it's working .

entries.title: Message from the founder
entries.summary: Before even creating an account on Balance, you can click to open a message from the founder.
entries.analysis: This is a really easy way of adding context (and a sales pitch) to your app, before asking people for their information.

entries.title: Progress bar personality
entries.summary: While placing an order, the Lego store will reveal a series of Lego heads lighting up. The curiosity builds excitement and makes the wait less painful.
entries.analysis: This could be a boring spinner, but you kind of want to see what the next Lego head is going to be. It's a combination of User Delight and the Curiosity Gap.

entries.title: Ending the doom scroll
entries.summary: If you browse experiences without selecting a date, GetYourGuide will show this quick-select component inside the feed—making it easy to narrow down your search.
entries.analysis: Importantly, they know that their users usually browse for same day / next day experiences, and this cuts the doom loop of browsing everything, but not knowing if anything is available. In a single tap, you're looking at a smaller list of things you can actually book.

entries.title: Quick-tap torch
entries.summary: Whilst scanning a device on Lime, there's a single tap button to turn on your iPhone's torch.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is helpful if you're trying to scan a code in the dark.

entries.title: Woah, a steep hill
entries.summary: When navigating, Apple Maps will indicate if a hill is particularly steep.

entries.title: Faux pause, forced social proof
entries.summary: During onboarding, Falou will (I imagine) artificially create a pause while it "builds" your plan, forcing you to read a few of their testimonials.
entries.analysis: This is a combination of intentional friction (getting someone to stop), the Labour Illusion (showing how much effort goes into "building" the plan), and social proof. Likely a killer 3x combo for conversion rates.

entries.title: Hands-free CTA
entries.summary: Key actions on Waze are CTAs that auto-progress without input. This means you can select a destination and start driving, without waiting for the navigation to complete.

entries.title: Clever use of a secondary CTA
entries.summary: When making a reservation, the secondary CTA isn't to 'cancel', but to "Check Your Booking". This subtly calms hesitation, with a much lower risk next step.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this is still a forward step. i.e., a CTA for "compare prices with other hotels" introduces more options. It may put the user off. But this funnels hesitant users into a modal that has a "Book now" CTA.

entries.title: Social pressure (and proof)
entries.summary: Headspace will show how many users are currently meditating. This uses social proof as a slight pressure to start.
entries.analysis: It adds a very slight collective bond to an otherwise very solo activity.

entries.title: Encouraging fairer reviews
entries.summary: Whilst reviewing an order, Fiverr will remind you of how long it took and how much you've spent. It's subtle, but helps frame the value that you've received.
entries.analysis: i.e., you might get frustrated towards the end of the project by revisions, small delays, or random exporting quirks. This helps to reframe the whole experience, which is typically very affordable, and offsets some of the negative Peak End effects. I've been told by Fiverr that this didn't create a flurry of "more positive" reviews, but reviews that were fairer overall.

entries.title: Speed = productivity boost
entries.summary: When selecting your reading speed, Speechify will frame it in terms of boosted productivity.

entries.title: Encouraging diversity
entries.summary: Waze will occasionally show 'special themed' prompts, which modify your avatar and voice settings. This helps keep the map diverse and visually interesting for everyone.
entries.analysis: Plus, it's a great way of getting the user to try new voices.

entries.title: Woah, 3 familiar voices
entries.summary: Speechify's (the text-to-voice app) onboarding process is all narrated, and they immediately cycle between recognisable voices.
entries.analysis: This helps you understand the value proposition within a few seconds: this isn't a boring text-to-speech tool, it sounds really human .

entries.title: Asana's Yeti
entries.summary: Very rarely, if you drag a card from one column to another, Asana will show an animated Yeti hiding underneath it.
entries.analysis: This objectively does nothing, but is a moment of randomness and user delight. The key is that is happens so infrequently that you don't get bored of it (maybe 1 in 100 times).

entries.title: Confetti on scroll
entries.summary: If you scroll to the GiveButter footer, they'll show a small blast of confetti.

entries.title: Rounding up prices
entries.summary: To reduce complexity, prices on Fiverr are rounded up on the browse pages.
entries.analysis: This should make deciding slightly easier, and browsing less overwhelming. i.e., seeing " from £105 " is less distracting than everything being " from £105.34 to £109.93 ".

entries.title: Setting context on every visit
entries.summary: After opening Waze, you'll immediately see the number of Wazers and reports around you. This social proof frames the value proposition within a few seconds.
entries.analysis: i.e., you load the app, and are then reminded " oh yeah, this is why I use Waze, it's got people reporting stuff ". It should be a lesson: you might need to "re-set the table" every time the user opens the app.

entries.title: Auto-pasting a URL
entries.summary: When tapping to load an article from a URL, Speechify will automatically paste your clipboard.
entries.analysis: i.e., this saves you having to paste the URL you've just copied from somewhere else.

entries.title: Hitting milestones
entries.summary: After hitting daily streak milestones, you'll be given a moment to celebrate.

entries.title: Offline meditation
entries.summary: If you access the Wholegrain Digital website without an internet connection, you'll be offered some in-browser meditation.

entries.title: Your next achievement is...
entries.summary: Skillshare will dynamically suggest your next milestone, based on activities you're started. If you ignore one, it'll refresh and show another.
entries.analysis: This is clever because it keeps your next milestone within reach, and reminds you what you need to do. Plus, if you ignore one, it'll refresh and show another. It maintains the progression towards that goal between sessions.

entries.title: Signalling that something has changed
entries.summary: To celebrate Prime day, Amazon uses their blue Prime colour in the search bar focus state.
entries.analysis: This is a subtle signal that something has changed (i.e., you're getting something special). It frames the experience. Or rather, it breaks the orange pattern that you're used to, and reminds you about the event.

entries.title: Password peekaboo
entries.summary: When typing your password on ReadMe, the owl will animate to cover their eyes.

entries.title: Action into sign-up loop
entries.summary: If you attempt to like a Twitch stream, without having an account, you'll see a sign-up prompt personalised with the streamer that you were just watching.
entries.analysis: This slight personalisation helps to frame the benefits of having an account better (and takes almost no effort).

entries.title: You're done for the day!
entries.summary: After completing your last to do item, TickTick will turn that row into a celebratory banner.
entries.analysis: This micro dose of celebration could help create stickier habits.

entries.title: Bouncing above the thumb
entries.summary: When tapping (and holding) to set a to-do item as important, the icon will bounce above where your thumb would be, so you can actually see it.
entries.analysis: Without this, your thumb would be covering the button itself, so you may not know when you can release the hold.

entries.title: Pizza confetti
entries.summary: After applying a coupon, you'll see a subtle confetti animation inside the pop-up.

entries.title: You've reached a 404 page
entries.summary: WhatsApp’s 404 page recreates a conversation.

entries.title: Making that first order
entries.summary: To help get new users into their first order, Fiverr will frame specific sellers as being good at guiding new buyers through the process.
entries.analysis: Let's assume that the Aha! Moment for Fiverr is when someone has received their first order, and they're happy with the outcome. This is a mechanism of pushing people into that funnel, addressing the "I don't understand" concern head-on.

entries.title: Maximise the value of Perks
entries.summary: If you've not redeemed your weekly perk, Monzo will prompt you that you can gift it to a friend.
entries.analysis: This does two things: 1. Provides the premium user with more "value", as their benefits aren't going to waste. 2. Acts as a referral flow for the receiver—they're told that they could get this every week with Monzo Perks. And it's loaded with social proof, because they're reminded that their friend already pays for it.

entries.title: Your Deliveroo rider is close
entries.summary: When your rider is less than a minute away, the tracker will update from a moving car to show an animation of a knife and fork.

entries.title: It's 19.24 for this person
entries.summary: When messaging a freelancer on Fiverr, it'll show their local time, with a little moon icon if it's late.
entries.analysis: This helps set realistic expectations of how long it will take for them to reply.

entries.title: 1 minute, 10 minutes
entries.summary: When configuring timers on Loops, it'll dynamically change from 'minute' to 'minutes' depending on input.
entries.analysis: This is really simple, but you'd be amazed how often people forget to do this.

entries.title: Skillshare's confetti shower
entries.summary: After completing a course on Skillshare, the video player is showered in confetti.

entries.title: Deliveroo's gift reveal
entries.summary: If you send an order to someone as a gift, they'll receive a wrapped present which tears as they open it, revealing the order.

entries.title: You've started making money
entries.summary: Uber will take a moment to celebrate when you've saved more money through Uber One, than the monthly cost of the subscription.
entries.analysis: This is reinforcing their value anchor (i.e., saving money), and will be a moment that really reduces churn.

entries.title: Context about hiding
entries.summary: If you hide a movie on Amazon Prime, you'll see reassuring context that you can still search for this title later.
entries.analysis: This is important because it explains the mechanism of hiding . Without this, people might be nervous to hide movies, incase they then can never see them again. i.e., explaining the mechanism will be helping to increase usage.

entries.title: This font size is omnipresent
entries.summary: If you manually take font sizes above the Figjam default options, you'll see extreme names like "towering" and "omnipresent".
entries.analysis: The purpose of this is to actually detract some of the complexity from the interface. Figjam don't want you spending ages finding the perfect font size. They want it to feel simple and easy to use, so once you've created a massive font, they'll just name it, and not show something specific like "1281px".

entries.title: Fill it up
entries.summary: Caribou Coffee’s loading animation is a coffee cup filling up.

entries.title: Run stick man, run
entries.summary: While waiting for Fitness+ to personalise your workout recommendations, the loading animation shows a stick figure running across the screen.

entries.title: Creating loss aversion
entries.summary: If you go to cancel an Uber, you'll see some clever wording. They'll anchor you to a reasonable waiting time (10 minutes) and trigger loss aversion (having to start again).
entries.analysis: This is subtle, but considerably better than a version of this without both of those elements.

entries.title: Comment left as a draft
entries.summary: Asana will remind you via email if you've commented on a task, but not published it, and most likely forgotten about it.

entries.title: A nudge that you're premium
entries.summary: After upgrading to a paid Monzo account, the logo on your card will have a gradient as a subtle reminder that you're on a premium experience.
entries.analysis: This acts as a nudge to frame your experience. It's a reminder that some of the silent benefits exist.

entries.title: Limited supply of hotels
entries.summary: In the checkout, Booking will tell you that similar hotels which match your criteria have already sold out, creating scarcity.

entries.title: Celebration feeds into challenges
entries.summary: After completing your budgeting goal, you'll earn points and be showered in confetti. Importantly, this then introduces you to new challenges.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is a great hook to bring people into the other challenges. Without this, they might not even bother looking at the challenges tab.

entries.title: Empty states can be personal
entries.summary: The spending categories on Monzo have dynamic and themed empty states.

entries.title: Euros 2024 celebration
entries.summary: If you Google a specific football result, you might see this celebratory overlay.

entries.title: Your order arrived early
entries.summary: If your order arrives sooner than expected, you'll get a custom notification. This might frame the experience and lead to more tips.
entries.analysis: If nothing else, you'll associate more value to your Uber One subscription (which they remind you about).

entries.title: Promos and hooks in your basket
entries.summary: Uber Eats will show progress towards promos, and the promo savings right above the CTA in your basket.
entries.analysis: This includes a hook to sign up to Uber One (their premium subscription).

entries.title: Suggested searches
entries.summary: After making a search, Ocado will show suggested new queries. These are accompanying products, not similar products.
entries.analysis: The difference here is subtle but important: they're showing fruit to go with your greek yoghurt, not another type of yoghurt. This requires mapping what else customers like (or buy with X). It's likely that this increases the number of items customers go on and add to their cart, because it acts as a kind of discovery tool.

entries.title: Casting a shadow
entries.summary: As your Uber Eats driver moves along the map, they cast a shadow on the ground.

entries.title: Tracking milestones
entries.summary: On Skillshare, you'll collect badges for completing certain actions, and they'll dynamically show you another reachable milestone in the header.
entries.analysis: The key here is that the milestone isn't too farfetched. It gives you something to aim for, and a sense of progression.

entries.title: Unlocking better interest rates
entries.summary: When upgrading Monzo, you'll see slightly modified content if you're boosting the interest rate in an existing savings pot, rather than a brand new one.
entries.analysis: This adds a bit of context and frames the benefit better. i.e., "you're now getting", rather than mentioning a perk you haven't utilised yet.

entries.title: Creating weekly reminders
entries.summary: When creating your weekly reminder, Skillshare will set the default suggestion as the current time and day (assuming you started using the app at a convenient time).
entries.analysis: They've also used more natural language, which may be easier to digest. e.g., " Tuesdays at 8pm " rather than " Weekly, Tuesday, 20.00 ".

entries.title: 7 seconds to check your order
entries.summary: After placing an order, Uber Eats will give you 7 seconds to verify the order details.
entries.analysis: This counts down as a bar inside the CTA, and the timer in the label. Also notice the badge "you seem far away from this address", which shows if your current location doesn't match where you're ordering the food to.

entries.title: Discounts and free delivery
entries.summary: Deliveroo have a progress bar encouraging you to reach the minimum spend for free delivery, and they'll also show you the value of your discounts inside the CTA.
entries.analysis: To be clear, the discounts aren't the free delivery—these are two separate things. There must be a "minimum discount" to warrant the CTA label strikethrough. The progress bar is a classic example of the Goal Gradient Effect.

entries.title: Pre-delivery "use by" dates
entries.summary: Ocado will order your receipt by the 'use by' dates, so you know which items will have a short shelf life before they arrive.

entries.title: Creating medium-term goals
entries.summary: Duolingo repackage daily tasks into overarching monthly "quests", which will earn you a reward.
entries.analysis: If daily challenges help you in the short-term, and progressing through the stages is your longer term goal, this is a great mid-term incentive. And, they've not created any more content—they've just repackaged existing daily tasks and given the user a clearer goal.

entries.title: Anchoring your wait
entries.summary: Instead of an infinite (or unspecified) waiting period, Uber will set a 10-minute timer. If they miss it, they'll just add 5 minutes on. But it keeps you waiting by anchoring to a "reasonable" time.
entries.analysis: Imagine if they didn't do this. After 6 minutes of waiting, it might feel like you've waited ages, and you may cancel. But this uses the goal gradient effect (i.e., you're so close now), by having an anchor to wait for . The 5 minute add-on feels more reasonable than your first wait, so you hold on again .

entries.title: Album release countdown
entries.summary: Spotify will show a countdown for some upcoming albums, and let you listen to released singles that will be in that album.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this helps discovery, and encourages you to pre-save albums that you're waiting for.

entries.title: Apple's middle ellipsis
entries.summary: Apple (MacOS) will add an ellipsis in the middle of a sentence, not the end. This makes it easier to distinguish file additions like "_final".
entries.analysis: Imagine if it was shortened at the end, "this_important_document_version2" might lose the version number, and then be totally indistinguishable from the first version.

entries.title: Animated stick man
entries.summary: When selecting your activity level, an animated stick figure works out with increasing intensity.

entries.title: Username reminder
entries.summary: When searching for a Discord user, you'll be reminded about your own username.
entries.analysis: This is presumably to remind you that your username is something obscure like "panda_food_1271", and so you should add more context about who you are.

entries.title: The next realistic target
entries.summary: After completing a one-day streak, Brilliant will frame the value of reaching a three-day one. This keeps progress within reach, and contextualises it with your goal.
entries.analysis: What you should consider is that this might be more effective than going for a "100-day" streak, or an ambiguous infinite challenge. By keeping the next target clear, and just within reach, people may be more motivated to engage with the daily tasks.

entries.title: Creating curiosity
entries.summary: GoalQuest introduce you to a number of 'friends' during your daily activity, to try and build curiosity and variability.

entries.title: Variable rewards
entries.summary: After completing your daily goal, Mimo will let you open a safe and unlock a variable number of coins.
entries.analysis: i.e., the variable nature of the prize (and the animation) is what makes this feel rewarding.

entries.title: Woah, 5 minutes free
entries.summary: To encourage you to experience their paid features, GoalQuest will unlock the entire app for 5 minutes, with a visible timer.
entries.analysis: This is conceptually really interesting for a few reasons: 1. They've added scarcity (of an offer—it might go after 5 minutes). 2. It really encourages you to actually explore the features, because it's "now or never". It reframes your experience as value-hunting .

entries.title: A social welcome
entries.summary: When joining a new Discord server, you'll have a temporary 'new user' badge, which encourages people to be welcoming.

entries.title: Reducing time-to-value
entries.summary: When you open Ahead, they'll prompt you with an 'easy to answer' daily question. This feeds immediately into a related lesson, reducing the time-to-value down to almost one click.
entries.analysis: It also reminds the user why they downloaded Ahead in the first place. i.e., say someone has logged in to cancel their account—this is a great way of reducing churn, by reminding them of the value.

entries.title: You've already started
entries.summary: Brilliant will frame the trial process by including a completed step (setting your goals), encouraging you via the Foot-in-the-door effect.

entries.title: Mistakes = learning
entries.summary: Mimo will reframe your first mistake as a part of learning, and then use that moment to introduce the concept of "hearts".

entries.title: Highlighting the body parts
entries.summary: Whilst creating your fitness plan, BetterMe will literally highlight the body parts on an avatar.
entries.analysis: To be clear, I very much doubt this is to remind people where their arms or chest are—that's not the value. Instead, it's to help people visualise the areas of success . It makes the process feel more personal. i.e., you may start to feel more of a connection to that avatar.

entries.title: The illusion of effort
entries.summary: After the initial set-up, Mimo will take a moment to pause, and add context that it's personalising your experience.
entries.analysis: To be clear, this might all be for show. It actually doesn't matter though. Either way, it's leveraging the labour illusion , by making you believe that what you're about to experience is really personalised. This is likely to then give you a higher perception of value.

entries.title: Tap to blur
entries.summary: Lifelight has a ‘tap to blur’ option that lets you blur out specific messages, so you can be more selective about sharing screenshots.

entries.title: Pros have dual screens
entries.summary: The learn-to-code app Mimo will show dynamic onboarding animations, based on your skill level.

entries.title: Creating curiosity
entries.summary: To encourage you to start the next lesson, Brilliant show an open-ended question, or statement, for each one. This motivates you by creating a curiosity gap.
entries.analysis: In other words, you see this and think to yourself: " oh yeah, what does that mean? ". This idea could be repurposed for almost any type of content. It's the same principle as a YouTube thumbnail.

entries.title: Variable daily rewards
entries.summary: Finch not only provides variable rewards (rainbow stones), but combines that with different colours and celebration animations.
entries.analysis: This helps increase the intrigue about what your next reward will be.

entries.title: Setting a comfort level
entries.summary: When setting your current skill level, you'll be shown example equations to help you identify which bracket you're likely in.
entries.analysis: This is fantastic, because just saying "good, average, great" (for example), would be really hard to identify with. Showing examples like this helps you immediately identify where you struggle, and therefore you're confident in your decision.

entries.title: Leveraging early progress
entries.summary: When showing you a trial offer, Babbel includes the 'completed' step of installing the app. This creates a slight feeling of progression and prior investment.
entries.analysis: What's interesting here, is that they're framing it as "you've already started this journey". It creates the impression of progress, which may motivate more people to continue.

entries.title: Daily rewards, with a twist
entries.summary: To leverage your curiosity, Finch will send your bird mascot off 'on an adventure' (to get your daily reward), and use the wait to frame notification permissions.
entries.analysis: This is interesting for a few reasons: 1. The curiosity gap it creates will keep people interested. It makes you want to come back later (and not immediately delete the app). 2. It's a great way of prompting for notification permissions. 3. Completing actions on Finch (like lessons) will reduce the time it takes the bird to return.

entries.title: Dynamic notification prompts
entries.summary: Soundcloud will ask you which artists you like, and then use that to immediately personalise their notification prompt.
entries.analysis: i.e., it's far more convincing to be dynamic—it's framed the notifications as more of a benefit.

entries.title: Pitstop for motivation
entries.summary: Halfway through a lesson, Babbel will pause to remind you about your progress.
entries.analysis: This works because of the goal gradient effect. It draws your attention to the positive progress you're making.

entries.title: Your busy schedule
entries.summary: After creating an account, BetterMe will frame the plan as being personalised, factoring in your busy schedule.
entries.analysis: I suspect that this is artificial latency, purely designed to make you perceive more value from the experience. Plus, it's brilliantly framed to remind you that general workouts don't factor in your busy schedule .

entries.title: Anti-churn (vacation mode)
entries.summary: When attempting to delete a focus session, Opal suggests disabling it temporarily instead of deleting if you’re going on vacation.
entries.analysis: This makes it easier to set things back up the next time you want to resume a focus session, and is likely to reduce churn, as people get back from holiday and can't be bothered to set it back up.

entries.title: Quotes during loading
entries.summary: Zomato display a series of quotes about food (or facts related to the company) while the page loads.

entries.title: Interactive scales
entries.summary: Entering your height and weight into JustFit brings up an interactive scale with a ratchet effect (haptics) as you swipe along.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is slightly more interesting than just entering it into a plain field.

entries.title: Your expected roadmap
entries.summary: Foodvisor’s fitness plan shows a literal roadmap that animates (unfolding) as you continue scrolling through it.
entries.analysis: This may be really motivating, as it helps to describe and plot the milestones between you now, and future you. It quantifies a pretty general goal of "getting fit".

entries.title: Bouncing audio levels
entries.summary: The 'unmute' button on Soundcloud has a background that bounces with the audio levels—making it more tempting to unmute.
entries.analysis: i.e., you can't hear it, but you can now see it. This is the curiosity gap —you want to "fill in the gaps".

entries.title: Context for SMS verification
entries.summary: If you request a second SMS message, Zomato will use a push notification to add context that you need to check your text messages.
entries.analysis: This only happens if the user has requested a second SMS (i.e., they're having trouble with something ).

entries.title: Nudging towards healthier options
entries.summary: Food delivery app Zomato will suggest healthier options, and let you replace those items in a single click.
entries.analysis: To be clear, this might have effects down the funnel. For example, people may feel guiltier using this app to order tasty (but unhealthy) food, and so use a guilt-free alternative. i.e., this may create churn, or , it could promote positive behaviour that leads to long term customer loyalty (or both).

entries.title: You lifted an entire bus
entries.summary: After a workout, Hevy will frame your achievement, and give you a social card to share.
entries.analysis: I imagine this has a really positive effect on doing another rep (i.e., adding another 20kg onto your total).

entries.title: Framing your goals
entries.summary: During onboarding, Wiser asks you about your daily reading goals, and then converts that into ‘books per year’ to frame the achievement.
entries.analysis: This is a common technique to turn effort (e.g., reading every day) into a longer term goal (reading lots of books). You could do this for fitness, accounting, TV—almost everything can be framed.

entries.title: Catching common typos
entries.summary: When creating an account, Slack will warn you if it thinks you've made a common typo in your email address.
entries.analysis: Importantly, you can still continue, this is just a way of catching typos early, before waiting for your confirmation email.

entries.title: Specialists not generalists
entries.summary: When searching for help with something, (e.g. furniture assembly), Taskrabbit dynamically shows metadata relevant to that specific task.
entries.analysis: This main purpose here, is building confidence that you’re hiring a specialist rather than a generalist. It's displaying dynamic content in the results, and relevant correct metadata.

entries.title: An onboarding progress (email)
entries.summary: During your Intercom trial, you'll get emails showing you your onboarding progression. This nudges you towards finishing the set-up.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is a reminder that you haven't configured Intercom yet, and so the trial isn't being maximised.

entries.title: Visualising the money
entries.summary: I Am Sober will help you visualise your savings, eventually accumulating to a diamond ring celebration.

entries.title: Exploding to-do lists
entries.summary: You can pull up to clear completed items from your to-do list. When you do, the letters start jiggling and eventually explode over your screen.
entries.analysis: This is a celebratory micro-interaction—a bit like confetti, but more novel.

entries.title: Playful empty states
entries.summary: Superlist’s empty states are hand-drawn scribbles, instead of fairly boring grey rectangles.
entries.analysis: This is an easy way to expose more of their brand.

entries.title: Onboarding sidebar view
entries.summary: You need to sync a Google account during onboarding. To reduce anxiety during this step, Granola opens a second, smaller, side widget, to temporarily sit alongside this main window.
entries.analysis: In other words, if Granola did nothing, you'd open the Google window, and no longer be able to see Granola at all. You may start to question the data privacy, or why you're doing that step. Keeping a window in view helps to bridge this step, and reduce some of that "leap" anxiety.

entries.title: Creating immediate satisfaction
entries.summary: When creating a new 'habit' to stop (e.g., smoking), you'll immediately see these bars that fill up in real time, so you can witness your first "minute" milestone.
entries.analysis: Also, it appears that they start at 45 seconds automatically, so when it loads you only have 15 seconds to sit and wait for this first milestone.

entries.title: Dynamic placeholders
entries.summary: During onboarding, Slack will use your company information to show dynamic placeholder text—like example email addresses.
entries.analysis: This is a really subtle way of making the process feel more personalised. They've already collected your email address (say @microsoft.com), they might as well make the placeholders more dynamic and specific to you.

entries.title: Multiple magic link sessions
entries.summary: If you click a magic link, but are already logged in for a different user, BetterStack will show a clear prompt with context.
entries.analysis: This is particularly important for BetterStack as developers might manage multiple accounts, for multiple companies or products. i.e., without this, the magic link might just error, which would be really unhelpful.

entries.title: Recording a podcast
entries.summary: When recording a session on Riverside, you'll see a reassuring notification that the recorded quality will be higher than what you're seeing live.
entries.analysis: This helps avoid the "oh, you're kind of blurry, let me check my internet" conversations, as Riverside records locally from both devices.

entries.title: Be there soon
entries.summary: If you're invited to a Slack huddle, you can quickly tap "be there soon", and your avatar will show that status to everyone else.

entries.title: That was kind of you
entries.summary: After referring a friend and giving them a free trial, Headspace will frame your action positively, and remind you of the benefits of the service.
entries.analysis: i.e., this does two things, frames "being kind", but more importantly it reminds you about the benefits of Headspace. It'd be like inviting someone to BFM, and seeing a message " Well done, now they'll be great at UX too ". It makes you think "oh yeah, I'm getting better at UX myself ".

entries.title: Social proof + milestones
entries.summary: The habit-breaking app will show upcoming targets, framed with social proof of how many other users are about to hit that same milestone.
entries.analysis: This helps a fairly single player app feel like there are others, with some level of social encouragement.

entries.title: Stay awake mode
entries.summary: BBC Good Food has a "stay awake" mode, which stops your phone screen turning off while you cook.

entries.title: Meditating on Slack
entries.summary: If nobody joins your huddle, Slack will playfully frame it as a nice meditative session.
entries.analysis: Also what's interesting here is that they log how long you were on your own. This might be useful for statements like " I waited for 5 minutes but nobody showed up ".

entries.title: Onboarding the salary sorter
entries.summary: If Monzo predicts that a transaction is your salary, they'll show an inline prompt onboarding you to their "salary sorter" feature.

entries.title: Blocking mobile trackers
entries.summary: Arc will block trackers by default. But if you're having usability issues, you can allow them temporarily, and if the issue is resolved, automatically allow them in the future.
entries.analysis: There are lots of small things they do right here: 1. Frame the silent benefit of blocking trackers. 2. Offer a single-click way of allowing them temporarily. 3. Following up to see if the issue was fixed (not relying on the user being proactive). 4. Configuring long term settings based on the result.

entries.title: Easier board creation
entries.summary: When saving an item on Pinterest, they'll suggest new 'board names' dynamically based on the item you're looking at.
entries.analysis: This is both a way to make the actual curation process easier, but it also creates new ideas about other things to look at.

entries.title: An anti-churn tactic
entries.summary: If you go to cancel Uber One, they'll remind you how much money you've saved so far that month.
entries.analysis: This is clearly likely to reduce churn, if the savings is above (or near to) the monthly subscription fee.

entries.title: The streak loyalty pass
entries.summary: To help make maintaining a habit more comfortable, Sweepy lets you earn a free pass every X days, like a loyalty scheme.
entries.analysis: Plus, they allow you to customise how many streak days you need to do, to unlock an additional wildcard. This gives the user control, without a 'free pass' feeling like a failure.

entries.title: Check out the new you
entries.summary: Opal takes the information you provided during onboarding and later on shows you your new productivity/screen time habits as a direct comparison.
entries.analysis: This is great, as it gives you a self-reported reference point to compare to. This then frames the benefit you've received from the app.

entries.title: Cooking for 2 or 6?
entries.summary: This recipe app has a scrollable counter for how many people you’re cooking for, which then adjusts all of the ingredient quantities.

entries.title: Snooze these emails
entries.summary: CV Library recognises that their emails might not be relevant once you find a job, so it gives you an option to snooze them for 6 months instead of unsubscribing.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is an anti-churn mechanism. It provides an option other than all or nothing, and is proactive .

entries.title: Rise's sleep detection
entries.summary: When configuring Rise's sleep detection feature, they'll make the decision easier by giving context about how long most people would need.

entries.title: Responsive copywriting
entries.summary: Stoic’s copywriting is dynamic, reassuring you if you’ve had a bad sleep, and encouraging you if you've had a good one.

entries.title: Proactive onboarding
entries.summary: If you've recently joined a workspace on another device, Slack will proactively ask you if you want to add that channel into the app.
entries.analysis: Ultimately, this isn't just a time saver, it's a way to help you keep your app experience " complete ". Without this, Slack may find app that usage goes down, as people would feel like they had to manually add everything to their app each time.

entries.title: Predicted prices
entries.summary: Hopper is a price tracking app for flights and hotels, and it frames the value of a booking by showing a timeline of the predicted prices over time.
entries.analysis: The brilliantly frames the value of those available prices.

entries.title: A mini celebration
entries.summary: When rating a book 5 stars, you'll see a celebration burst onto screen.
entries.analysis: Interestingly, it only happens for 5 star ratings. One thing to be aware here is that people may want to see the animation again, and so if they're on the fence about 4 or 5 stars, it may nudge them towards better ratings. i.e., this could feasibly improve the average rating (which may or may not be sensible long term).

entries.title: Look at yourself, go on
entries.summary: If you attempt to use a blocked app, OneSec offers a variety of novel interventions, like opening the front camera so you have to stare at yourself breaking your own rules.
entries.analysis: This mirror example is amazing Cognitive Dissonance. It's essentially like saying: "look at yourself, you promised you wouldn't use Instagram, is that who you want to be?". It's also intentional friction, clearly. But it will be helping to stick to a habit.

entries.title: Add to calendar
entries.summary: After ordering a food delivery, you can easily create an event in your calendar with all of your slot information and booking reference.
entries.analysis: This is kind of obvious, and you'll see this in other places too. But it feels like a no brainer to add for food shopping, because you're going to have to be in .

entries.title: A utility loyalty scheme
entries.summary: Octopus Energy will let you earn Octopoints for completing habit-forming actions (like submitting meter readings), which you can then redeem for credit.

entries.title: Frequently used emojis
entries.summary: Loom will let you easily swap the default selection of emojis for your most frequently used ones.

entries.title: The goal gradient effect
entries.summary: When quitting an in-progress workout, JustFit tries to motivate you by showing how many steps are left.
entries.analysis: This is the Goal Gradient Effect, because it's essentially saying: " hey wait, you're already so close! ".

entries.title: Your life in milliseconds
entries.summary: Gyroscope shows your age to the millisecond level, as a running counter. As a health app, this frames the scarcity of your time.
entries.analysis: To be clear, this might also create a lot of anxiety, so I wouldn't recommend that you blindly copy this. But, it's an interesting idea to create a mindset .

entries.title: We'll text your teammate
entries.summary: ClearSpace texts a person (accountability teammate) of your choice if you don’t stick to your screen time goals.
entries.analysis: Accountability is a really useful lever when motiving someone to commit to a new habit.

entries.title: Contextual app events
entries.summary: As an anti-churn measure, Opal has a contextual "unblock all apps", with a motivational message, if you go to delete their app.
entries.analysis: For context, Opal is an app that blocks other apps, for focus. They know that people might just delete their app, instead of modifying all the blocking settings, and so this is a way to reduce unnecessary churn.

entries.title: Luggage icons
entries.summary: Instead of a typical basket icon, the Chase Travel portal uses a tiny suitcase.

entries.title: Dynamic onboarding actions
entries.summary: During onboarding, Speechify dynamically show usable settings and preferences in response to your answers.
entries.analysis: This is fantastic, because it both demonstrates that Speechify is a tool for you , and it helps you set up your account so that it's more personalised to your preferences. If there are no settings, they'll display really dynamic social proof.

entries.title: Rewarding household chores
entries.summary: After completing a task, a coin animates up from the task card and flies into your coin balance. Even better, if you don't want the gamification, you can turn toggle these elements off.
entries.analysis: Being able to turn these off feels like a great UX touch here.

entries.title: Select your own sticker
entries.summary: Crumbl encourages you to select a sticker for your gift wrapping, and will show a live preview on the box.
entries.analysis: This small act of customisation triggers the Endowment Effect (you see the box, you think of the person receiving it, and you can imagine them opening it).

entries.title: Adding friction into a snooze
entries.summary: Opal (the distraction-blocking app) will let you snooze a session of focus, but before you are able to access these options, there's a timer to give you a chance to reconsider.
entries.analysis: This is intentional friction, to give you a moment to engage your level 2 thinking. (i.e., level 1 is your 'subconscious' decision-making, which might not be what you want long term).

entries.title: Time-based defaults
entries.summary: Depending on the time of day, Apple Health will default to either a daily mood, or a "moment in the day" feeling.
entries.analysis: i.e., they've understood that if you're opening the app towards the end of the day, you're more likely to be posting a full day journal.

entries.title: Searching animation
entries.summary: When searching for old emails, Chuck uses an animated mascot that scuba dives into a bunch of envelopes to find your email.

entries.title: Live preview of cookies
entries.summary: Crumbl shows a live preview of all the cookies you’re ordering as you add them to your box, and then again during the checkout.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic way of using the endowment effect, to make you feel like they're your cookies. You picked them, that's your box.

entries.title: Vacation Mode for streaks
entries.summary: Sweepy has a Vacation Mode that pauses all tasks and streaks while you’re away, to help you maintain your habits when you come back.
entries.analysis: I'd go as far to say that this is a must have for any "streak" mechanism.

entries.title: Suggestions with definitions
entries.summary: In addition to spelling corrections, Word includes the definition of each suggestion, to make sure you choose the right word in context.

entries.title: Framing your progress
entries.summary: If you back out of reading a book before you’ve completed your daily reading goal, Wiser reminds you how close you are to completing it.
entries.analysis: This is a great way to frame the progress that they're making, using the goal gradient effect. There's no reason this couldn't be applied to reading articles, watching course videos, or engaging with something like Duolingo.

entries.title: Reducing freemium churn
entries.summary: After reaching the limits of the free tier, Clearspace gives you an option to upgrade, or to swap your new selection for an older one.
entries.analysis: This helps deliver a little extra value instead of people just backing out of the upgrade prompt (i.e., it may reduce churn).

entries.title: Fist bump as a pact
entries.summary: Instead of a 'continue' button, Opal has a fist bump to “solidify” your commitment to use the app.
entries.analysis: These micro-moments of commitment are trying to trigger cognitive dissonance, and the foot in the door effect. In short: by "committing" to this thing, they hope you'll then not want to go back on your word.

entries.title: Amazon Prime's X-Ray
entries.summary: When pausing on Amazon Prime, you'll see their feature X-Ray, which shows metadata like which songs are playing, or which cast are in the scene.
entries.analysis: The reason that this is fantastic UX on pause, is that they are anticipating that people might pause the film, to then Google which song is playing, or who the actor on screen is. This feature is timed really well, and non-intrusive.

entries.title: Your profile could look like this
entries.summary: Discord will let you view and interact with what your profile could look like, if you upgraded to their paid plan.
entries.analysis: This is the Endowment Effect, because they're hoping the user sees their account with a new theme, and then starts to feel like it shows off their personality / profile better.

entries.title: Overlaying videos in iOS
entries.summary: To help you configure permission settings, Amo opens a short video in multi-tasking mode, which then overlays the native iOS screen.

entries.title: Accountability partner
entries.summary: To improve the stickiness of a new habit, Atoms encourage you to invite an accountability partner.
entries.analysis: Having someone hold you accountable for a habit is a proven technique. The great UX here is building this directly into the app.

entries.title: Are you trying to sell it all?
entries.summary: Robinhood will proactively ask you if you're trying to sell everything, if you attempt to sell an amount within 35¢ of your total balance.

entries.title: Etsy's quick-select discount
entries.summary: As a seller, you've got a button in the chat interface to instantly create and send a coupon to the person you're talking to.

entries.title: A hidden easter egg
entries.summary: Tigerbeetle have an interactive mascot in their footer, which will greet you when clicked.

entries.title: An obvious visual cue
entries.summary: When changing the temperature on Tado, the entire background colour indicates how hot it is.
entries.analysis: This is both a cool visualisation trick, and also may help people build habits around not turning the heat up too high. i.e., if you crank it to 24', and the entire screen goes dark red, it'll make you rethink how hot you actually need the house to be.

entries.title: Atom's forced friction
entries.summary: To stop new users attempting to start too many new habits at once (and then failing), Atoms forces you to focus on one at a time.
entries.analysis: This is a type of intentional friction, and helps to create (hopefully) healthier habits, and reduce churn (failure).

entries.title: Winning Nectar points
entries.summary: After making a purchase, the Sainsbury's app shows a 'rub to reveal' mini game to unlock nectar points.
entries.analysis: This combines interactivity with variable rewards . The variable nature makes it addictive to play, creating a habit cue.

entries.title: Karate Kid easter egg
entries.summary: Statamic have a subtly interactive Daniel San (Karate Kid) figure that shows a message and plays the sound of Mister Miyagi saying "if do right no can defence".
entries.analysis: This is pure user delight.

entries.title: Reducing sign-up friction
entries.summary: After downloading the Atoms app, you can dive straight into creating a habit, without having to first create an account.
entries.analysis: This minimises loss aversion, because it reduces the sunk costs of trying the app.

entries.title: Animated learning
entries.summary: To teach you about how different market orders work, Robinhood have these animated charts demonstrating different market scenarios.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic way of explaining concepts, without requiring much reading.

entries.title: Double XP boosts
entries.summary: To encourage you to connect with friends, Duolingo will offer a time-limited XP boost.
entries.analysis: This is a fantastic referral scheme, because it encourages a positive behaviour (connecting with a friend improves your actual Duolingo experience), and you get an immediate reward that isn't financial. i.e., the reward encourages more engagement with the platform.

entries.title: Test your keyboard now
entries.summary: Matnsaz is an Urdu keyboard app that has a “try keyboard” option built into the onboarding. This opens a blank page where you can try out the keyboard and test the settings.
entries.analysis: i.e., this is so you don’t have to swipe back and forth between another app to test the changes.

entries.title: Where do you typically exercise?
entries.summary: Fitbod asks where you typically exercise, and then uses that information to pre-populate the equipment you’re likely to have there to help find suitable workouts.
entries.analysis: There's two bits of UX magic here: 1. Pre-populating common equipment, and letting the user modify the kit. 2. Personalising the workouts for their space.

entries.title: Leaderboards & the promotion zone
entries.summary: After completing a few lessons, you'll unlock leaderboards. These match you up against similarly skilled players, where the 'promotion zone' always feels within reach.
entries.analysis: What'll make this so effective is that the promotion zone isn't too challenging—it just requires a few more minutes of activity. In fact, Duolingo might populate the lower half of the leaderboard with bots. It doesn't matter, it all creates a sense of social achievement by completing more lessons. And by introducing it after you've made some progress, they're leveraging the Goal Gradient Effect.

entries.title: A jumping pig
entries.summary: The Porkbun mascot will jump up from the screen if you hover over the logo in their footer.

entries.title: Guided breathing, from a llama
entries.summary: Mindllama has beautiful animations to help you relax by guided breathing. The llama breathes in and out with you, complete with facial expressions.

entries.title: Remind me next month
entries.summary: After asking for a donation, The Guardian has an option to remind you next month—a subtle way of framing 'no', as a potential future commitment.
entries.analysis: This is a subtle way of changing 'no', using The Foot in the Door technique.

entries.title: Posting on social?
entries.summary: If you screenshot the Tiimo app, you'll see a prompt with their social media handle.

entries.title: Scratch to reveal
entries.summary: Mindllama hides daily inspirational quotes behind a sparkling filter that you ‘scratch’ over to reveal.
entries.analysis: This is a perfect example of the Curiosity Gap (and variable rewards). You know that something is there, and it's hard to ignore the intrigue—this builds anticipation.

entries.title: Animated Uber Eats icons
entries.summary: The active state for Uber's category icons are slightly modified, with variable background colours, to make it clearer which tab is selected.

entries.title: A mini celebration
entries.summary: Whenever you complete a task on Tiimo, the icon sparkles in celebration.

entries.title: Make a backup of this data
entries.summary: When you long-press the icon (to delete the app), Happy Scale reminds you to make a backup of your weight data so you don’t lose it.
entries.analysis: This is a really subtle and clever way of triggering loss aversion, and framing the potential risk of leaving the app.

entries.title: Padlet's loading bar
entries.summary: Padlet has a beautiful loading animation that lights up hexagons at the bottom.

entries.title: A unique colour selection process
entries.summary: Timepage offers a unique way to choose your theme colour by dropping animated balls on the screen.
entries.analysis: To be clear, this might not be good UX—I'm not even that sure. It's unique, and interesting, but hardly a good way to actually select a colour.

entries.title: Another way to verify your email address
entries.summary: When creating an account on Loops, instead of clicking a link in your inbox, you can verify your email address via a social login.
entries.analysis: This is great, as it removes the need to actually have to open your inbox.

entries.title: Uber's dynamic empty state
entries.summary: If you click to add a promo code onto an order, you'll see available promos (upsells) in a carousel at the bottom.
entries.analysis: This is a really non-intrusive way of reminding people that other offers are available.

entries.title: Sharing with your partner
entries.summary: This budgeting app will ask you who you spend money on. If you choose someone other than yourself, it lets you know that you can share your subscription with them too.
entries.analysis: This is a form of progressive disclosure, because you're introducing complexity only when the user is interested in it.

entries.title: Etsy's gift reveal
entries.summary: Before the physical item arrives, Etsy will let you share a "gift is on the way" message, where the receiver taps to reveal the item that's being sent to them.
entries.analysis: This helps offset the "this item might not arrive in time" hesitation, as it gives them something to send before the event (e.g., a birthday).

entries.title: Tap to unwrap
entries.summary: When gifting someone an order on Uber Eats, they'll receive a notification with a 'tap to unwrap' gift card.
entries.analysis: Even better, Uber Eats will let the sender see exactly what they're sending.

entries.title: Scheduling an Uber
entries.summary: When scheduling an Uber ride, you'll see a dynamic journey estimation for that time of day.

entries.title: Framing a cancellation
entries.summary: If you go to cancel Canva, they'll frame how good your current deal is, and leverage loss aversion by listing the features that your team are using, but would lose access to.
entries.analysis: This is really epic loss aversion plus framing. It doesn't feel predatory, instead it's clear context, at exactly the right time.

entries.title: Copied without tracking links
entries.summary: When copying a URL, Arc will automatically strip out any tracking links, then introduce that feature to you in a tooltip.
entries.analysis: This is both a great feature, and good UX to progressively onboard the user when they copy a link.

entries.title: Time to tune in
entries.summary: Tiimo has a “Tune in” button that offers a selection of radio tunes to provide background music as you go about planning your day.
entries.analysis: As a bonus bit of psychology, notice the "690 listeners". It creates the sense of community around the app, with many people listening to the same thing, at the same time.

entries.title: Ignoring silent mode
entries.summary: Mindllama relies on audio for guided meditation, so it offers a toggle to choose whether to respect or ignore the system-wide silent setting.
entries.analysis: This makes it easier to pick up the habit of using Mindllama, because you don't need to keep remembering to switch silent mode off and on again.

entries.title: A fantastic notification permission screen
entries.summary: This is a very convincing notification permission screen. You're asked a question for which the answer is almost certainly yes, shown an example, prompted to select a time, and reassured that this can be changed later.
