entries.title: Ask my parents to buy
entries.summary: Aceable is an app to help you learn to drive. Knowing that their target audience are usually under 18, and may not have their own credit card/purchasing power, they've added an “ask my parents to buy” button.
entries.analysis: This is an example of solving an external problem, outside of the app. i.e., the person could just hand the phone to their parents, but this helps in situations where you may not physically be near them. It aids a secondary issue.

entries.title: Contextualised performance
entries.summary: 'Peak' contextualises (and frames) your performance of brain-training games so you get a better sense of how well you did relative to others.
entries.analysis: i.e., the value is in the social comparison. It frames how well you've done.

entries.title: Let Duo find your level
entries.summary: When starting a new language, you can take a test to set an appropriate difficulty, and skip whatever lessons you already know.
entries.analysis: This helps with the initial habit formation, because the difficulty isn't too easy. If it were too easy, people might get bored and churn.

entries.title: Elevate's fresh start
entries.summary: If you’re returning to Elevate after a while, the app gives you an option to pick up where you left off, or start anew (leveraging the Fresh Start Effect).

entries.title: What you should be earning
entries.summary: Adzuna analyzes your CV to tell you how much you should be making. It'll then show an “email my boss” button, with a link to that calculation.
entries.analysis: The magical UX here is that they're linking the calculation to the next action (asking for a raise). i.e., it allows the user to send this information, without copy / pasting it somewhere.

entries.title: Creating your avatar
entries.summary: Duolingo let you create an avatar, which leverages the Endowment Effect, to show off your personality and build your character.

entries.title: Timestamping comments
entries.summary: On the app, YouTube have an button to quickly timestamp a video in the comments. This is important on mobile, as the time is hidden behind a click (not a hover).

entries.title: A better way to share
entries.summary: If you screenshot an item on Shop, it'll show a prompt to natively share the link instead.
entries.analysis: This is a great way to intercept people who clearly want to save (or share) an item, and give them a more functional way of doing so. It's a form of progressive disclosure, as you're explaining the feature (sharing) at the time when they may need it.

entries.title: On-brand spinners
entries.summary: When creating an account on SkyBet, the spinner looks like a football.

entries.title: Investing is like a plant
entries.summary: The investing app Sarwa has a 'pet plant' to promote regular investing. The plant stays healthy and continues to grow as long as you "water" it at least once a month by making a deposit into your investing account.

entries.title: Easier medication recognition
entries.summary: When setting up a medication in your Apple Health app, you'll be able to assign a shape and colour. This helps immediately visualise what you need to take.
entries.analysis: i.e., it makes it easier to recall, if your medication is in different shapes and forms.

entries.title: Live chat widgets
entries.summary: If you've got an active live chat, Revolut will show it dynamically on the home feed.

entries.title: Quantifying a free benefit
entries.summary: At the end of HackerNoon's free email, you'll get a reminder of how many 'free minutes' you received.
entries.analysis: This helps frame the value of what you're getting.

entries.title: Reassuring reload message
entries.summary: Figma understand that forcing an app update may cause anxiety about losing work between versions, so they've included this reassuring context in a banner.

entries.title: Goodreads' loading state
entries.summary: As the Goodreads' feed loads, it'll display a random quote about reading.

entries.title: Automatic versioning of files
entries.summary: Mac Preview will notify you if there's an older (or newer) version of a document available on your computer.

entries.title: Lean back mode
entries.summary: Cricbuzz has a ‘lean back’ mode that enlarges the cricket scores and strips away the excess, so you can glance at it while working at a desk.

entries.title: Navigating to a friend
entries.summary: If you use an iPhone's 'Find My' to navigate to a contact's location, you'll see a contextual prompt inside Apple Maps to share your ETA with that person.

entries.title: Onboarding test drive
entries.summary: After creating an account, Picture This will offer to demo the 'plant finder' with a stock image, to show you how it works.

entries.title: Custom and personalised goals
entries.summary: Twitch streamers can show custom goals (e.g., sub count, points or followers), and it'll be personalised to their brand colours.

entries.title: Google Fonts' 404
entries.summary: Searching for a Google font that doesn't exist, will show a random font-related 404 page.

entries.title: Viewed vs unviewed properties
entries.summary: Airbnb will shade map pins a slight grey once you've viewed them.
entries.analysis: i.e., this helps you actually efficiently use the map and not keep looking at the same ones.

entries.title: You're expecting?
entries.summary: When choosing your clothing size, Rent the Runway has an option for if you're pregnant. Clicking on this showers the screen in confetti, and guides you on how to choose maternity sizes.
entries.analysis: It also adds a pram icon to indicate maternity sizes and updates the clothing options automatically to show maternity styles.

entries.title: The local time is...
entries.summary: When chatting with a client or contractor, Fiverr displays their local time to help set a realistic expectation of when they might respond.

entries.title: Tinder-like mood tracker
entries.summary: Gyrscope has a swipe-based mood tracking interface. After the user has swiped across all cards, it calculates a summary of the user’s mood.

entries.title: Seconds since ring
entries.summary: When someone rings your Nest doorbell, it'll show how long it's been since the ring (so you know if your connection or reaction was slow).
entries.analysis: i.e., this adds context about how long ago the "real world" event was, and you can judge if it's worth you waiting for the feed to load.

entries.title: Personalised goals
entries.summary: To help encourage your new fitness habit, BetterMe encourages you to set a personalised goal and date, which they'll then frame your progress with.
entries.analysis: The takeaway here is that a personalised goal will usually always perform better than one arbitrarily assigned to the user.

entries.title: Post-call snapshots
entries.summary: After finishing a session (recording a podcast), Riverside will generate a variety of snapshots from the call, which you can share easily.
entries.analysis: What you need to remember here is that this isn't a Zoom call—it's a podcast / video interview, and therefore is likely to be published (one day).

entries.title: An anti-churn strategy
entries.summary: If you back out of making a pledge to offset your carbon footprint, Klima shows a popup offering to remind you if you need time to think.
entries.analysis: For context, this is an attempt to notice Decision Fatigue , and allow the user to come back to it later (instead of churning).

entries.title: Away from home
entries.summary: The Hue app uses illustrations to demonstrate how it can turn your lights on and off during the day if you’re away from home. This includes an animated clock.

entries.title: Instagram's hidden mini game
entries.summary: Tapping on an emoji in an Instagram chat will load a 'pong' mini game.

entries.title: Encouraging consistency
entries.summary: During onboarding, Stoic reminds you that consistency is key, and then asks what time of day you'd like to start your habit.
entries.analysis: Even better, they've subtly framed the benefit as being good for your mental health. i.e., reminding you why you're bothering.

entries.title: Auto-updating is a silent benefit
entries.summary: Automatically updating an app is a silent benefit that you may not notice. To combat this, Arc will notify you of updates, with a clear action to see exactly what's new.
entries.analysis: i.e., if they didn't do this, you might have no idea that the app is gradually getting better and better. This leverages the Labour Illusion, because you can see how much effort is going into the app.

entries.title: Scratch to win
entries.summary: John Lewis occasionally give out 'golden tickets', which you need to scratch to reveal if you've won.
entries.analysis: Design-wise, this is actually quite poor. But the mechanism of having the user interact with the voucher, and literally scratch it off builds the moment up. It also introduces a variable reward (i.e., you're not sure what you're going to get).

entries.title: Copywriting on Slack
entries.summary: Slack's empty states are really playful, and designed to put a smile on your face.
entries.analysis: To be clear, there is an arguement that this is bad UX, if it obscures the actual purpose of the empty state. For example, in one, it just says " You've read all the unread messages. Here's a pony ". The pony is playful, but is it confusing? Possibly.

entries.title: Dynamic flight information
entries.summary: If you long-press on the app icon, it’ll offer the helpline number for the airline of your upcoming flight.

entries.title: Onboarding shortcut
entries.summary: Hubspot notices if you're copy/pasting between emails, and will show you related feature onboarding.
entries.analysis: This is epic progressive disclosure.

entries.title: How did it feel?
entries.summary: As you enter a level of 'perceived exertion', the scale changes color, the mascot animates, and provides vibration haptics that grow stronger as you slide towards greater exertion.
entries.analysis: Even better, it also shows you what your average rating is, so you know if you're above average. This lets your calibrate what your 'normal' is.

entries.title: Confirmation bias in the checkout
entries.summary: When opting for the 'subscription' option in the Shop checkout, you'll see a subtitle praising you for your good decision.
entries.analysis: I'm torn about if this is great UX, or manipulative. Perhaps it's a bit of both. Essentially, they're using confirmation bias to tell you how great your decision-making is.

entries.title: 99+ tabs on Chrome
entries.summary: In order to not break the design when you have 100+ tabs, Chrome will swap the label for a smiley face.
entries.analysis: i.e., if they were to put "100" tabs, there would be an extra digit, and would need more spacing.

entries.title: A literal commitment pact
entries.summary: As a way to encourage commitment, Headway will literally ask you to sign a 'pact' to commit to reaching your goals.
entries.analysis: This is a classic case of the foot-in-the-door effect, tied up with some internal self-commitment. i.e., the cognitive dissonance of "I said I was going to do it, so now I can't be the type of person to not do it".

entries.title: Incognito browsing on Arc
entries.summary: When switching to incognito mode, these eyes will close to illustrate the additional privacy.
entries.analysis: This may actually help to reduce anxiety, as it reminds people why they're using incognito (although I imagine the impact is very small).

entries.title: An interactive clock
entries.summary: When scheduling an email to send later, you can click and drag the hands of the clock to set the time.

entries.title: Specific social proof
entries.summary: When selecting a journey on Ahead, you'll see specific social proof for each option, including a rating for how well matched to that course they think you are.
entries.analysis: This is a combination of social proof and specificity. Knowing that you're 90% matched to a course with 19k members really is really motivating.

entries.title: Splitting the bill
entries.summary: When splitting the bill, you'll see a scribbled strike-through of the original price, and an underline of the amount that you'll be paying.

entries.title: Gems for simply coming back
entries.summary: When returning after a long period of time, you’ll be rewarded a few gems for simply coming back, and a reminder that you get bonuses for returning each day.
entries.analysis: This makes returning feel like the first step (which you've completed), and can motivate you to continue doing more (i.e., completing a lesson).

entries.title: Roll the dice
entries.summary: To reduce option-paralysis (or decision fatigue), Headway has a “roll the dice” button to suggest a random read.

entries.title: Dynamic sun and moon
entries.summary: Deepstash lets you scroll through a timeline with a sun/moon animation, to choose when you want to receive notifications.

entries.title: Pre-cropped sharing
entries.summary: Taking a screenshot of your to-do list in Clear brings up a custom modal, where the app offers a pre-cropped version to share.
entries.analysis: This helps people easily share better quality screenshots.

entries.title: You previously used Google
entries.summary: When logging in, Canva remembers and displays which method you used to log in previously.
entries.analysis: This is super useful for social logins, as it stops people creating multiple accounts accidentally.

entries.title: Using data as social proof
entries.summary: To help you visualise the improvement of using their app, Ahead will show this (admittedly very non-scientific) chart during onboarding.
entries.analysis: The UX gem here is that they're helping visualise the data point, instead of meering stating "a XX% improvement".

entries.title: An update is available
entries.summary: When an update is available, Arc will show an animated banner. Clicking it explodes the heart into confetti, and then when the update is complete it'll show you what was changed.

entries.title: Don't leave anything in the car
entries.summary: As you approach your destination, Bolt will send you a notification reminding you not to leave anything in the car.

entries.title: Send tutorial to another device
entries.summary: Configuring the app requires using the Apple Shortcut app, so 'One Sec' offer to send a walkthrough to another device so you can follow along on a different screen.
entries.analysis: i.e., you can't watch a video on your phone and do the steps, as you'd have to close the video.

entries.title: You've got this!
entries.summary: When pulling to refresh the Slack mobile app, you'll see a random motivational message.

entries.title: Duolingo's fresh start
entries.summary: When returning after a long period of time, you’ll be asked if you want to continue with your previous course, or start something new.
entries.analysis: This is something called the Fresh Start Effect—people may find it easier to pick up a habit if they're starting fresh.

entries.title: Your knock has been heard
entries.summary: While waiting to join a call (in the lobby), you'll see when the host has acknowledged your 'knock', and they can message you in a single click.
entries.analysis: This massively reduces the "have they seen me" anxiety many people get when waiting to join a call.

entries.title: Introducing the mobile app
entries.summary: After commenting on the Figma app, you'll be prompted to download the mobile app to be notified if anyone responds. Even better, they'll show a QR code, or email you a link.
entries.analysis: This is a two-step benefit. 1. It progressively onboards a feature (the app). 2. It makes it easy to send instructions to another device (as you're on your laptop).

entries.title: YouTube's reaction emojis
entries.summary: When reacting to a live stream on YouTube, you'll see a waterfall of emojis, and yours will be marked so you can see them more clearly.
entries.analysis: This helps to reward people for actually 'reacting'. Without this, you might not know that your reaction ever even made it.

entries.title: Shouldn't you be offline?
entries.summary: If you send a message while your out-of-office is turned on, Slackbot will send you this private message.

entries.title: Quickly test this meeting
entries.summary: Whereby make it easy for people to test their new meeting rooms with a mobile phone, before inviting guests.
entries.analysis: This is incredible, as it allows them to see what their guests see. And they can be confident that the room is up and working.

entries.title: 3x more likely to reach your goal
entries.summary: When signing up to AllTrails, they'll frame their pro subscription in terms of your goal (i.e., actually getting outside and walking more).
entries.analysis: This helps people frame the cost / value, as "I'll be more likely to actually use this subscription / improve my fitness".

entries.title: Leave on all my devices
entries.summary: Teams lets the same person join a call on multiple devices (i.e., one for video, one for audio). Then, when leaving, there's an option to leave from every device at once.
entries.analysis: This is especially useful in board rooms, where you want your video individually without audio, and then one device only being connected to audio, for the entire room.

entries.title: Card number chunks
entries.summary: SadaPay shows your card number broken into chunks of 4 digits, making it easier to remember when typing or reciting.
entries.analysis: You could extend this to many things. For example, image you have a product code. Which of these is easier to read: Product #: 771822AAB Product #: 771-822-AAB

entries.title: Setting realistic reply expectations
entries.summary: When drafting an email to a contact who shares their 'out of office' with Google, you'll see when they're back. This helps to set a realistic expectation of when they'll reply.

entries.title: Visual seat selection
entries.summary: When checking-in through App in the Air, you can specify your seat selection with a visual of the plane cabin and seat position.

entries.title: You're alone on this call
entries.summary: If you multi-task while on a Google Meets call (iOS App), you'll see this prompt if all the other people leave.
entries.analysis: In other words, there's not enough space to show everyone's avatar, and it's so small you may not notice anyway. This prompt is a clear reminder that you're not talking to anyone.

entries.title: You burned one burger
entries.summary: Gentler Streak shows food emojis as an estimate of how many calories you burned—helping you visualise what is otherwise an abstract number.

entries.title: Welcome back celebration
entries.summary: When returning to Duolingo after a period of inactivity, you’ll see an animation of the bird waking up, and dancing when they see you.
entries.analysis: This may slightly help reduce some of the guilt that the user has, about giving up on their goal.

entries.title: Dynamic 'next' buttons
entries.summary: When listening to a pre-recorded Clubhouse room, the 'next' button dynamically shows the profile picture of whoever is talking next (and who you'd be skipping to).

entries.title: Hover-over lighting
entries.summary: When hovering over lights and lampshades on Pooky, the image will change to show the light turned on.

entries.title: A coffee savings calculator
entries.summary: Pret have an interactive savings calculator, to help frame the value of their coffee subscription.

entries.title: Top YouTube comment
entries.summary: When watching YouTube on the iOS app, the top comment will change every 30 seconds.
entries.analysis: This helps people get a flavour of what the comments are like, without the distraction of the feed.

entries.title: Safe volume levels
entries.summary: Apple highlights if the volume is above a healthy level for your ears. Once you bring this down, it'll turn green.

entries.title: Calculating your carbon footprint
entries.summary: As Kilma calculates your carbon footprint, it shows a dynamic background image for each question, and the score will update in real time at the top.

entries.title: Binge eating on weekends
entries.summary: LoseIt! recognizes the difficulty of maintaining a consistent diet plan, and allows you to have higher limits for weekends if you so choose.
entries.analysis: This helps you maintain the habit, and likely reduces churn.

entries.title: Lemonade's dynamic background image
entries.summary: After providing Lemonade your address, it dynamically updates the background of the hamburger menu to add an image of your building from Google street view.
entries.analysis: This is a light form of Endowment Effect (as it feels more personalised), but is also a little easter egg.

entries.title: Better curation on Pinterest
entries.summary: To help you keep your library organised, Pinterest will prompt you if an item has already been saved, but not sorted into a 'board'.
entries.analysis: Fundamentally, better curation will lead to a healthier long term habits.

entries.title: Financial demo mode
entries.summary: If you’re hesitant to link your bank accounts during onboarding, Copilot gives you the option to enter a demo mode with fully-populated data to get a feel of the app before committing.
entries.analysis: i.e., this lets you experience some of the benefits, without the risk.

entries.title: You're crushing it
entries.summary: MyFitnessPal has a smiley-shaped progress bar to add a bit of personality.

entries.title: A mystery newcomer discount
entries.summary: Impulse adds some mystery to the subscription experience, by letting users tap on a box to reveal a one-time discount.
entries.analysis: This feels like a type of variable reward, even if every user always gets the same discount.

entries.title: A grid of emotions
entries.summary: When creating a journal entry, Stoic shows emotions that dynamically match the mood you selected, which you can scroll in one giant dynamic grid.

entries.title: Thumb print signature
entries.summary: To boost commitment during onboarding, you're shown a contract in your own name, in first person, and you commit to it by mimicking a thumb impression as your signature.
entries.analysis: This is a form of FITD effect, because it's hoping that a small commitment (literally, your thumb), makes it more likely that you'll give the app a good go.

entries.title: Personalised referrals
entries.summary: When opening links from inside Reddit, Washington Post will show personalised offers.
entries.analysis: This kind of personalisation might make the referral stickier.

entries.title: Pre-defined allergens
entries.summary: You can pre-define any allergens or ingredients you want to avoid, and Sweetgreen will flag them app-wide.
entries.analysis: i.e., you don’t have to tap into each product to check its individual ingredient list.

entries.title: Amie's test mode onboarding
entries.summary: During onboarding, the questions you answer will dynamically populate the right-hand side, which effortlessly builds out your calendar and to do list.

entries.title: Here are some ideas
entries.summary: When creating a new list, TickTick will suggest common tasks relevant to that type of list. This helps you think of mundane daily tasks to include, making the experience stickier.
entries.analysis: In turn, but having more of your life written down in TickTick, you're more likely to stick around. i.e., this idea generation is a subtle way of getting you to embed more into the app.

entries.title: Framing the value of notifications
entries.summary: Citymapper frames the value of allowing notifications, by showing a few specific examples.

entries.title: Let us know when you're back
entries.summary: If you don't use Peanut for a while, they'll automatically hide your profile (from accumulating matches and notifications), and show this prompt when you next log in.
entries.analysis: This makes returning much easier, as you don't come back to hundreds of notifications and messages.

entries.title: A landmark spinner
entries.summary: The loading spinner on Turkish Airlines is styled like a radar, revealing landmarks from all around the world.

entries.title: Auto-muting duplicate notifications
entries.summary: To avoid duplicate notifications, which may frustrate the user, Slack will automatically turn off a less-preferred option.
entries.analysis: This both stops notifications being ignored habitually, and makes it less likely that users just turn notification permissions off (because they get the emails anyway).

entries.title: Super granular train data
entries.summary: CityMapper tells you which section of the train to sit in to minimize walking time along the platform to reach your exit.
entries.analysis: It also tells you which entrance and exit will minimize walking time to your destination.

entries.title: Review your focus
entries.summary: When a user returns to Refind after some time, the platform prompts a "Review Focus" to realign content with the user's current interests.
entries.analysis: In other words, this anticipates that the user may have stopped using Refind due to their changing interests.

entries.title: Your evening to-do list
entries.summary: If you sign up during the evening, Stoic will only prompt you to add one reflection in preparation for the typical three-a-day the next day.

entries.title: Dynamic weight loss timeline
entries.summary: If you're trying to lose weight, Foodvisor will ask you questions and then dynamically show a timeline, helping you visualise what the process will look like.
entries.analysis: Even better, they use a personal goal (such as a wedding) to remind the user why they're investing the time into this change. This all subtly uses the Endowment Effect—it feels like your journey.

entries.title: Finite streaks (not perpetual)
entries.summary: Stoic will give you a choice of how long you want to keep your streak for, so that it’s finite instead of perpetual.
entries.analysis: This is great, because not everyone wants to (or is ready to) commit to a lifetime habit. More people should try this.

entries.title: Turning off gamification
entries.summary: Stoic recognizes that the pressure of gamification isn’t for everyone, and lets you opt out of the streaks mechanism.

entries.title: Recovering your streaks
entries.summary: Readwise offers you the opportunity to recover your daily reading streak, similar to Snapchat. This motivates users not to give up on the habit.
entries.analysis: This is an interesting idea, because you wouldn't even necessarily need to tell the user that this mechanic exists in advance. Instead, this is a "oh, you failed, but never mind, you can undo that and not lose all your streaks". That's why this leverages Loss Aversion.

entries.title: Framing your savings
entries.summary: Bank of America assigns a dollar value to all the savings you’ve made as a Platinum member—quantifying the benefit you’re getting from things like waived fees.
entries.analysis: Importantly, this is reminding you (and framing) benefits that you otherwise may not be aware of.

entries.title: Notification previews
entries.summary: You can choose how many reminder notifications you want each day, and it'll dynamically update the image above to reflect your selection.
entries.analysis: The image being dynamic is more or less just user delight. But the better UX is the control, and the user initiated triggers. This helps people build better habits.

entries.title: Global summary settings
entries.summary: Zillow allows you to customize the property summaries, so that only relevant items appear when you're browsing and comparing options.
entries.analysis: This helps you quickly create a search that feels far more efficient and specific. i.e., you can change what appears at the top of an actual listing, not just what’s shown in the search results.

entries.title: Games inside video calls
entries.summary: The video chat service Around has built in 'team games', which allow a seamless transition into playing a whole team mini game.
entries.analysis: At first, I wondered why this was valuable. But after speaking to teams who use it, I was told that it adds an incentive to speed a meeting up. " If we finish early, we can play Bomberman quickly ".

entries.title: Gamified reading
entries.summary: Artifact gamifies the reading experience by visualising your entire progress in a streak, with badges and levels.

entries.title: Your area is not supported
entries.summary: If you’re not currently in a location supported by Transit, the app will offer to notify you when they’re available in that region.
entries.analysis: They even anticipate that you might delete the app, so they provide an option to send an email instead of a push notification.

entries.title: Airplane mode animation
entries.summary: When turning off airplane mode, the plane icon will actually fly away.

entries.title: Customising 'midnight time'
entries.summary: As a journaling app, Stoic recognizes that users may have very different routines, and has an option to customize the ‘midnight time’ that determines when a new day should start.
entries.analysis: This makes habit-forming behaviour easier to adopt—i.e., the user doesn't need to modify their night schedule / journaling behaviour, in order to fit the Stoic routine. It removes an arbitary rule.

entries.title: We're learning from you
entries.summary: After answering questions, Ahead will literally show a notification that they're listening and learning from you. This reiterates how personalised the experience is.
entries.analysis: This is a textbook example of the Labour Illusion. They're making it clear that every data point is included in the personalisation engine, encouraging you to answer more

entries.title: That product isn't available
entries.summary: If a product in your cart isn’t available, IKEA gives you options to save them for later and be reminded when they’re back.
entries.analysis: This may prevents shoppers from abandoning those items altogether, and gives IKEA data about which items people are actively waiting for.

entries.title: Leveraging loss aversion
entries.summary: When setting up the Arc browser, it'll remind you of all the effort that you’d have to put in if you don’t import settings from your existing browser.
entries.analysis: This is loss aversion, and there’s also some social proof to assure you that the majority of others do the same.

entries.title: Your investing breakdown
entries.summary: Robinhood uses circles to visualize how you’ve invested your money.
entries.analysis: This is purely a way of making your feed more interesting, and giving people a better way of quickly understanding where their money is split. It's not instead of a list—the full list is still below. But it's a nice way of visualising data.

entries.title: Updating to km and miles
entries.summary: The distance will be automatically updated to miles or kilometers, depending on which city you select.

entries.title: Personalising your experience
entries.summary: After collecting user preferences, Blinkist shows a loading screen indicating what’s happening behind the scenes.
entries.analysis: This gives the user a sense that there’s actual work being done to process the preferences that they just entered. i.e., The Labour Illusion.

entries.title: Dynamic search titles
entries.summary: When sorting results on Rent.com, the page title will dynamically change to reflect your intent (e.g., price high/low becomes "luxury").

entries.title: Dynamic trading hour themes
entries.summary: Robinhood has an option to dynamically change the app theme from light to dark based on stock market trading hours.
entries.analysis: This helps users immediately identify that the market is closed, and so any transactions they make will not be processed immediately. They are also warnings (etc), but this helps as a visual cue.

entries.title: You've saved precious seconds
entries.summary: By default, Loom automatically increases the playback speed of a video. After which, they'll show how many seconds you've saved by watching the video at the faster speed.
entries.analysis: This is both a great way to keep people engaged, and frame the benefit of Loom over say, a live video call.

entries.title: Chat to the founder
entries.summary: Instead of a generic ‘Contact Us’ menu, Refind humanizes the interface by having a ‘Chat with founder’ option in the menu.

entries.title: Warning: you're muted
entries.summary: If you’ve accidentally muted your microphone, Loom double-checks to make sure you want to record a video without audio.

entries.title: Animated NFT deletion
entries.summary: When deleting a collectable, the image literally animates dropping into a trash can.

entries.title: An interactive marble run
entries.summary: Scrolling on Trunk.io will trigger a ball to roll down a series of winding tracks, until it drops into the logo in the footer.
entries.analysis: This brilliantly encourages scrolling, and will be reducing their bounce rate. It's a fantastic idea to get people reading the content.

entries.title: Automatically trigger focus mode
entries.summary: A mindfulness app that provides a toggle to automatically activate 'Do Not Disturb' mode when you start a meditation.

entries.title: A toggle to be reminded
entries.summary: During sign up, users can opt in to be reminded before the trial ends—reassuring them that they won’t forget.
entries.analysis: It'd be worth an experiment, but I wouldn't be surprised if this was more effective than automatically sending a reminder. I think that the user taking an action to be reminded is more reassuring than a blanket "everyone always gets reminded" message. If I were to try and improve this though, I'd specify exactly when the reminder would be sent (e.g., "2 days before renewal"). The specificity would help.

entries.title: Set a reminder to finish this later
entries.summary: If you abandon a workout midway, Peleton will ask if you want to be reminded to finish it later.
entries.analysis: This is a user-initiated trigger, because they're choosing to be reminded about it later.

entries.title: Dynamic placeholder text
entries.summary: After reacting to a post, the comment placeholder text changes.

entries.title: Secret cancellation gift
entries.summary: If you attempt to delete Waterllama, you unlock a secret discount. To activate it, you tap the llama, which explodes into confetti to reveal the discount.

entries.title: Making charts interesting
entries.summary: The colour of your Apple card, and the charts, change dynamically to reflect your spending categories and debt level.

entries.title: Tinder for books
entries.summary: Blinkist’s onboarding has a Tinder-like swipe experience to refine and populate your initial library.
entries.analysis: That final step is the most important: giving the user a library of suggestions, previously read books, and next steps. Without this, the apps would feel empty and very impersonal.

entries.title: Cart or basket?
entries.summary: Amazon localizes the shopping experience by calling it ‘Cart’ in the US, and ‘basket’ in the UK.
entries.analysis: Apple does the same thing by calling it Trash if your region is set to US and Bin if it’s UK. This is a very low form of tribalism.

entries.title: Left-handed accessibility
entries.summary: Sofa lets you customize which side of the screen to place the ‘Add’ button, making the experience easier for left-handed people.

entries.title: Uber Eats drop off options
entries.summary: Uber Eats offer a range of drop-off options for apartment living so you can specify according to your building’s rules and comfort level.
entries.analysis: I also love the placeholder text, suggesting that you can literally say what you're wearing, and where you'll be standing.

entries.title: Paying your balance on Apple Card
entries.summary: The rotating ring on your Apple Card app changes colour as a cue that you’d be clear of the minimum payment to avoid interest.

entries.title: Dynamic learning roadmap
entries.summary: During onboarding, you'll set a target of when you want to level up, and then the roadmap will adjust how often you need to practice.
entries.analysis: This is really motivating because the user is setting their own goal. People might not know how often they should practice, or for how long, but they may have a specific time-based goal in mind. e.g., " I want to be okay at Spanish before my summer holiday ".

entries.title: Handling substitute groceries
entries.summary: When grocery shopping on Uber Eats, you can specify what to do if an item that you wanted isn’t available.
entries.analysis: This tackles one (possible) major point of churn with Uber Eats as a delivery service: what to do if items aren't available. Having the granularity to be able to add a back-up item would be especially useful for something like ice cream, where there are many flavours.

entries.title: Don't forget the dressing
entries.summary: Sweetgreen warns you if you attempt to order a salad without any dressing.

entries.title: Starbucks' real cookie
entries.summary: Agreeing to the Starbucks' cookie notice will show a prompt to order actual cookies.

entries.title: One more fact is waiting
entries.summary: Nibble adds some interactivity and mystery to the process of learning, with a 'slide to reveal' mechanism.
entries.analysis: Importantly, revealing the fact isn't necessary to continue—it's an optional extra. They're leveraging both the curiosity gap and variable rewards, by showing that something is available, and asking if you want to know what it is.

entries.title: Animated quiz master
entries.summary: As you answer questions, the Ahead ghost will animate to scribble on their clipboard.

entries.title: Image compression on LinkedIn
entries.summary: LinkedIn profile images are usually cropped, but they store the original in full quality, in case you want to make changes.

entries.title: Mobbin's 404 quiz
entries.summary: Mobbin's 404 page is an interactive quiz for designers, repurposing their archive of content.
entries.analysis: This is a brilliant example of tribalism. Designers use Mobbin as a reference library, and this easter egg is a bit like an in-joke.

entries.title: Take photos when it's light outside
entries.summary: While listing a car to sell, Car Wow will let you set a reminder to take photos later, but only when it's light outside.

entries.title: Emotional tap challenge
entries.summary: After completing lessons, you'll trigger this 'emotional tap' mini game. It compares your personal best score to the all time high score.
entries.analysis: Don't get me wrong, this has almost nothing to do with the actual learning value proposition. But, it's a nice mental break from the educational content, and is actually quite addictive. It motivates people to do one lesson (to then try and beat their score).

entries.title: Join with your camera off
entries.summary: When joining a meeting, Whereby are really clear that your camera is off, and not in a loading state.
entries.analysis: Yes, the icons exist, but the user may not know that red = off, rather than on. It's clearer this way, and has almost no downsides.

entries.title: Are you a student?
entries.summary: During onboarding, you're asked you if you’re a student—anticipating that a student's sleep patterns will be different. It'll then automatically apply a student discount.

entries.title: Swipe to confirm booking
entries.summary: When completing your booking, you can swipe, or draw the Hotel Tonight logo to confirm your booking.
entries.analysis: This both reinforces the brand and prevents accidental taps—particularly since all bookings are non-refundable.

entries.title: 5 second cancellation timer
entries.summary: After placing an order, the CTA will turn into a 5 second timer where you can cancel the process.

entries.title: Deleting? Tell us why
entries.summary: When deleting the app, the long-press shortcut menu on iOS asks for feedback before deletion.

entries.title: Clarifying check-in times
entries.summary: If you book a room after midnight for the same day, you'll be warned that the check-in time is in the afternoon, not immediately.
entries.analysis: i.e., if you booked a hotel at 1am, for that day, this clarifies that it's actually for the following night.

entries.title: This will not be your profile photo
entries.summary: When uploading an image for ID verification, you're reassured that it won't be used for your profile picture.

entries.title: Apple watch is syncing
entries.summary: The progress bar whilst setting up an Apple Watch is circular like a clock face. Plus, it reassures you that it’s safe to go do something else without interrupting the process.
entries.analysis: Even better, it gives you a quick link (the button) to learn more about how to use your Apple Watch while you wait.

entries.title: 65% of our users are just like you
entries.summary: When signing up to Babbel, you'll see a combination of social proof and reassurance, based on your current skill level.

entries.title: Your alarm is about to go off
entries.summary: Nest will warn you before it runs the routine alarm test. Tapping allows you to postpone or skip.

entries.title: Paste your booking reference
entries.summary: If you have a booking reference copied in your clipboard, you can tap once to attach it to your tracked flight.
entries.analysis: What's great here is that you don't need to right click, or open the field. Literally clicking on that bubble will paste and save it.

entries.title: Catching email typos
entries.summary: You'll be warned if you've made common typos (e.g., 'gmial' not 'gmail').

entries.title: Anti-churn notifications
entries.summary: If you attempt to cancel your subscription during the free trial, you'll be reassured that you'll get a notification 2 days before you're charged.
entries.analysis: To make this slightly better, they could remind the user about the benefits of the service on this screen.

entries.title: Experience Flighty Pro
entries.summary: To let you experience the benefits of Flighty Pro, they'll let you track a random flight with all the features unlocked.
entries.analysis: Even better, it'll auto-delete once the flight is over, so it doesn't get stuck in your travel passport.

entries.title: Snow is falling
entries.summary: Around Christmas, your daily transaction ring will occasionally snow.

entries.title: Amazon's christmas gift card
entries.summary: If an item will arrive after Christmas, Amazon will show a prompt to instead purchase a gift card.

entries.title: The fact grabber machine
entries.summary: To make the process of reading facts more interesting, Nibble have a minigame to grab the fact with a claw machine.
entries.analysis: If you try this, you'll notice that it's essentially just an animation to select a random ball—which likely isn't related to the fact. But, the slight illusion of interactivity is interesting, and can break up the monotony of reading fact after fact.

entries.title: Musical instrument audio
entries.summary: When Googling a musical instrument, you can play a clip of what it sounds like.

entries.title: Forced breaks
entries.summary: Ahead don't want you to binge all of the lessons in one day. Instead they force you to take breaks, and instead (hopefully) build more regular habits.
entries.analysis: This is super interesting, because it makes it clear to the user that this is an app they will have to use frequently, rather than all in one evening. It's a very literal form of intentional friction, and I imagine it boosts their retention and trial conversions.

entries.title: Unlock a treasure box
entries.summary: After hitting certain milestones you'll be prompted to open a treasure chest. These variable rewards are often "pro tips", which educate you about features.

entries.title: Time-sensitive iOS widgets
entries.summary: Throughout the day, the Duolingo iOS widget will display time-sensitive prompts, encouraging you to take an action.

entries.title: Dynamic sign-up content
entries.summary: Sketch will dynamically change the sign-up content depending on your role.

entries.title: You're halfway through, take a pause
entries.summary: When you're halfway through an Ahead lesson, you'll be forced to pause and reflect on why you're bothering to do it.
entries.analysis: This is fantastic for a few reasons: 1. Intentional Friction — the user is being forced to pause, and take a moment to understand where they are in the process. Without a specific step for this (i.e., if it were just a progress bar), it may be ignored. 2. Goal Gradient Effect — reminding the user that they're now halfway through. I imagine churn is very low from this point of the lesson. 3. Cognitive Drift — this might be a fantastic way of reducing drift, and re-focusing people on the task ahead. I imagine that the input quality is higher after this pop-up, than before it. (i.e., because it's reset their drift).

entries.title: Dynamic marketing screens
entries.summary: If you click through to Canny from someone else's board, the marketing page will dynamically include their board information.
entries.analysis: Instead of social proof being "100,000 people use Canny", they've gone the other way and narrowed down onto one specific user—hoping it's someone you know or trust.

entries.title: Variable success states
entries.summary: There are different 'answer correct' animations, depending on the difficulty of the question. These include varying strength of haptic feedback.
entries.analysis: When you first answer a "hard" question, you'll notice it as being extra celebratory, which is in itself motivating.

entries.title: Toggle the torch
entries.summary: When scanning a QR code, you can turn the torch on with a single tap.
entries.analysis: This isn't that useful when scanning digital QR codes, like screens, as the screen itself is the backlight. But, when scanning printed QR codes, that have no light source, this could be really helpful.

entries.title: Are you sure you shared it?
entries.summary: Ahead has an anonymous quiz to send to your friends. If you try to proceed without copying the link, you'll see this prompt.
entries.analysis: This is both to catch people who accidentally didn't copy the link, and a little nudge to go ahead and do it.

entries.title: Remember, this is personalised
entries.summary: The loading state for a meditation reminds you how personalised the experience is.
entries.analysis: The more you engage with the post-meditation questions, the more personalised it gets. This helps people feel like the app is theirs, and you may start to see the Endowment Effect kicking in.

entries.title: A literal signature to commit
entries.summary: When signing up to Ahead, you're asked to interact with a signature box to make a commitment to yourself, to use the app regularly.
entries.analysis: This is incredible because it leverages both User Initiated Triggers (making the commitment), but also Cognitive Dissonance (the user not wanting to go back on themselves). i.e., " I'm not the kind of person who goes back on contracts ". The signature box does nothing, to be clear. You can draw any squiggle you want. Instead it's just a medium for getting you to make a committment to yourself.

entries.title: Installing an iOS widget
entries.summary: Duolingo will prompt you to install an iOS widget, and then give you step by step instructions, all with beautiful animations.
entries.analysis: This would be better if they gave a better reason why you should install the widget. For example; " users with widgets installed are 25% more likely to keep to their daily streak goals ".

entries.title: 3D fitness badges
entries.summary: To make the Apple fitness badges feel that little bit more personalised, you can spin them in 3D to reveal your name engraved on the back.

entries.title: Filtering by workout length
entries.summary: When filtering Apple Fitness workouts by length, the options are listed inside abstract clock faces.

entries.title: Netflix's love animation
entries.summary: There's a really satisfying 'love this' animation, that bursts out of the container for a few seconds.
entries.analysis: Sure, it's not groundbreaking UX, but it's done really well.

entries.title: Personalised learning
entries.summary: After a meditation, you may be asked a few questions. You then see an icon, teaching you that every time you see it, your experience is getting more personalised.
entries.analysis: This is fantastic because as you invest more time into the app, you feel like it's getting slightlymore personalised. It might not be, but seeing that icon is a tiny reward, and helps motivate you to engage even more.

entries.title: Added context under a video
entries.summary: Under selected videos, YouTube will automatically display a banner with more context about the topic.
entries.analysis: This is likely to help avoid the spread of misinformation, but is also great UX. It doesn't interupt the video, but gives you a place to learn more.

entries.title: Navigating Kanban columns
entries.summary: If you have a Kanban board with many columns, you'll see a visualisation of the whole board, with a blue rectangle indicating what you're currently looking at.
entries.analysis: This solves the recurring issue of hidden content. The indicator displays all available columns, and a blue overlay indicates which columns are within the visible range.

entries.title: Dynamic sleep meditation settings
entries.summary: Balance will automatically shift to a dark mode view on the 'sleep' meditation tab.

entries.title: Learning how to use Twitch
entries.summary: In your Twitch creator dashboard, you'll see this onboarding prompt to learn about the definitions used.

entries.title: Quick, cover your eyes
entries.summary: The mule character will cover their eyes as you type your password.

entries.title: Ordering a round of drinks
entries.summary: When viewing the bill online, there's a quick action to order another round of drinks.

entries.title: Now slowly close your eyes
entries.summary: When starting a meditation, you'll see a prompt to close your eyes, and then the text will gently blur as the screen closes like an eyelid.
entries.analysis: It might sound tacky, but it's done really well.

entries.title: Monzo's free replacement card
entries.summary: When ordering a new card, you'll be notified that this one is free, whilst being reminded about the 'annual free replacements' scheme.
entries.analysis: One way I'd improve this is by actually saying how many free replacements you get. e.g., "we've removed delivery fees by using one of your free annual replacements (2 remaining after this)".

entries.title: Tracking your next goal
entries.summary: Every time you use your Radicant card, you'll progress towards an environmental reward—like planting a mangrove forest. The benefits are framed well, with plenty of context.
entries.analysis: Although this is, in concept, just a rewards scheme, it's executed really well.

entries.title: Ordering a new card
entries.summary: After ordering a new card on Revolut, you'll see this, which serves both as a reassuring reminder, and a tracking link.

entries.title: Mute Mother's Day deals
entries.summary: Zip give people an option to opt-out of Mother's and Father's Day notifications.

entries.title: Auto-mute suggestions
entries.summary: If you open TikTok and immediately turn the volume off, you'll see a prompt to change the settings, so it opens on mute by default.

entries.title: Smart delivery options
entries.summary: Packfleet will notify the recipient that their parcel is about to arrive, and give them a quick way to set preferences, like not ringing on the doorbell.

entries.title: Pausing ignored notifications
entries.summary: In an attempt to reduce churn, Duolingo will automatically pause sending notifications if you've been ignoring them for a while.

entries.title: Reinforced learning and recaps
entries.summary: After consuming content, Imprint will give you a recap with answerable questions. This incentivises people to pay more attention.

entries.title: Xbox's mobile streaks
entries.summary: After logging in, Xbox will show you your mobile streaks, with a clear breakdown of your increasing rewards.
entries.analysis: You may also notice that the CTA is "claim rewards", this immediately reminds the user why points are important, and what their incentive is.

entries.title: Dynamic day / night themes
entries.summary: Superlist will dynamically change the app imagery depending on your dark / light settings.

entries.title: Sharing YouTube's recap
entries.summary: When sharing YouTube's annual recap, it'll rephrase the quotes to be in the first person.

entries.title: Dynamic playbar colours
entries.summary: Spotify dynamically change the colour of the playbar to match the album artwork.

entries.title: Canva's trial breakdown
entries.summary: When trialing Canva, you'll see a clear timeline of what happens next, including a promise that you'll get a reminder 7 days before your trial ends.

entries.title: Incorrect recipient details
entries.summary: Monzo will warn you if the recipient's details don't match what you've entered, and they'll highlight exactly what the difference is, with this clear breakdown.

entries.title: Working towards a reward
entries.summary: Xbox will prompt you to select a reward (e.g., a gift card), and then use that to frame all of your daily challenges.
entries.analysis: The magic here is that you can switch your reward at any time, but the act of selecting something interesting to you is a motivation. It adds context to the progress bar. Every time you see a challenge, you can see your progress towards that specific reward.

entries.title: Creating onboarding anticipation
entries.summary: Deepstash leverage the Labour Illusion, and build anticipation, by showing a series of loading labels that sound really personalised.
entries.analysis: I imagine that some of this loading time is artifical. It exists to build anticipation and demonstrate "important and interesting stuff is happening".

entries.title: Optimised audio for your situation
entries.summary: When joining a meeting, you'll be asked if you're in the same room as another participant or not. This automatically configures your audio to avoid an echo.

entries.title: Multi-day challenges on Headway
entries.summary: Headway has bundled content into multi-day challenges, with a progression system.
entries.analysis: This is a combination of the Goal Gradient Effect (as you progress through the challenge), and User Initiated Triggers (as you commit to a specific challenge).

entries.title: Asana's flying unicorn
entries.summary: Occasionally, after completing a task, you'll see a purple unicorn fly across the screen.
entries.analysis: Although the unicorn is usually the same character, it's variable because you don't know when it'll happen. When it happens, it's a tiny moment of celebration.

entries.title: XP happy hour
entries.summary: Duolingo have an XP happy hour, to motivate you to start building a habit.

entries.title: Gmail's birthday surprise
entries.summary: On your birthday, Gmail will show you confetti and balloons when viewing your profile.

entries.title: Timestripe's trial onboarding
entries.summary: During your 7-day trial of Timestripe, your emails will include a clear progress bar showing how much longer you have left.

entries.title: WhatsApp groups in common
entries.summary: If an unknown contact messages you on WhatsApp, it'll display any groups that you have in common.

entries.title: Monzo's interest pots
entries.summary: Monzo will give you a breakdown of both the interest you've earned that month, and what you've earned in total.
entries.analysis: Showing "you've earned £15 this month" on its own might actually be demotivating. But, displaying the lifetime earnings is more likely to frame a sense of long term value.

entries.title: Framing the value of Rise
entries.summary: Rise Calendar will show you a real time breakdown of the benefits that your team has received.
entries.analysis: This is important, as it frames a silent benefit of using Rise, over say, Google Calendar.

entries.title: Dragging multiple photos
entries.summary: If you select multiple photos, they'll appear as a dynamic stack. You can then drag this stack, and each item will have its own slight motion.

entries.title: Taylor Swift's sparkle
entries.summary: On the iOS app, Spotify will show a star inside the playbar for Taylor Swift songs.

entries.title: Avoiding unnecessary messages
entries.summary: When you go to contact an Airbnb host, you'll see a clear bullet point list of the most common answers. This is dynamic, based on that specific listing.

entries.title: Your first Airbnb booking
entries.summary: When listing an Airbnb to host, you can choose to only allow experienced guests to take the first reservation.

entries.title: Revolut's payment timeline
entries.summary: After making an international transfer, Revolut will show you a timeline, which both explains the process, and gives an estimation of completion time.
entries.analysis: The other major benefit here is that it gives you a place to actually go and check on the status of this payment. i.e., rather than it just saying "pending" in a feed.

entries.title: Monzo raising payment limits
entries.summary: If you request to temporarily increase your daily transfer limits, the confirmation will include a clear breakdown of the payment you can now make, and when this limit will revert back to normal.

entries.title: There's a better way to share this
entries.summary: If you take a screenshot, you'll see an onboarding pop-up explaining how to more efficiently share and save content.

entries.title: Unsending iMessages
entries.summary: When unsending an iMessage, your chat bubble will explode into tiny pieces and disappear.

entries.title: Calendar 'Focus Guard'
entries.summary: Rise's calendar has a 'focus guard' mode, which automatically blocks off focus time if your calendar gets too busy.
entries.analysis: What takes this beyond just "a feature", and into great UX, is how hands-off it is. It's a form of reactive onboarding, because the notification email explains how the feature works, and gives you a quick way to modify the settings.

entries.title: Achievement unlocked
entries.summary: As you hit key Headway milestones (such as a reading target), you'll unlock notifications, which appear inline.

entries.title: Discord's dynamic skip button
entries.summary: When creating a Discord server, you're asked to invite people. The link in the top right will be dynamic, based on if you've already shared the link or not.
entries.analysis: In other words, this is avoiding a situation where you've already shared the link, but now have to click on a button that says "skip". " I don't want to skip it, I want to do it ". Done confirms to the user that it's a positive action.

entries.title: Outlook's celebration
entries.summary: If you receive an email with the word "congratulations", in Outlook, you'll see confetti when you hover over it.

entries.title: Time your notifications
entries.summary: Uptime will let you choose when to receive your 'streak reminder' notifications, based on when you're most likely to take an action.

entries.title: Headway's user-initiated triggers
entries.summary: During onboarding, Headway will ask you to commit to a learning schedule, and then frame that effort in terms of how likely you are to reach your goals.
entries.analysis: Yes, this is very similar to Duolingo. But I imagine that we're seeing this pattern more often because it's so effective.

entries.title: Your direct debit is higher than usual
entries.summary: Monzo will notify you if the value of a direct debit has changed.

entries.title: Displaying preferred meeting times
entries.summary: When sending your availability to someone else, you can mark preferred times.

entries.title: Spotify Wrapped's video messages
entries.summary: Your Spotify wrapped may include a video from your favourite artist, thanking you for being a super fan.
entries.analysis: This helps to incubate a sense of tribalism between you (the listener) and Spotify's content (the artists).

entries.title: Apple's sleep schedule
entries.summary: When setting an alarm, you'll be able to see if the amount of sleep you'll get meets your nightly sleep goals.

entries.title: Timed custom statuses
entries.summary: When you set a custom status on Discord, you're asked if you'd like it to auto-reset. This stops people having to remember to undo it later.

entries.title: Deleting Duolingo
entries.summary: In an attempt to stop you deleting the app, there's a prompt to practice your weak skills. This reminds the user about the value of Duolingo, and gets them straight back into a lesson.

entries.title: Daily commitment to reading
entries.summary: During onboarding, Headway will ask you to commit to a daily reading target. They'll then display how many books this is equivalent to, over the course of a month.
entries.analysis: This is both framing (the number of books per month), and user-initiated triggers, because you're creating the target yourself.

entries.title: Uptime's memory quiz
entries.summary: Uptime will show you saved 'hacks' (essentially mini lessons from books or courses), and ask you if you remembered it. This impacts how frequently you're shown that specific 'hack'.

entries.title: Magically arranging your photos
entries.summary: When a host uploads photos, Airbnb will automatically reorder them based on (presumably the most interesting).
entries.analysis: What's also great here is the spinner label. It makes the user start anticipating that something great is about to happen.

entries.title: Localised emergency support
entries.summary: Airbnb support will use your IP to dynamically provide information on how to contact emergency services.

entries.title: Stripe's export notification
entries.summary: While Stripe is compiling data for an export, you're shown an option to get notified via email when it's ready.

entries.title: Helping hosts set pricing
entries.summary: To reduce the friction of hosts calculating a "fair" listing price, Airbnb will suggest a price based on similar bookings, including an interactive map of local prices.

entries.title: It's pronounced "why-nab"
entries.summary: Hovering over their logo will tilt it slightly, and show you how their name is pronounced.
entries.analysis: This is really subtly leveraging tribalism, because it's like letting you in on the "truth" behind a logo. You now feel slightly more connected to the brand.

entries.title: Comparing items while shopping
entries.summary: Humane will automatically cycle the images of all visible items at the same time (rather than just the product you're interacting with), so you can easily compare different views.

entries.title: Apple's autocorrect
entries.summary: If your iPhone autocorrects a word, it'll show a backwards arrow, along with any other suggestions, so you can easily revert back to the original spelling.

entries.title: Google Maps fuel usage
entries.summary: When there are multiple routes, you are shown approximate fuel savings based on your engine type.

entries.title: Dynamic sunlight preview
entries.summary: When browsing an apartment, you can see the position of the sun at different times throughout the day.

entries.title: This was in your basket
entries.summary: Etsy will let you know if an item that was previously in your basket (but has since been removed) is on sale.

entries.title: Snapchat's social proof
entries.summary: When signing up to Snapchat, you're shown how many of your phone contacts have been active within the last month.

entries.title: Duolingo's listening exercises
entries.summary: If you are shown a listening exercise, but your phone's volume is too low to hear, you'll see a prompt.

entries.title: Waze's social proof
entries.summary: By default, you'll see other Waze users on a map in (near) realtime. This is immediate social proof.

entries.title: Remind you before you leave
entries.summary: Waze will send you a notification to your phone when you need to leave, if you start navigating on your desktop.

entries.title: We've frozen your streaks
entries.summary: Duolingo automatically apply a 2-day "streak freeze", so if you churn quickly, you don't lose your progress. They'll then email you reminders to encourage you to come back.
entries.analysis: This is a clever combination of both loss aversion (not wanting to lose your streaks) and the goal gradient effect (feeling more motivated as you reach the "7-day streak"). To tie it up nicely, they've got an animated bird in the email, literally jumping and straining to reach the CTA.

entries.title: Cycling elevation in Google Maps
entries.summary: Google Maps will show a "progress bar" of elevation, when plotting a cycling route. You can then click and see on the map exactly where the hills are.

entries.title: Instagram's dynamic dock
entries.summary: On iOS, if you click to share an image to Instagram, you'll see prompts with the different types of share, with dynamic renders of how your image would look in-situ.

entries.title: Clear accessibility features
entries.summary: Airbnb will clear list (and verify) accessibility features of a home, including photos and descriptions (e.g., of step-free access).

entries.title: Framing the CO2 equivalent
entries.summary: Ecologi (a climate action platform) will frame your positive environmental impact in a number of dynamic ways.

entries.title: Github's tribalism
entries.summary: GitHub ooze personality and tribalism by positioning their sign-up as an 'adventure'. This includes objectively difficult input methods, like answering with 'N' or 'Y'.

entries.title: Requesting feedback when deleting app
entries.summary: If you long tap to delete the AsaTools iOS app, there's a "quick feature" and a prompt to give feedback before deleting it.

entries.title: Social proof while loading
entries.summary: Duolingo don't miss a chance to show you social proof. When loading a lesson, you'll see a random statistic like this.
entries.analysis: Plus, there's a subtle element of the labour illusion at play here. Reading that the course is building , makes it feel like something is being prepared for me, or meaningful amount of work is happening behind the scenes.

entries.title: Automatically increasing the difficulty
entries.summary: If you get too many answers correct in a row, Duolingo will automatically increase the difficulty. This helps keep the challenge high enough to be interesting, which is motivating.
entries.analysis: This subtly leverages the Pygmalion Effect, because it broadcasts to the user that they're is capable of moving up a level.

entries.title: You're nearly getting free shipping
entries.summary: If a seller on Shop offers free delivery, you'll see a progress bar visualising how close you are to getting it.

entries.title: Animating the basket
entries.summary: Adding an item into your basket will show an animation of that specific item falling into the menu tab.
entries.analysis: This acts as non-verbal onboarding, because it literally shows you where your basket is.

entries.title: This store policy boosts conversions
entries.summary: Stripe display badges (with more context) next to store settings that are very likely to increase your conversion rates.
entries.analysis: The key here is that Stripe is helping their customers be more successful with their product. It's essentially a form of onboarding.

entries.title: Klarna's time to buy
entries.summary: The Klarna iOS app will show you historic prices, visualising when it's a good time to buy an item.
entries.analysis: Other services like this exist, but what makes this so effective, is how simple it is. Notice that they've also not used green, amber and red (which would be a fairly obvious choice), as that'd likely put people off buying items that aren't cheaper than average.

entries.title: Etsy's dynamic basket CTA
entries.summary: After adding an item to your basket, the label in that CTA changes. If you click it again, you'll be taken to your basket.
entries.analysis: This has a subtle benefit of stopping people adding the same item to their basket twice.

entries.title: Double or nothing
entries.summary: Duolingo have a "double or nothing" mechanism, which is likely to really motivate users for a specific time period.
entries.analysis: This hits both loss aversion (i.e., not wanting to lose double), and gamification by almost literally being gambling. It's not gambling, because you're in full control of if you hit your streak or not, but it has that feel to it.

entries.title: Workaround for items without reviews
entries.summary: If a specific item has no reviews, Etsy will instead show that seller's store reviews, with this badge.

entries.title: Gamification to maintain focus
entries.summary: How do you add a sense of importance when engaging with a task? Duolingo do it with a "lives" mechanic, which encourages people to pay attention and stay focused.
entries.analysis: If you run out of lives, it's easy to get more (without paying money), you simply need to answer questions correctly. In other words, it's a mechanism to reward focus, and punish people clicking anything without thinking.

entries.title: Approximate seating view
entries.summary: For some events, StubHub will show an approximate view (by 'zone') of your selected seats.
entries.analysis: What's important here is that it contextualises the ticket that you're about to buy, in an industry where zoned pricing is really common. Unless you know the venue well, you may not have any real concept of how far away the seats are, or what your experience will be like. i.e., it's harder to justify a ticket that's twice the price, without really understanding what the differences will be.

entries.title: The Guardian's perception of value
entries.summary: The Guardian will show you how many free articles you've read, to frame the value of supporting their service.

entries.title: Apple's band size
entries.summary: When configuring an Apple Watch in the store, if you select a strap size without first opening the fitting guide, you'll see this prompt.

entries.title: Slide, release and go hands-free
entries.summary: While filming, if you slide your thumb to the left and release, you can go 'hands-free' and record without having to hold down the button.

entries.title: YouTube's moments
entries.summary: YouTube will take out "moments" of a video, and for step-by-step guides, show chapters inside the feed. Plus, the colour is dynamic based on the video thumbnail.

entries.title: Tesla's charging breakdown
entries.summary: Tesla will show you all of your charging analytics, with predicted costs. This frames the value of your electric car, and reminds the user how much they're saving in alternative fuels.

entries.title: Custom notification timers
entries.summary: When setting a custom notification timer, you'll turn this satisfying dial.

entries.title: Amie's emoji burst
entries.summary: When marking a task as complete in Amie, you'll see a random emoji, or two, pop out in celebration. Very occasionally, they'll flood the screen.
entries.analysis: This is a classic variable reward . Not knowing what you're going to see next adds a bit of unpredictability, that can help motivate people to take action.

entries.title: Sending audio to the bin
entries.summary: If you cancel an audio message, the icon will flip around, and be dropped into a bin.

entries.title: Returning TikTok users
entries.summary: If you've previously logged into TikTok on a device, you'll see a badge on one of the log in options. This helps remind you which (of the many) social logins you've used.

entries.title: Apple store's sticky close buttons
entries.summary: There are a lot of modals on the Apple website. To help make navigation easier, the close 'X' is sticky in the top right of modals.

entries.title: Superlist's random chimes
entries.summary: Whenever you mark an item as 'done' on Superlist, you'll hear a random chime noise as it ticks the item off. It sounds distracting and annoying, but the variability in gentle chimes feels satisfying and addictive.

entries.title: "Just watched" on TikTok
entries.summary: When going from a TikTok in the feed, into someone's profile, the video you just watched will be darkened and labelled.

entries.title: Privacy settings for notifications
entries.summary: When setting up WhatsApp for Mac, you'll be asked how you want notifications to appear. This includes a dynamic preview of a notification (i.e., visually demonstrating what the options actually do).

entries.title: Their local time is 11.36
entries.summary: After sending a message to a host, you'll be shown what their local time is. This frames a realistic expectation for when you can expect a response.
entries.analysis: This is particularly relevant for Airbnb, because you may be travelling internationally, and not consider the time zone difference.

entries.title: Apple Mail's follow-up reminders
entries.summary: If you've sent an email that hasn't been replied to in 3 days, Apple Mail will push it to the top of your inbox, with a prompt to follow-up. You can turn this off too.

entries.title: Hotspotting an iPhone on low battery
entries.summary: If you're hotspotting an iPhone (for WiFi), and that device drops below 20% power, you'll see a notification on your laptop, with a prompt to disconnect.

entries.title: Onboarding on time
entries.summary: If you opt to download a Pitch presentation as a PDF, you'll see an onboarding slide outlining the benefits of instead sharing it as a link.

entries.title: Transfering a call into a Tesla
entries.summary: When taking a call on a Bluetooth-connected phone, Tesla will show a prompt asking if you want to transfer it to the car. This allows you to use the cars microphone and speakers.

entries.title: Asana reminding you of the benefits
entries.summary: Asana will notify you that your trial is about to end, and offer to send an email explaining the benefits of a premium tier.
entries.analysis: This is fantastic, because it allows the user to be lazy in the moment (i.e., "yeah, send me some info"), and then make a more thoughtful decision later.

entries.title: Uploading and transcoding a video
entries.summary: When uploading a video using the 'Background Video' component on Webflow, it reminds you that you can keep designing while the uploading and transcoding are ongoing.

entries.title: How Strava maintains your privacy
entries.summary: On Strava, you can hide the start and end parts of your route (e.g., your house), when you share your workout.

entries.title: Google's dynamic image search
entries.summary: When looking at an image on Google, you can click the 'image search' icon, drag the rectangle over anything, and it will (if possible) tell you what it's looking at.

entries.title: Midjourney's cancellation transparency
entries.summary: In certain circumstances, when you cancel your Midjourney account, you'll be offered two options. This may include receiving a refund on your previous payment.
entries.analysis: This is transparency at its finest.

entries.title: Yoti's 'ready' CTA
entries.summary: While taking a picture of your face with Yoti, the CTA will only become clickable once your face is roughly inside the oval. This will be helping to minimise errors.

entries.title: Live daily customer count
entries.summary: When reactivating your HelloFresh account, they'll display a "live" count of their new daily customers in the sidebar.

entries.title: Dominos' shake to shuffle
entries.summary: Dominos have a 'slot machine' style feed, where you can physically shake your phone to cycle through the sides.

entries.title: Onboarding Figma dev mode
entries.summary: Figma has two modes; one for designers, and one for developers. When sharing a link from Figma Dev mode, you'll see confirmation that the recipient will open the link inside dev mode too.

entries.title: Google Nest's QR code
entries.summary: When setting up a Nest camera, you'll see an animation of the QR sticker being removed from the lens, and being placed in the "quick start" guide. It also gives context as to why this step can save you time in the future.

entries.title: Apple's 'zoomed' progress bar
entries.summary: When listening to a podcast, Apple will automatically enlarge the progress bar to help you be a bit more accurate when scrubbing through the track.
entries.analysis: This is far more valuable in a podcast, than on a 3 minute song, because the track may be hours long. The progress bar is small and hard to accurately scrub through. i.e., one tiny movement may skip 5 minutes of content. That's why enlarging it gives you a bit more (needed) accuracy.

entries.title: Sequential lazy loading
entries.summary: YouTube will load the key metadata before the images. This subtle difference means that on slow internet connections, you can still choose a video without waiting for the high-resolution previews.

entries.title: Pausing audio exercises
entries.summary: If Duolingo show you a listening exercise, you can click an "I can't listen to right" button, to pause that type of question for 15 minutes.

entries.title: Sainsbury's ingredient bundle
entries.summary: Sainsbury's will show you recipes, with the ability to add all of the items to your basket in a single click.

entries.title: ChatGPT's privacy context
entries.summary: When using another creators GPT, you'll see clear context that they cannot see your chats.

entries.title: Focusing your attention on deleted items
entries.summary: When deleting messages on the T-Mobile app, they inverse the focus (making everything slightly transparent), and highlight any items that you've selected to delete. This forces you to pay more attention to what you're about to discard forever.

entries.title: Automatically deleting OTP codes
entries.summary: Apple can auto-delete any OTPs (one time password) that you receive, after you've used them, to keep your inbox clear.

entries.title: Tesla's storm warning
entries.summary: If a storm is forecast in your area, Tesla will remind you that powerwall is still able to provide back-up power.
entries.analysis: This is fantastic, because it's highlighting an often silent benefit of their powerwall. i.e., you might never actually use the "back-up power" feature, but this reminds you that you're protected.

entries.title: Handoff maps to mobile
entries.summary: You can send Google Maps routes from your desktop to your mobile phone, which then appear as a notification.

entries.title: Google's contributor badges
entries.summary: Google incentivise you to contribute more (i.e., add reviews or answer questions about local places) with badges and awards on your public profile.
entries.analysis: The best thing about this, and why it's likely so effective, is that you're given a clear list of actions to level up. And, they're not all grinding the same action (i.e., they're not all to upload more photos). This keeps the challenge variable .

entries.title: Instagram's editing onboarding
entries.summary: When starting an Instagram conversation, you'll be taught how to edit messages (it's really great progressive disclosure).

entries.title: Fishing inside Google
