entries.summary: If you make a search without any results on Google, you'll see an animation of this monster fishing. If you click on them, they'll catch an assortment of items (tin cans, boots, etc).

entries.title: Audible listening badges
entries.summary: Audible has 'listening achievements', badges and a dashboard to view all of your analytics.
entries.analysis: The magic here isn't only that it encourages you to reach another level , it's that it provides a way to see the silent benefits of your subscription. i.e., it reminds you how much you've used it, and therefore, how much value you've got from it.

entries.title: Cineworld's error messages
entries.summary: If you run into an error while booking cinema tickets, you'll see a themed movie quote.
entries.analysis: To be clear, I think this execution is poor. The styling is off, and it's awkward to read. But conceptually this is a decent way of reducing some of the frustration of hitting an error.

entries.title: Progress bar + slider count
entries.summary: Apple TV have combined a slider count (i.e., to cycle through) with a progress indicator of the video that's currently playing.

entries.title: Apple Maps' alternative routes
entries.summary: Apple Maps will suggest alternative routes with clear labels, such as options without stairs, fewer hills or fewer turns.

entries.title: Turning on the air con
entries.summary: When the air con is turned on, you'll see an animation of air blowing into the cockpit.

entries.title: Avoiding duplicate payments
entries.summary: With Capital One, if you have an automatic payment scheduled and try to make another (regular) payment, it’ll ask you if you would like to still keep your automatic payment or skip it.

entries.title: Stay an extra night for free
entries.summary: Airbnb will prompt you to add an extra night for free (in a single click), if there's no additional charge.

entries.title: Highlighting the like button
entries.summary: If someone mentions " Like this " in a YouTube video, the 'like' button below will be highlighted with a special effect. This is to encourage feature usage.

entries.title: You don't need to delete everything
entries.summary: If you go to restart (and delete) a Loom recording, you'll see context that you can edit and trim clips at the next step. In other words, if it's just one mistake, you don't need to re-record the entire video.

entries.title: Loom's audio visualiser
entries.summary: Before creating a loom, the button to change your input source also shows a sound visualiser. i.e., you can see that the input source is working.

entries.title: Redeeming variable rewards
entries.summary: You need to actively redeem rewards on Duolingo (which then gives you a random number of gems). The action of redeeming makes this more satisfying than if it automatically unlocked.

entries.title: Are you trying to share this?
entries.summary: If you screenshot an item on Etsy, they'll automatically show you a prompt to then share a link to the item.

entries.title: Flip to hide your balances
entries.summary: Physically flipping your phone over, with the Revolut app open, will hide all of your balances.

entries.title: Collecting data at the right time
entries.summary: If your location tracking is turned on, Google will send you a push notification when you arrive at certain locations, asking you specific questions.

entries.title: Automatic language settings in Google Docs
entries.summary: If Google Docs is suggesting a typo based on a British or American spelling, it'll give you a one-click way to change your language settings.

entries.title: Directing using landmarks
entries.summary: In rural places, like India, it's common to have roads without names. To get around this, Google Maps will add context to their instructions, by including local landmarks, businesses or places.

entries.title: Spotify's goodbye playlist
entries.summary: When you leave Spotify Premium, they'll show you a playlist full of sad songs (the song names say: " If you leave us now, you'll take away the biggest part of us ").

entries.title: Screen sharing during FaceTime
entries.summary: Whilst on a Facetime call, you can share your iPhone screen to the other person.

entries.title: FaceID reminder (great timing)
entries.summary: When making a payment on Monzo, without FaceID turned on, you'll see a reminder to turn it on.
entries.analysis: What's fantastic here, is that this form of progressive disclosure is exactly the right time to demonstrate the benefits of FaceID. Simple, yet effective.

entries.title: Google Meet's infinity mirror
entries.summary: Google Meets warns you that sharing your entire window (i.e., the browser where Google Meets is), will create an infinity mirror.

entries.title: Etsy's loyal customers
entries.summary: For a bit of awesome social proof, Etsy will display badges when a seller has returning customers.

entries.title: Argos' persuasive prompts
entries.summary: Argos combines both scarcity and social proof to motivate you to place an order.

entries.title: Social proof on login
entries.summary: Every time you login to Supabase, you'll see a random (they cycle) testimonial from their customers.
entries.analysis: You may think that once the user has created their account, social proof isn't needed any more. But, it helps set an impression every single time you log in , which could decrease churn, and increase the perceived value of the product.

entries.title: GoDaddy's loading message
entries.summary: When searching for a domain, GoDaddy will show you a loading state that emphasises the potential of what a new name could mean. It's not just "finding domains", it's more powerful than that.

entries.title: Medium's dynamic social proof
entries.summary: Medium will display an assortment of people (job titles and company info), on their premium sign-up prompt. This social proof is dynamic based on the article that you're reading. i.e., if you're reading an article about web development, you'll see roles like this.
entries.analysis: The more specific you can be with social proof, the better. So, without explaining who these people really are, or what they do, their titles (and company) feel credible.

entries.title: Quick action to copy address
entries.summary: eBay make it easy to copy someone's address with 'click to copy' prompts.

entries.title: Searching 'halloween' on Google
entries.summary: When searching for 'halloween' on Google, you'll see a bunch of (not so) spooky creatures float across the screen. (Note, this may only work when it's actually halloween).

entries.title: Revolut's festive welcome
entries.summary: During festive periods (halloween shown), Revolut will show special login icons.

entries.title: Monzo's spooky payday interaction
entries.summary: When opting to receive your salary a day early with Monzo, you usually see an animation to drag a dollar down into a wallet. For Halloween, they were made into a sweet and a pumpkin.

entries.title: Did you leave anything in the car?
entries.summary: After riding in a Bolt, you'll be asked to rate your driver, and be given a clear prompt to contact them if you left anything in the car.
entries.analysis: This is a 'visible lifeboat', because the user will feel reassured even if they haven't left anything in the car. i.e., " ah, if I ever do leave anything in a Bolt, I can always get it back easily ".

entries.title: Slack's dynamic placeholder
entries.summary: The placeholder text in Slack is dynamic depending on where (or to who) you're sending a message. When messaging yourself, it says " jot something down ".

entries.title: Items flying into your basket
entries.summary: On the Mr Beast store, to demonstrate that your item has been sucessfully added to your basket, you'll see a tiny image fly from the button , into the basket icon in the header .

entries.title: There's nothing attached
entries.summary: Superhuman warns you if you’ve mentioned that there’s an attachment to an email, but nothing is attached.

entries.title: Personalising the spinner
entries.summary: Citymapper have personalised the 'loading' spinner, by labelling it "thinking".

entries.title: Duolingo lesson streaks
entries.summary: Duolingo effectively gamifies the process of completing lessons, by keeping 'daily streaks'. The more you invest in the streak, the less likely you'll want to skip a day.
entries.analysis: Also, it subtly uses the Fresh Start Effect, by not dwelling on the old streak, but instead only focusing on your fresh streak.

entries.title: Jumping for joy, literally
entries.summary: After getting every question right on Duolingo, the character will perform some celebratory animation.
entries.analysis: They're just random enough to offer some kind of variable reward. You don't quite know what the animation will be, and it's addictive to watch them get so happy.

entries.title: Reminder before their subscription renews
entries.summary: If you go to cancel your Duolingo subscription, you'll see this message. It reassures the user that they'll be reminded 2 days before the trial ends.
entries.analysis: This leverages two important pieces of psychology. The Law of Minimal Effort — people don't have to make the decision today . They can postpone it until later, and not fear that they'll accidentally forget. Visible Lifeboats — it's reassuring to know that Duolingo will notify you (so you don't forget). You may not have known that they do this. Also, notice how the default action is to continue with your trial. It's really convincing.

entries.title: Powerwall usage animation
entries.summary: To help you quickly understand how (and if) power is being used, Tesla's powerwall shows an animation of yellow and green pulses moving throughout your home.

entries.title: Annual billing toggle in Stripe's checkout
entries.summary: Stripe will show you multiple billing cycles in a single checkout, switchable with a single toggle.

entries.title: Hiding annoying GIFs on Slack
entries.summary: On Slack, you can hide GIFs (without deleting the message), to remove the distraction. There's also a setting to hide them by default.

entries.title: Reminding users about the value
entries.summary: Every time you upload a Loom, you'll see a notification which reminds you about the value of their service.
entries.analysis: This is really smart, because it'd be easy to forget that the value of Loom is in reducing the number of meetings. It's a silent benefit, and this helps frame the value every time.

entries.title: Starting a trial without a credit card
entries.summary: If you're on the Asana free plan, and you click to create a project type that is only available for premium members, you'll see a button to start a trial without a credit card.

entries.title: Turn by turn directions
entries.summary: Google will show interactive street views, for every turn on a route.

entries.title: Dynamic backgrounds
entries.summary: Trello will automatically add backgrounds to content cards, based on the primary image colour. This helps keep the pages dynamic, and not really empty.

entries.title: Reminding the user about offers
entries.summary: John Lewis will remind the customer about key offers inside the checkout.
entries.analysis: If you've seen an offer previously, but you cannot find it again when you're trying to make a purchase, you may be less likely to continue with that order. That incentivises people to abandon their carts, by cancelling, and trying to re-trigger the offer. This is smart, because it enoucrages people to stay in the checkout and complete their order.

entries.title: Peloton's audio optionality
entries.summary: During a workout, Peleton has an option to focus on either the trainer’s voice, or the music, or a mix of both.

entries.title: Reminding the user about coupon codes
entries.summary: Stripe will save previously entered coupon codes (but that you've removed), so you can add them back in with a single click.

entries.title: In-call upgrading
entries.summary: On the Google Meets free plan, you'll get a 15 minute warning that your call will end, and a prompt to start a 14-day trial, without leaving or ending the call.

entries.title: Try Arc for a week
entries.summary: After downloading Arc, you'll be asked if you want to set it as your default browser. But what's smart here, is that they offer a lower commitment option of "try for one week".
entries.analysis: Functionally , this appears to be identical to setting Arc as your default browser—just with the added reassurance of a reminder. Objectively, a user would already understand that they can re-set to their previous default browser at any time. But the idea of having to make a second decision (to go back to it) is daunting. And that’s why it’s so effective; it lowers the commitment required to take an action in the first place. This is a great UX trick to get people to try new habits.

entries.title: Gamifying answering emails
entries.summary: Superhuman have gamified the process of answering emails ("hitting inbox 0") by recording streaks, with badges you can share.

entries.title: Visualizing habits
entries.summary: Visualizehabit.com is a habit tracker, with a shuffable habit in their hero. It demonstrates both common habits, and frames them well. i.e., "walk 10 minutes a day, and in a year you'll have walked 277km".

entries.title: Animating Pokemon cards
entries.summary: When browsing the Pokemon card library on the official website, clicking on each card will show a rotating 3D card, that settles as a modal.

entries.title: Pricing per meal
entries.summary: HelloFresh will frame the cost of their mealkits 'per serving' in the checkout.
entries.analysis: The inherent problem for HelloFresh, is that the ingredient cost for home-cooked food is difficult to price. They're subject to the Recency Bias of the last few food shops. This framing allows the customer to try and reverse benchmark the cost of homecooked meals. i.e., can you produce a tasty home cooked meal for £1.93 per serving? It feels like better value now, right?

entries.title: Take a break, you're doing great
entries.summary: Duolingo have intentionally added friction into each lesson, to say how great you're doing when you hit a streak.

entries.title: Inline date suggestions
entries.summary: When typing a date into an email, you can change it with a single click (or keyboard shortcut).

entries.title: Pirate mode
entries.summary: Trello has a 'pirate mode', where old tasks (that pressumably haven't been completed), start visually aging and curling at the sides.
entries.analysis: This is a playful way of telling the user that they're failing at keeping on top of their to-do list. It helps promote healthy habits, without being too punishing.

entries.title: Messaging on eBay
entries.summary: To help avoid scams, eBay will notify you if someone who has won your item (i.e., bought), but not paid yet, messages you.

entries.title: This email is loading
entries.summary: If you try to reply to an email on Apple Mail, before it's finished downloading, you'll see this prompt.

entries.title: Google Maps utilising local business data
entries.summary: If you try and navigate to a business that's closed (or will be closed) on Google Maps, you'll see this prompt before you set off.

entries.title: Size sticker for large items
entries.summary: Smyths toy store will show a label on large items that can fit in a regular sized car (i.e., for pick-up).

entries.title: Dynamic insurance reminder
entries.summary: Returning Compare the Market users may see a dynamic image, showing when their car insurance needs to be renewed.
entries.analysis: Another thing they've done really well here is put in brackets how many days away that is. This is effective because it's digestible without the need for calculating dates and days. It's a small touch, but can make deciding whether or not to take action , much easier.

entries.title: Deleting an event on Cron
entries.summary: Before deleting an event, you're given context that it won't be deleted for everyone else.
entries.analysis: Also what's fantastic here is that you're given a choice of cancelling the event without sending a notification (i.e., a silent cancel).

entries.title: Renaming download titles
entries.summary: Arc has a feature that automatically renames downloaded files, to remove some of the 'naming junk'.

entries.title: Avoiding missed parcels
entries.summary: When printing a postage label, eBay will warn you if it thinks that the address is entered slightly wrong, and give you a one-click way of fixing it.

entries.title: Clean up on isle 7
entries.summary: After setting up your Roomba for the first time, you'll see an animation where confetti falls to the floor, and then the Roomba will drive around cleaning it up.

entries.title: That payment only took 4 seconds
entries.summary: Wise subtly encourages the user to reflect on a benefit they've received, (speed of a transfer) in the confirmation email.
entries.analysis: If the fees were transparent, and the recepient received their money, it's easy to overlook the fact that the transfer was almost instant. I asked Wise, and I'm told that this isn't an arbitary number, but is actually how fast that specific transfer was.

entries.title: Missing attachment prompt
entries.summary: If you reference an attached file in an email, but don't attach one, you'll see this warning when hit send.

entries.title: Personalised notifications
entries.summary: When you follow a player or team (to receive notifications), you'll get a breakdown of every possible notification, and a quick way to toggle them on or off.

entries.title: Sir, this is Wendy's
entries.summary: Wendy's 404 page has an interactive game, very similar to Donkey Kong, where you have to stack burgers.

entries.title: Learn to code games
entries.summary: Codecademy's 404 page has a mini game, very similar to the popular Google Chrome one. But they also have a CTA to learn how to build games, via a Codecademy course.

entries.title: Google search easter egg
entries.summary: When searching for 'Bletchley Park' (the historic British landmark, known for code-breaking during WWII), Google will display an animation of code being solved.

entries.title: iPhone's suspend charging when wet
entries.summary: If you plug a wet iPhone in to charge, it may be automatically blocked from charging.

entries.title: Clear "this is a test" context
entries.summary: When sending a test alert, the email is clearly marked as a test (to reduce anxiety). Then, acknowledging the test alert acts as onboarding, because it tells you what would have happened during a real one.

entries.title: Context about muting conversations
entries.summary: Before muting a chat on WhatsApp, you'll be given really great context about what other participants can see.
entries.analysis: It's important that this context is shown before the user mutes notifications. As an example, people might be nervous about muting a work WhatsApp group for a week while they go on holiday.

entries.title: Custom emojis
entries.summary: To help a Slack channel feel more like yours, they'll let you create custom emojis. This creates community 'in-jokes', which adds to the experience.

entries.title: Wolt's easter egg
entries.summary: Wolt (the food delivery service) has a hidden game, where you tap the screen as fast as you can, while shopping items pour out of it.

entries.title: Weather-based tipping prompt
entries.summary: Uber will prompt you to tip more if it's raining, with a default amount of 20% pre-selected (higher than normal).
entries.analysis: Notice that they've got an illustration of a determind-looking delivery rider, climbing up steps and battling against the rain to get to you. Also, there are intentionally five options, and the selected default option (20%) is the second in the list Abstract away the numbers for a moment—choosing the second item in a list of five feels reasonable. It's not the cheapest option, but the second cheapest . This is framing.

entries.title: Amazon's profile character will wave
entries.summary: Occasionally when logging into Amazon you'll see your profile character wave at you.

entries.title: You're a champion
entries.summary: Answering your emails is an inherently single-player experience, but Superhuman have gamified the process of hitting 'inbox zero', by comparing your performance to their userbase.

entries.title: Timezone awareness on Slack
entries.summary: If you're messaging someone out of office hours, it'll display their timezone.

entries.title: We have not notified your boss
entries.summary: When sending yourself a test notification, you'll be reassured that your colleagues have not been messaged.
entries.analysis: To be clear, this would be better if the prompt happened before sending.

entries.title: Uber's safety toolkit
entries.summary: Uber's safety toolkit includes automatic route detour detection (you'll be detected if you go off-route). You then have the option to live chat with a trained agent.

entries.title: Spotify's Star Wars playbar (lightsaber)
entries.summary: When listening to the Star Wars soundtrack, the Spotify playbar will be a lightsaber.

entries.title: Thoughtful email unsubscribe
entries.summary: If you've not opened Dribbble's emails for a while, they'll unsubscribe you automatically (to keep their open rates high). But, they'll then show you this prompt when you next login, making it quick and easy to resubscribe.

entries.title: Time to hack
entries.summary: Column.com (a bank infrastructure API) will give you an estimated "time to hack" for your password. If it's too easy, you'll also get tips for creating a more secure one.
entries.analysis: This works so well, because it's on-brand with what the service is trying to sell (security / secure APIs). It's not something that every product should adopt, because most of the time you don't want your users obsessing over security or the risk of being hacked.

entries.title: Framing the value of YouTube
entries.summary: It can be easy to forget that the experience you're receiving is better than the free alternative (i.e., lack of ads). To help frame the value of your subscription, YouTube has a summary dashboard to remind you about the value you've received.

entries.title: Snowballing commitment from CTA labels
entries.summary: The first two clicks, when opening the HelloFresh iOS app, are two very small acts of compliance. They've been labelled to spark the foot-in-the-door effect .
entries.analysis: The subtle brilliance of the CTA labelling, is that they both signal that the user is going to do something. And to be clear, this commitment is all internal for the customer. It's them pursuading themselves that they're going to do it. 1. Commitment to picking my plan—i.e., the action of selecting, not "learning" or "viewing" plans. 2. Committing to multiple orders—i.e., I will save on multiple boxes and orders (plural).

entries.title: Automatically importing workout data
entries.summary: Gymshark will automatically import workout data when you create an account, to create a 'streak' that matches your goal. This is more motivating than starting at "0 weeks" again.

entries.title: Integrating with Uber
entries.summary: Sweetgreen has an option to request an Uber when viewing the address of any of their locations.

entries.title: Free delivery upsell on Deliveroo
entries.summary: Once you've ordered on Deliveroo, you (may) see a timer offering you free delivery on other items from another place.
entries.analysis: This is both scarcity (of time), and a form of progressive disclosure , as it upsells the complexity of ordering items from multiple places at once (packaged with a temping upsell offer). The timing is perfect.

entries.title: Figma's interactive 404
entries.summary: Figma's 404 is a totally interactive render, where you can modify the curves and points, exactly like you would with the real tool.

entries.title: Google's reactive onboarding
entries.summary: If Google's assistant can do more specific tasks than you're asking it to do, it'll show you prompts about how you can utilise the tool better. This is reactive onboarding, as it's in context with the current task.

entries.title: Smart location-based warning
entries.summary: When ordering Deliveroo to a location that isn't where you currently are, you'll see this warning on the payment screen.
entries.analysis: This is both an attempt to counter the user's selective attention , as they're likely to focus on their hunger, and the 'input-required' debit card fields. And, it's intentional friction (i.e., a warning message), to get the user to slow down and check their details.

entries.title: Using Reddit at work?
entries.summary: Inspecting the web console of Reddit will show a message prompting you to apply for a job.

entries.title: It doesn't have to be confetti
entries.summary: After listing your Airbnb to host, the success screen includes a short (and really high quality) video of the founder thanking you.

entries.title: Modifying the design for drunk people
entries.summary: If you're out after midnight, Citymapper will make the "get me home" CTA larger.

entries.title: Specific LinkedIn recommendations
entries.summary: When recommending pages or groups, LinkedIn will often show you why they think you may be interested in it.
entries.analysis: This feels obvious, but is usually not done because social graphs can be really complicated, and there often isn't any one single reason why it's recommended.

entries.title: Spinning the wheel of rewards
entries.summary: When joining Zizzi's members club, you get to spin a wheel to unlock a free gift.
entries.analysis: It's rare you see this in retail or dining, and I was surprised at how well it worked. The variable rewards are addictive, and I imagine doing really well to get people into the restaurant.

entries.title: Dynamic price framing
entries.summary: If you look on Airbnb for availability of more than a month, the price will automatically be framed in months instead of days.
entries.analysis: This is important, because if you're booking 90 days of accommodation, seeing "$90 per night" is harder to digest.

entries.title: Asana needs to be reloaded
entries.summary: If Asana errors, and needs to be reloaded, there's a chance you may lose your data when it resyncs. Asana will warn you of this risk, provide suggestions (to copy the text), and give you an easy way to contact support.

entries.title: Citymapper app icon
entries.summary: The alternative app icons come with quirky names that stay true to the original green branding.

entries.title: Failed payment prevention
entries.summary: Monzo will automatically identify recurring payments (such as direct debits), and notify you a day in advance if it looks like you won't have enough money to cover it.

entries.title: Getting lost in a 404
entries.summary: Medium have featured articles about being lost, on their 404 page.

entries.title: Handling multiple suggestions
entries.summary: Siri can handle multiple suggestions at once, and will combine them into a neat list. (i.e., adding a new contact, and an event).

entries.title: Committing in a CTA
entries.summary: The CTA where Duolingo ask you to set your own usage goals, is labelled "I'm committed". Clicking this is in itself, a foot-in-the-door commitment.

entries.title: Introducing you to new content
entries.summary: YouTube will periodically offer to show you 'something different' in your feed. This is likely an attempt to get you to discover new niches and interests.

entries.title: Don't worry, it's still pasting
entries.summary: If the process of pasting something into Figma takes longer than a second, you'll see this message. This is designed to keep you waiting (without panic clicking somewhere), and reassure you that it's not frozen.

entries.title: Viewing similar Airbnb prices
entries.summary: In Airbnb's host view, you can click on any dates in your calendar, and quickly compare (on a map), prices of local booked and unbooked properties.

entries.title: Hello HTML enthusiasts
entries.summary: The HTML header tags have a shoutout to HTML enthusiasts (who are inspecting under the hood of the site).

entries.title: Refreshing carousels
entries.summary: If you've seen the first slide of a carousel on Instagram, but not swiped through the rest, then when you refresh your feed, it'll automatically refocus on an unseen slide. This is a subtle way of making the feed content feel new, by recycling content you haven't seen within the same post.

entries.title: Substack's danger zone
entries.summary: Substack have compiled and clearly highlighted any 'danergous actions' that might lead to a loss of data. i.e., rather than just having a setting that blows up your entire livelihood.
entries.analysis: This is a great example of both intentional friction, and selective attention. Substack is intentionally slowing the user down , forcing them to digest something new (the red warnings), before they hastily take an action.

entries.title: Citymapper easter egg
entries.summary: On Citymapper, if you scroll to the end of the page, and then forcibly keep scrolling , you'll find an underground illustration easter egg.

entries.title: This is Spinal Tap
entries.summary: On IMDB, the Spinal Tap movie has a rating out of 11 (as apposed to 10—it's a Spinal Tap reference).

entries.title: Dribbble's 404
entries.summary: Dribbble's 404 page includes an interactive colour slider, which will show a collection of images in that colour.

entries.title: Automatic question detection
entries.summary: On Google Forms, the question 'type' will automatically update based on the question title (and what it thinks the best format of question will be).

entries.title: Search for Kerning
entries.summary: If you search for 'Kerning', the results will display the word, using Kerning.

entries.title: You look nice today
entries.summary: If you hover over yourself in a huddle, you'll be told that you look nice.

entries.title: Send directions to Tesla
entries.summary: You can share directions from your phone (via maps), with your Tesla, and it'll pre-load navigation in the car.

entries.title: Hidden job prompt
entries.summary: Inspecting the console on Slack.com will show a prompt to apply for a dev job.

entries.title: Paywalled content warning
entries.summary: LinkedIn will show you a warning if it thinks that an external link you're about to open hides content behind a paywall.
entries.analysis: LinkedIn knows that if you click on external links, and those are paywalled, you may be less inclined to click on any external links. i.e., it reduces the 'expected value' of a click, inside LinkedIn. The warning allows people to feel more confident in how they use their time (and clicks) on LinkedIn.

entries.title: Apple calendar's dynamic icon
entries.summary: The app icon for Apple calendar is dynamic, and will show the current date.

entries.title: Suggesting the email intro
entries.summary: Intercom will pull the name from the user profile, and suggest "Hello (name)" as the introduction to any direct emails.

entries.title: Audio to Open Graph
entries.summary: After recording a Loom video, your naration will automatically be transcribed, and then used as the Open Graph description. This immediately makes the 'social card' feel personal and inviting.
entries.analysis: This leverages the endowment effect, because it helps to turn otherwise plain and boring social cards into something really personal. It's probably a small impact, but I imagine that you're more likely to (widely) share a Loom video that has a personalised meta description like this.

entries.title: Looks like you need a new car
entries.summary: CarWow's 404 has an interactive racing game. Once you lose (crash your car), it cleverly directs you into finding a new (real) one.

entries.title: A mermaid in Hawaii
entries.summary: When hovering over Hawaii, the Google Maps man will turn into a mermaid.

entries.title: Automatic screen brightness
entries.summary: iPhones will recognise when a QR code is on screen, and automatically turn the screen brightness up—making it easier to scan.
entries.analysis: When you consider what the alternative is, this is really smart. People wouldn't be bothered to turn their screen brightness up, just to scan one QR code. But then if the QR code failed to read, they'd blame their iPhone, or the scanner. They've factored in the law of minimal effort (people are lazy), and built a feature that generally reduces frustration.

entries.title: Refind's coin buy back
entries.summary: Refind incentivizes user referrals by giving coins and offering to buy them back later at a mystery value.
entries.analysis: They've since stopped doing this—perhaps it was too complicated. But, conceptually it's an interesting idea to use variable rewards to encourage referrals.

entries.title: Wait, don't give up yet
entries.summary: If you've created a Clubhouse room, but click to end the room before anyone has joined, you'll see a prompt reminding you that it can take a while for people to arrive.
entries.analysis: The context that it usually takes a minute or two for people to show up is important. It allows the user to not panic, and self-criticse their own importance. i.e., " ah, it's not me, it's just how long things take ". This is clever framing, by providing more context.

entries.title: A smarter way to block accounts
entries.summary: If you block an account on Instagram, it'll automatically block any other accounts that they own, and any accounts that they make in the future.

entries.title: Avoiding accidental orders
entries.summary: If you open Uber Eats, and it's defaulted to an address that is far away from where you currently are (i.e., if your last order was at a friends house), it'll warn you.
entries.analysis: There are two key pieces of psychology at play here: 1. This is an attempt to counter the user's selective attention , as they're likely to focus on their hunger, and the tantalising images of food in front of them. 2. It's a moment of intentional friction (i.e., a warning message), to get the user to slow down and check their details before placing an order.

entries.title: Jamming to Figjam
entries.summary: You can turn on a timer and audio when working in Figjam, to help you focus. While the track is playing, this little record player spins too.

entries.title: Leading with your prices
entries.summary: Wise are so transparent with their prices, that they'll link directly to a fee calculator on their splash page (i.e., before you've made any commitment to use the app).

entries.title: Google Docs removing an unnecessary decision
entries.summary: If you click to rename a document in Google Docs, it'll automatically use your first piece of text. Then, it'll automatically highlight it all, so you can delete it in one keystroke.
entries.analysis: This helps to reduce decision fatigue, by giving a sensible default option (i.e., removing one decision). They don't want you having to think of something creative to name your doc—they'd rather let you focus on being productive, and writing content.

entries.title: Live chat to a super host
entries.summary: When signing up to become a host, Airbnb will offer to connect you via live chat to another superhost. This is the ultimate social proof.

entries.title: Your wallet profile picture
entries.summary: When creating your wallet, you can choose to use an emoji or an icon as an avatar, instead of having to upload an image.

entries.title: Dynamic calendar sidebar
entries.summary: The sidebar includes a dynamic overview, highlighting the days that are currently in view. It updates in realtime with a smooth animation.

entries.title: Last available train
entries.summary: Google Maps will let you sort public transport by 'last available'.

entries.title: Interactive Figma preview in Asana
entries.summary: Pasting a Figma URL into an Asana task, will show a live and interactive preview of the board. This increases the likelihood that a developer actually bothers to look at Figma for a small task.

entries.title: Dynamic offline mode
entries.summary: When in offline mode, Spotify will dynamically prioritise a 'downloaded music' component, showing all of your available music first.

entries.title: Your content is out of view
entries.summary: If you zoom out of your artboard in Framer, and your content is there (but out of view), you'll see a reassuring message.

entries.title: Apple's dynamic clock icon
entries.summary: The iPhone icon for the clock app, displays the actual time.

entries.title: Automatic timezone detection
entries.summary: Cron will notice if you've changed timezones, and ask you if you'd like to view everything in your currenct timezone (i.e., rather than changing automatically, or not at all, and confusing the user).
entries.analysis: This utilises the law of minimal effort, because they've made it just as easy to change your time zone, as it is to keep it the same (one click). i.e., there's no benefit to being lazy. This encourages people to change their time zone, and will then have a better experience inside the app. But, if they were to change it automatically, it may be too confusing.

entries.title: Figma saving your comments
entries.summary: To prevent you from deleting "real" comments, once you've typed 5 characters, Figma won't let you click-out to stop typing a comment. It'll shake the pop-up to warn you too.

entries.title: Special 'like' animations on Twitter (X)
entries.summary: For special events (like Apple's WWDC), there are special 'like' animations on Twitter.

entries.title: Syncing Slack to Google Calendar
entries.summary: You can have your Slack status automatically set itself to 'busy' during Google calendar events.

entries.title: WeWork's annual recap
entries.summary: To remind you of the (often silent) benefits of using WeWork, they'll send you an annual recap, explaining how much you used your membership.
entries.analysis: This is very similar to Spotify Wrapped in concept.

entries.title: Silence unknown callers
entries.summary: Apple will let you automatically silence calls from unknown users.

entries.title: Watch battery notification
entries.summary: If your watch is charging, while your phone isn't (i.e., during the day), you'll be notified when it's fully charged.

entries.title: Celebrating audience milestones
entries.summary: If you add content to Google (e.g., an image / review of a business or location), you'll be notified when your content hits milestones. This demonstrates how useful your content has been.

entries.title: A change in your fitness data
entries.summary: If Apple notices a new trend in your fitness behaviour, it'll prompt you to view that change with this bar chart overlay.

entries.title: One-tap password sharing
entries.summary: If someone in your contacts attempts to join the same WiFi as you, you can instantly share the password instead of them having to type it.

entries.title: Smart comment positioning
entries.summary: Comments associated to another element (e.g., a frame or image) will automatically reposition if you move that thing. i.e., if you comment on an image, and then someone moves the image, your comment will "stick" to it.

entries.title: Playable game of Snake
entries.summary: Spotify has built a playable game of 'Snake', where you eat tracks in your playlist.

entries.title: Onboarding Ahref's broken link checker
entries.summary: Ahref's 404 page actually offers a tool to find broken links on your website. i.e., they've used their 404 page, to advertise a key feature of theirs.
entries.analysis: This is a form of 'reactive onboarding', because it's essentially saying " you've landed on a broken link—this is what a broken link means ". Then, they'll introduce you to a feature; their broken link checker.

entries.title: Terminal easter egg
entries.summary: Stripe built an interactive terminal with an easter egg: if you enter 1337, it takes you to their careers page. (1337 is internet slang for 'Elite').

entries.title: Providing an alternative promo code
entries.summary: If you enter an incorrect code into Dominos, they'll present you an offer from their library.

entries.title: Sentry's misery index
entries.summary: Sentry (the error-tracking service) will include a 'Misery Index' for each issue. This may feel like the moment where huge service value is unlocked.

entries.title: Slack's clear huddle notifications
entries.summary: When starting a 'huddle', you'll be reassured that the other person has been notified—i.e., so you don't feel the need to message them separately or share a link.

entries.title: Covering eyes animation
entries.summary: The bear will cover their eyes while you're typing your password.

entries.title: Automatic clipboard purging
entries.summary: When copying your card details, Monzo will automatically purge your clipboard after two minutes.

entries.title: Favicon as confirmation
entries.summary: When creating an account, Hotjar will pull your favicon as a confirmation that you've entered the right URL. This helps it feel like yours.

entries.title: User-initiated trigger
entries.summary: After your first lesson, you'll be prompted to set your own goal—which will be more motivating than an arbitrary pre-set one.

entries.title: Alternative routes for safety
entries.summary: When mapping a walking route, you are shown an alternative route along main roads, for safety.

entries.title: Secret console message
entries.summary: Inspecting the console tab on Soundcloud will show a prompt to join their team.

entries.title: Making dashboards meme-y
entries.summary: Dashthis is a tool for creating dashboards and reports, and they've used a chart to recreate a Lord of the Rings meme.

entries.title: Shopping cart investment
entries.summary: The MrBeast store will let you earn freebies by slapping their avatars hundreds of time. This is an investment in the purchase and will decrease cart churn.
entries.analysis: They've since removed this (it was likely a temporary mini game), and although the subject matter is fairly controversial (slapping people isn't a great look for an influencer), it's a clever way of getting potential customers to earn a freebie . People will feel more incentivised to make a purchase, after sitting there clicking hundreds of times.

entries.title: Overlay onboarding
entries.summary: When installing the Mac app, you'll see an extra window that sticks to the native Apple settings page, literally pointing at the thing you need to do.

entries.title: Single-click to remove template
entries.summary: If Notion has pre-populated a template or demo for you, they'll give you a single-click way of removing it too.

entries.title: Known bike lane risks
entries.summary: Uber will warn you if you're being dropped off near a known bike lane.

entries.title: Event-based focus mode
entries.summary: If you have an event in your calendar, Apple will show a new option for 'focus mode' to end after that event.

entries.title: Dynamic loss aversion
entries.summary: When you get 3 questions in a row incorrect, you'll be reassured that you're making progress even making making mistakes. This is to offset the Pygmalion Effect.

entries.title: Leveraged scarcity to motivate bookings
entries.summary: Airbnb will let you know if a property is usually booked, leveraging the scarcity effect.

entries.title: Smart 'focus mode'
entries.summary: If you use Focus mode, Apple will offer to automatically turn it on when you arrive at work.

entries.title: Searching Yelp using emojis
entries.summary: You can search using emojis.

entries.title: Space invaders 404
entries.summary: Kualo's 404 includes a playable version of Space Invaders.

entries.title: Discord's social proof during sign-up
entries.summary: When joining a Discord, you'll be shown how many other people like you are also in that group.

entries.title: IMDB showing movie quotes in their 404
entries.summary: IMDB's 404 page will display random (slightly edited) movie quotes.

entries.title: Wheel of fortune
entries.summary: To incentivise you to submit your utility meter readings, every time you submit one, you can spin a wheel of fortune to win Octopoints.

entries.title: Showing the real date in the favicon
entries.summary: On Chrome, the favicon will update to show today's date.

entries.title: Realtime day/night shadows
entries.summary: In satellite views, when zoomed out, you can see day/night in realtime.

entries.title: Smooth filtering animation
entries.summary: Filtering by room type will display this smooth animation, which illustrates the type of place you're hovering over.

entries.title: Displaying options in their format
entries.summary: Capitalisation options are displayed in their outputted format.

entries.title: Displaying hex code previews in-chat
entries.summary: Slack will automatically show previews of hex codes.

entries.title: Logging recent parking location
entries.summary: Your most recent parking location will be automatically saved.

entries.title: Parcel recognition via Nest camera
entries.summary: Your Nest camera will recognise parcels, and send specific notifications if a parcel has been left on your doorstep.

entries.title: Post-video analytics
entries.summary: It's addictive and motivating to be shown analytics of your recordings, including how many 'filler' words you've used, and how much total time you've saved your team.

entries.title: Optimised battery charging
entries.summary: Your iPhone will initially charge to just 80% overnight, and restart automatically based on your expected wake-up time. You start your day on 100%, and your battery hasn't been charging all night—which is bad for long-term battery health.

entries.title: Alert that visitors are on the call
entries.summary: Google Meets will proactively let you know if guests are on the call.

entries.title: Interactive hero
entries.summary: The auto-unsubscribe service has an interactive 'unsubscribe' button on their homepage, which is super satisfying to play with.

entries.title: Keynote format warning
entries.summary: Apple will warn you if you're pasting an image in a format that may not work on another device in their ecosystem. It's annoying that the file is incompatible, but the crutch is clever.

entries.title: Swinging by their arm
entries.summary: Dragging the Google Maps man will actually swing him by his arms.

entries.title: Copy logo as SVG
entries.summary: When right-clicking on their logo, you'll be able to copy as an SVG, and view their brand guidelines.

entries.title: Paste a web URL
entries.summary: Figma have built the feature to allow you to paste a web URL into the native app.

entries.title: Animated digging
entries.summary: When changing VPN location, the bear will dig a hole, and then tunnel to that location on the map.
